```mermaid
    style P1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style P2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style P3 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
    style P4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style P8 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows the unidirectional mass-transfer chain — solid → saturated layer → diffusion layer → bulk — with the agitator closing the loop on the bulk, ensuring sink conditions.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Ion-Exchange Column Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Inlet Feed Solution containing Ion A plus] --> Q2[Resin Bed with Fixed Negative Groups and Counter Ion B plus]
    Q2 --> Q3[Equilibrium Exchange Site]
    Q3 --> Q4[Outlet Solution depleted in A plus enriched in B plus]
    Q4 --> Q5[Detection of Breakthrough 5 percent Criterion]
    Q5 --> Q6[Regeneration Step with Concentrated Regenerant]
    Q6 --> Q2
    Q3 --> Q7[Selectivity Coefficient K governs preference]
    Q7 --> Q3

    style Q1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style Q2 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00
    style Q3 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#b71c1c
    style Q4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style Q5 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style Q6 fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0
```

**Interpretation:** A cyclic, regenerable process — the hallmark of ion-exchange systems. Notice the regeneration arrow loops back to the resin bed, indicating the process can be repeated indefinitely.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparative Decision Tree for Drug Resin Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Target Drug Analyte] --> R2{Ionizable Group Present?}
    R2 -- Yes --> R3{pKa versus Physiological pH}
    R2 -- No --> R4[Use Alternative Taste Masking Technique]
    R3 -- Charged in Mouth pH 6.8 --> R5[Select Resin with Opposite Charge]
    R3 -- Neutral --> R4
    R5 --> R6[Cationic Drug plus Anionic Resin e.g. Polacrilin]
    R5 --> R7[Anionic Drug plus Cationic Resin e.g. Cholestyramine analog]
    R6 --> R8[Complex Formation in Acidic Medium]
    R7 --> R8
    R8 --> R9[Drug Resin Complex Stability Testing]
    R9 --> R10{Desired Release Profile?}
    R10 -- Immediate --> R11[Choose Low Selectivity Weak Acid Resin]
    R10 -- Sustained --> R12[Choose High Selectivity Strong Acid Resin plus Coating]

    style R1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style R8 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style R12 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Interpretation:** This is the practical decision framework a formulation scientist follows. The resin selection depends on charge, pKa, and target release profile — all derived from ion-exchange principles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Noyes–Whitney equation and explain the significance of each term in predicting drug dissolution.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Noyes–Whitney equation describes the rate of dissolution of a solid drug in a liquid medium under sink conditions.

**Equation:**

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{D \cdot A}{h \cdot V}\,(C_s - C)$$

**Significance of each term (1 mark each):**

- **D (Diffusion Coefficient, cm²/s):** Represents the inherent mobility of the drug molecules through the diffusion layer; increases with temperature, decreases with molecular size and viscosity of medium.
- **A (Surface Area, cm²):** Greater exposed area gives faster dissolution; explains why micronization improves bioavailability of poorly soluble drugs.
- **h (Diffusion Layer Thickness, cm):** Inversely related to agitation speed; smaller $h$ (e.g., with vigorous stirring) accelerates dissolution.
- **V (Volume of Medium, cm³):** Larger volume maintains sink conditions for longer duration.
- **$C_s - C$ (Concentration Gradient):** Driving force for dissolution; vanishes as saturation is reached.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: What is an ion-exchange resin? Mention its classification based on the nature of the exchangeable ion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An ion-exchange resin is a high-molecular-weight, water-insoluble polymer carrying fixed ionic functional groups, capable of exchanging its mobile counter-ions with ions of the same charge from the surrounding solution without any physical change in the resin matrix.

**Classification (2 Marks):**

1. **Cation Exchange Resins** — exchange positively charged ions; contain fixed acidic groups:
   - **Strong-acid cation (SAC)** resins: Sulfonic acid groups (–SO₃⁻H⁺); active across entire pH range.
   - **Weak-acid cation (WAC)** resins: Carboxylic acid groups (–COO⁻H⁺); active at pH > 5.

2. **Anion Exchange Resins** — exchange negatively charged ions; contain fixed basic groups:
   - **Strong-base anion (SBA)** resins: Quaternary ammonium groups (–N⁺(CH₃)₃); active across entire pH range.
   - **Weak-base anion (WBA)** resins: Tertiary amine groups; active at pH < 7.

**Example:** Polacrilin potassium (weak acid cation) used in pharmaceutical taste-masking of basic drugs.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students forget to **state units** for each variable — KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark per term** in derivations when units are missing. Always write them.
> 2. In ion-exchange numericals, students confuse **equivalent ionic fractions** ($\bar{x}_A, x_A$) with **concentrations**. The former are dimensionless ratios summing to 1; the latter have units.
> 3. In Noyes–Whitney plots, students plot $\log C$ vs $t$ instead of $\log(C_s - C)$ vs $t$ when sink conditions are **not** assumed — examiners specifically deduct for not justifying the assumption.
> 4. Drawing the **diffusion layer** in a diagram without labeling $C_s$, $C$, and $h$ leads to a **2-mark penalty** in the diagram section.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Q: Derive the Noyes–Whitney equation from Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Discuss the effect of particle size, agitation speed, and temperature on the dissolution rate of a drug.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Starting from Fick's First Law:

$$J = -D\,\frac{dC}{dx}$$

Assuming a linear gradient across the stagnant layer of thickness $h$:

$$\frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{C_s - C}{h}$$

Therefore:

$$J = \frac{D\,(C_s - C)}{h}$$

Since $J \cdot A = \dfrac{dW}{dt}$ and $W = C \cdot V$:

$$\frac{D\,A\,(C_s - C)}{h} = V\,\frac{dC}{dt}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{D \cdot A}{h \cdot V}\,(C_s - C)\;}$$

**Effect of parameters (2 Marks):**

- **Particle Size ↓ (micronization):** Increases surface area $A$ → higher dissolution rate. This is the rationale for BCS Class II drug micronization.
- **Agitation Speed ↑:** Reduces stagnant layer thickness $h$ → higher dissolution rate. Affects the hydrodynamic boundary layer, especially in USP Apparatus II.
- **Temperature ↑:** Increases $D$ (via Stokes–Einstein: $D \propto T/\eta$) and also $C_s$ (endothermic dissolution). Net result: substantially higher rate.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Q: A drug has saturation solubility $C_s = 1.0$ mg/mL. A 100 mg tablet dissolves in 900 mL of medium at 37 °C under sink conditions. If the apparent dissolution rate constant is $k = 0.05$ min⁻¹, calculate (i) the time to reach 50 % dissolution, and (ii) the concentration at $t = 30$ min.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $C_s = 1.0$ mg/mL, $W_0 = 100$ mg, $V = 900$ mL, $k = 0.05$ min⁻¹.

**(i) Time for 50 % dissolution (3 Marks):**

At $t = t_{50\%}$, dissolved amount $W = 50$ mg, hence:

$$C = \frac{50 \text{ mg}}{900 \text{ mL}} = 0.0556 \text{ mg/mL}$$

Using the integrated form (sink condition approximation):

$$\log C = \log C_s - \frac{k\,t}{2.303}$$

$$\log(0.0556) = \log(1.0) - \frac{0.05\,t}{2.303}$$

$$-1.255 = 0 - \frac{0.05\,t}{2.303}$$

$$\frac{0.05\,t}{2.303} = 1.255$$

$$t = \frac{1.255 \times 2.303}{0.05} = \frac{2.890}{0.05} = 57.81 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Concentration at $t = 30$ min (4 Marks):**

Using the exact first-order (non-sink) integrated form:

$$C = C_s\left(1 - e^{-k\,t}\right)$$

$$C = 1.0 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.05 \times 30}\right) = 1.0 \times \left(1 - e^{-1.5}\right)$$

$$e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$$

$$C = 1.0 \times (1 - 0.2231) = 0.7769 \text{ mg/mL}$$

Mass dissolved = $0.7769 \times 900 = 699.2$ mg — **but this exceeds 100 mg**, indicating that the first-order model with $C_s = 1.0$ mg/mL and 900 mL medium means complete dissolution would saturate the system at only 100 mg. Hence the **sink-condition integrated form** is more appropriate:

$$\log C = \log(0.1111) - \frac{0.05 \times 30}{2.303}$$

$$\log C = -0.954 - 0.652 = -1.606$$

$$C = 0.0247 \text{ mg/mL} = 22.3 \text{ mg dissolved}$$

(Where 0.1111 mg/mL = 100 mg / 900 mL = total possible concentration.)

**Conclusion:** At 30 min, ~22.3 % of the drug has dissolved; the sink-condition linear-log model is the correct choice here, as the drug mass is far below the saturation mass ($C_s \cdot V = 900$ mg).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Q: Explain the principle of ion exchange with a suitable example. Derive the selectivity coefficient for a binary ion-exchange reaction between two monovalent cations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 Marks):**

Ion exchange is a reversible stoichiometric interchange of ions between an insoluble solid (resin) and a surrounding electrolyte solution. The resin's fixed ionic groups (e.g., –SO₃⁻ on a cation exchanger) hold mobile counter-ions (e.g., H⁺ or Na⁺). When the resin is contacted with a solution containing a different cation (e.g., Ca²⁺ in hard water), the more preferred ion displaces the resident one, achieving a new equilibrium.

**Example:** Domestic water softening uses a strong-acid cation exchanger in the Na⁺ form:

$$2\,\overline{\text{R–SO}_3^-\,\text{Na}^+} + \text{Ca}^{2+}_{(s)} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \overline{(\text{R–SO}_3^-)_2\,\text{Ca}^{2+}} + 2\,\text{Na}^+_{(s)}$$

The Ca²⁺-loaded resin is later regenerated with concentrated NaCl brine.

**Derivation of Selectivity Coefficient (4 Marks):**

For two monovalent cations ($z_A = z_B = 1$), the exchange is:

$$\text{A}^+_{(s)} + \overline{\text{B}^+} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \overline{\text{A}^+} + \text{B}^+_{(s)}$$

Applying the Law of Mass Action:

$$K_A^B = \frac{[\overline{\text{A}}]\,[\text{B}]}{[\text{A}]\,[\overline{\text{B}}]}$$

Substituting equivalent ionic fractions $x_A + x_B = 1$ in solution and $\bar{x}_A + \bar{x}_B = 1$ in resin:

$$K_A^B = \frac{\bar{x}_A \cdot x_B}{x_A \cdot \bar{x}_B}$$

**Interpretation:** If $K_A^B > 1$, the resin prefers ion A; if $K_A^B < 1$, ion B is preferred.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Q: An ion-exchange resin with capacity 5 meq/g is used to remove Na⁺ from a 0.05 N NaCl solution. If 200 g of resin treats 5 L of solution, calculate (i) the maximum amount of Na⁺ removed (in meq), and (ii) the percentage removal assuming complete equilibration and $K = 1.5$.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Na⁺ Removal Capacity (3 Marks):**

Resin capacity = 5 meq/g, Resin mass = 200 g.

$$\text{Total Capacity} = 5 \times 200 = 1000 \text{ meq}$$

This is the theoretical maximum if the resin were fully loaded to capacity.

**(ii) Percentage Removal (4 Marks):**

Initial Na⁺ content:

$$N_{\text{initial}} = 0.05 \text{ eq/L} \times 5 \text{ L} = 0.25 \text{ eq} = 250 \text{ meq}$$

Since the resin capacity (1000 meq) far exceeds the available Na⁺ (250 meq), **all** Na⁺ could theoretically be removed (if selectivity is sufficient). However, selectivity limits full removal.

Using $K = 1.5$ and assuming the resin initially in H⁺ form, mass balance:

- Total solution: $C_T = 0.05$ N.
- Total resin: $Q = 1000$ meq.

For monovalent exchange with $K = \bar{x}_A x_B / (x_A \bar{x}_B)$:

At equilibrium, let $x_A$ = fraction of Na⁺ remaining in solution (so $x_B = 1 - x_A$ = H⁺ in solution) and $\bar{x}_A$ = fraction of Na⁺ on resin ($\bar{x}_B = 1 - \bar{x}_A$ = H⁺ on resin).

By total-mass conservation (charge neutrality):

$$C_T \, V = Q \, m \left(\bar{x}_A - x_A\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\text{factor}}$$

For simplicity, assuming the resin takes up almost all available Na⁺:

$$\bar{x}_A \approx \frac{250}{1000} = 0.25, \quad \bar{x}_B = 0.75$$

$$1.5 = \frac{0.25 \cdot x_B}{x_A \cdot 0.75} = \frac{x_B}{3\,x_A}$$

With $x_A + x_B = 1$:

$$1.5 = \frac{1 - x_A}{3\,x_A}$$

$$4.5\,x_A = 1 - x_A \;\Longrightarrow\; 5.5\,x_A = 1$$

$$x_A = 0.1818 \quad (\text{i.e., } 18.18\% \text{ Na}^+ \text{ remains in solution})$$

$$\textbf{Percentage Removal} = (1 - 0.1818) \times 100\% = \mathbf{81.82\%}$$

Equivalents of Na⁺ removed = $0.8182 \times 250 = 204.5$ meq.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Dissolution & Ion Exchange**

- **Noyes–Whitney Equation:** $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = \dfrac{D \cdot A}{h \cdot V}(C_s - C)$ — always state units ($D$: cm²/s; $A$: cm²; $h$: cm; $V$: cm³; $C$: mg/mL).
- **Integrated (sink) form:** $\log C = \log C_s - \dfrac{k\,t}{2.303}$, where $k = D A / (h V)$. Plot slope is $-k/2.303$.
- **First-order exact form:** $C = C_s (1 - e^{-k t})$. Use this **only when sink condition fails**.
- **Stokes–Einstein link:** $D = k_B T / (6\pi\eta r)$ — explains temperature and viscosity dependence of diffusion.
- **Hixson–Crowell Cube-Root Law:** $W_0^{1/3} - W^{1/3} = \kappa t$ — applies when surface area *decreases* proportionally as dissolution proceeds.
- **Diffusion Layer Thickness (h):** typically **30 μm** in USP Apparatus II; decreases with rpm.
- **Sink Condition rule of thumb:** $C \leq 0.10\,C_s$ throughout the sampling interval; USP uses 900 mL medium for most IR tablets.
- **Selectivity Coefficient (binary, equal valence):** $K_A^B = \dfrac{\bar{x}_A \, x_B}{x_A \, \bar{x}_B}$ — dimensionless; $K > 1$ means resin prefers A.
- **Selectivity Coefficient (unequal valence):** $K_A^B = \dfrac{[\overline{A}]^{z_B}\,[B]^{z_A}}{[A]^{z_B}\,[\overline{B}]^{z_A}}$ — a strong dependence on ionic charge.
- **Resin Capacity (Q):** $Q = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_t)\,V}{m}$, expressed in **meq/g** of dry resin.
- **Four resin classes:** SAC (–SO₃⁻H⁺), WAC (–COO⁻H⁺), SBA (–N⁺R₃), WBA (–NR₂). Match resin to drug ionization state at relevant pH.
- **Pharmaceutical Applications of Resins:**
  - **Taste-masking** (e.g., dextromethorphan–polistirex).
  - **Sustained release** (drug–resin complex in coated beads).
  - **Cholesterol binding** (cholestyramine).
  - **Stability improvement** (protecting labile drugs from gastric pH).
- **Henderson–Hasselbalch decides release site:** A weak-base drug bound to a cation exchanger stays bound in saliva (pH 6.8) and releases in stomach (pH 1.2, where it is fully protonated and competes for H⁺).
- **Exam tip — always define abbreviations** at first use: DVB (divinylbenzene), SAC (strong-acid cation), BCS (Biopharmaceutics Classification System), USP (United States Pharmacopeia).
- **Exam tip — draw the diffusion layer** with explicit labels for $C_s$, $C$, $h$, and arrow indicating flux direction. Half-mark lost per missing label.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam-Strategy Note for Module 1:** Dissolution and ion exchange together contribute typically **~15–20 marks** in the KTU PEBB862 End Semester Exam. Mastery of the Noyes–Whitney equation (derivation + numericals) and the selectivity coefficient (definition + Kerr simplification for equal-valence case) will secure the majority of these marks. Practice at least **three numericals per equation** before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physicochemical and Biological Properties of Drugs Relevant to Controlled Release Formulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB862 - DRUG DELIVERY PRINCIPLE & APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physicochemical and Biological Properties of Drugs Relevant to Controlled Release Formulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Controlled Release (CR) Formulation:** A dosage form engineered to release one or more active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) at a predetermined rate, for a defined duration, and at a specific site within the gastrointestinal tract (GIT) — thereby maintaining a therapeutically effective plasma drug concentration within the therapeutic window for an extended period.

A **Controlled Release Formulation** differs fundamentally from conventional immediate-release (IR) dosage forms. While an IR tablet releases ≥ 85% of its drug content within 30 minutes, a CR system is designed to release drug slowly over hours, days, or even months using physicochemical principles such as diffusion, dissolution, erosion, and osmosis.

For a drug to be successfully formulated as a CR product, its **physicochemical properties** (intrinsic to the molecule — solubility, partition coefficient, stability, molecular weight, pKa) and its **biological properties** (extrinsic/host-related — absorption, distribution, metabolism, elimination, half-life) must satisfy specific criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly mandates study of how drug solubility, dose, pKa, molecular weight, partition coefficient, stability, and protein binding influence the design of oral CR systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Trickling Tap"

Imagine filling a kitchen bucket from a tap:

- **Conventional (Immediate-Release) Tablet** = Opening the tap fully — the bucket fills fast, water overflows (toxicity risk), and then the tap must be reopened many times (frequent dosing).
- **Controlled-Release Tablet** = Installing a **flow restrictor** — water trickles steadily into the bucket at a constant rate, exactly matching the consumption rate, eliminating overflow and the need for repeated refilling.

In this analogy:
- **Tap (drug reservoir)** = Tablet dose
- **Flow restrictor (CR mechanism)** = Polymer matrix / coating / osmotic pump
- **Bucket (patient's plasma)** = Blood compartment
- **Leakage through restrictor** = Drug release rate (governed by Fick's law of diffusion)
- **Pipe diameter** = Drug's physicochemical properties (solubility, diffusivity)
- **Water loss through drain** = Drug elimination (biological property)

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in CR Design

| Property Category | Why It's Important | Consequence if Ignored |
|---|---|---|
| **Aqueous Solubility** | Determines release rate from matrix | Drug may dump (dose dumping) or never release |
| **Partition Coefficient (log P)** | Governs membrane permeability | Poor absorption despite release |
| **Molecular Weight** | Affects diffusivity | Cannot diffuse through polymer |
| **pKa** | Determines ionization at GI pH | Variable absorption along GIT |
| **Biological Half-life (t½)** | Determines dosing interval | Short t½ → impractical CR |
| **Therapeutic Index (TI)** | Safety margin | Narrow TI → unacceptable toxicity risk |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in CR Formulation Design

The following physicochemical/biological metrics are universally accepted in pharmaceutical R&D:

- **Dose (mg)** — Amount of drug administered
- **Aqueous Solubility (Cs, mg/mL)** — Maximum dissolvable concentration
- **Partition Coefficient (K or P)** — Lipid/water concentration ratio
- **Dissociation Constant (pKa)** — pH at which 50% ionization occurs
- **Molecular Weight (MW, Da)** — Molar mass of the drug
- **Diffusion Coefficient (D, cm²/s)** — Rate of molecular diffusion
- **Elimination Half-life (t½, h)** — Time for 50% plasma concentration drop
- **Bioavailability (F, %)** — Fraction reaching systemic circulation
- **Therapeutic Index (TI)** — Ratio of TD50 to ED50

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Relevant Properties

The properties governing CR formulation design are grouped into **two major domains**, as per the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus:

### A. Physicochemical Properties (Drug-Intrinsic)

These are inherent to the drug molecule itself and determine its behavior in a delivery system.

#### 2.1.1 Aqueous Solubility (Cs)

The maximum concentration of drug that dissolves in water (or aqueous GI fluids) at a given temperature. CR formulations require drugs to have **sufficient** but **not excessive** solubility.

- **Low solubility (< 0.1 mg/mL)** → Drug release is dissolution-limited; may be too slow.
- **High solubility (> 10 mg/mL)** → Risk of dose dumping; difficult to retard release.

> [!NOTE]
> For oral CR, ideal solubility range = **1 – 10 mg/mL** (BCS Class I/II range).

**Noyes–Whitney Dissolution Equation:**

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{D \cdot A}{h} \cdot (C_s - C)$$

Where $\frac{dM}{dt}$ = dissolution rate, $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $A$ = surface area, $h$ = diffusion layer thickness, $C_s$ = saturation solubility, $C$ = bulk concentration.

#### 2.1.2 Partition Coefficient (K or P)

Ratio of drug concentration in lipid phase to aqueous phase at equilibrium. Measured as **log P** (logarithm to base 10).

$$\log P = \log \frac{[Drug]_{octanol}}{[Drug]_{water}}$$

- log P < 1 → Hydrophilic (poor membrane permeability)
- log P 1–3 → Optimal for oral absorption
- log P > 5 → Too lipophilic (poor aqueous solubility, high protein binding)

#### 2.1.3 Molecular Size and Molecular Weight

Drug diffusion through polymer matrices follows the free-volume theory. Diffusion coefficient $D$ decreases with molecular radius.

$$D = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r}$$

- Drugs with MW > **500 Da** show poor diffusion through most polymer matrices.
- Ideal MW for CR diffusion-controlled systems: **< 400 Da**.

#### 2.1.4 Acid Dissociation Constant (pKa)

Defines the ionization state of the drug as a function of pH. Governs solubility variation along the GIT.

**Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation:**

$$pH = pK_a + \log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$$

For weak acids: $pH - pK_a = \log \frac{[\text{ionized}]}{[\text{unionized}]}$

For weak bases: $pH - pK_a = \log \frac{[\text{unionized}]}{[\text{ionized}]}$

Only **unionized fraction** crosses biological membranes. Hence, drug absorption (and release–absorption coupling) is pH-dependent.

#### 2.1.5 Drug Stability

Stability against hydrolysis, oxidation, photolysis, and enzymatic degradation in GI fluids affects the choice of CR polymer and packaging.

#### 2.1.6 Polymorphism and Crystallinity

Different polymorphs of the same drug exhibit different solubilities. The **most stable form** is preferred for CR to ensure reproducible release.

#### 2.1.7 Dose Size (Dose Strength)

According to the **Dose Number (Do)** concept:

$$D_o = \frac{Dose}{C_s \cdot V_{GI}}$$

Where $V_{GI}$ = volume of GI fluid (~250 mL).

- $D_o < 1$ → Soluble (BCS Class I/III)
- $D_o > 1$ → Poorly soluble (BCS Class II/IV)

#### 2.1.8 Diffusivity (D)

Drug diffusion coefficient in polymer matrix — typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ cm²/s in hydrogels.

#### 2.1.9 Protein Binding (Plasma)

Percentage of drug bound to plasma proteins (albumin, α1-acid glycoprotein). Highly bound drugs (> 95%) have small free fractions for elimination, prolonging effective half-life.

### B. Biological Properties (Host/Patient-Related)

#### 2.1.10 Absorption (Permeability)

Drug must be absorbed from the GI tract efficiently. **Biopharmaceutics Classification System (BCS)** categorizes drugs into 4 classes:

| BCS Class | Solubility | Permeability | CR Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | High | High | Limited (already well absorbed) |
| II | Low | High | **Excellent** — solubility-enhancing CR |
| III | High | Low | Moderate — permeability-limiting |
| IV | Low | Low | Poor — not recommended |

#### 2.1.11 Distribution (Volume of Distribution, Vd)

$$V_d = \frac{\text{Total Drug in Body}}{C_{plasma}}$$

A high Vd indicates extensive tissue binding; affects loading dose but not CR design directly.

#### 2.1.12 Metabolism (Biotransformation)

Hepatic first-pass metabolism can degrade drug before systemic absorption. Drugs with high first-pass loss are **poor candidates** for oral CR (alternative: transdermal, parenteral).

#### 2.1.14 Elimination Half-Life (t½)

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693 \cdot V_d}{CL}$$

- **Short t½ (< 2 h)** → Excellent CR candidate (reduce dosing frequency).
- **Long t½ (> 8 h)** → CR offers little advantage.
- **Ideal t½** for CR: **2 – 6 h**.

#### 2.1.15 Bioavailability (F)

$$F = F_a \times F_g \times F_h$$

Where $F_a$ = fraction absorbed, $F_g$ = gut wall survival, $F_h$ = hepatic survival.

CR formulations should maintain F close to the IR formulation (typically F > 80%).

#### 2.1.16 Therapeutic Index (TI)

$$TI = \frac{TD_{50}}{ED_{50}}$$

- **Wide TI (TI > 10)** → Safe for CR (e.g., propranolol, theophylline).
- **Narrow TI (TI < 2)** → Risky for CR (e.g., digoxin, lithium) — small fluctuations cause toxicity.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Key Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Noyes–Whitney Dissolution | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = \dfrac{D \cdot A}{h}(C_s - C)$ | $D$: cm²/s, $A$: cm², $h$: cm, $C_s$: mg/mL |
| 2 | Henderson–Hasselbalch (acid) | $pH = pK_a + \log \dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Henderson–Hasselbalch (base) | $pH = pK_a + \log \dfrac{[BH^+]}{[B]}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | Partition Coefficient | $\log P = \log \dfrac{[Drug]_{oct}}{[Drug]_{aq}}$ | Ratio, dimensionless |
| 5 | Stokes–Einstein Diffusion | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r}$ | $k_B$: J/K, $\eta$: Pa·s, $r$: m |
| 6 | Elimination Half-Life | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693 \cdot V_d}{CL}$ | $V_d$: L, $CL$: L/h, $t_{1/2}$: h |
| 7 | Therapeutic Index | $TI = \dfrac{TD_{50}}{ED_{50}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Dose Number | $D_o = \dfrac{Dose}{C_s \cdot V_{GI}}$ | $C_s$: mg/mL, $V_{GI}$: mL |
| 9 | Bioavailability | $F = F_a \times F_g \times F_h$ | Fractions (0–1) |
| 10 | Higuchi (Matrix Release) | $M_t = A \sqrt{D \cdot C_s \cdot (2C_0 - C_s) \cdot t}$ | $M_t$: mg, $t$: s |
| 11 | Zero-Order Release Rate | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = k_0$ | $k_0$: mg/h |
| 12 | First-Order Release Rate | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = k_1 \cdot M$ | $k_1$: h⁻¹ |
| 13 | Diffusion Layer Thickness | $h = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot \pi \cdot t}{2}}$ | cm |
| 14 | Permeability (Fick) | $P = \dfrac{D \cdot K}{h}$ | cm/s |
| 15 | Loading Dose | $LD = \dfrac{V_d \cdot C_{target}}{F}$ | mg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Always commit the **Noyes–Whitney**, **Henderson–Hasselbalch**, **Higuchi matrix**, and **Half-life** equations to memory. They are the most frequently asked equations across all 5 modules of the course.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This integrated physicochemical–biological framework is the cornerstone of:

1. **Pre-formulation screening** — Selecting CR-eligible candidates from NCE libraries.
2. **Polymer selection** — Choosing matrix formers (HPMC, ethyl cellulose, Eudragit) based on drug solubility.
3. **Predictive pharmacokinetic modeling** — Simulating plasma profiles in-silico (GastroPlus™, Simcyp™).
4. **Regulatory dossier preparation** — FDA's BCS-based biowaiver documentation.
5. **Bioequivalence design** — Comparing CR vs. IR in BE studies.
6. **Personalized medicine** — Adjusting CR release rates based on patient-specific metabolism.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation (for Weak Acid)

### Starting Point (Acid Dissociation Reaction)

$$HA \rightleftharpoons H^+ + A^-$$

### Step 1: Write the Acid Dissociation Constant

$$K_a = \frac{[H^+][A^-]}{[HA]}$$

**Logic:** By definition, $K_a$ is the equilibrium constant relating the product of ionized species to unionized drug.

### Step 2: Take the Negative Logarithm of Both Sides

$$-\log K_a = -\log [H^+] - \log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$$

**Logic:** We apply $-\log$ to convert the constant into the more convenient p-notation.

### Step 3: Rearrange to Introduce pH and pKa

$$-\log [H^+] = -\log K_a + \log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$$

**Logic:** Recognizing $pH = -\log[H^+]$ and $pK_a = -\log K_a$.

### Step 4: Final Form

$$pH = pK_a + \log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$$

**Logic:** This is the working Henderson–Hasselbalch equation. Note: $[A^-]$ is ionized fraction (poor permeability), $[HA]$ is unionized fraction (good permeability).

### Application Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid, pKa = 3.5). In the stomach (pH = 1.5):
> 
> $$\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} = 10^{(1.5 - 3.5)} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$$
> 
> So only 1% is ionized in the stomach — aspirin is well absorbed in the stomach.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Noyes–Whitney Dissolution Equation

### Step 1: Fick's First Law of Diffusion

The flux $J$ of dissolved drug molecules through a stagnant diffusion layer of thickness $h$:

$$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx} = D \frac{(C_s - C)}{h}$$

**Logic:** Fick's law states that mass transport is proportional to concentration gradient. Drug moves from high concentration $C_s$ (at solid surface) to low concentration $C$ (in bulk).

### Step 2: Mass Dissolved per Unit Time

Multiplying the flux by the surface area $A$ gives the dissolution rate:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = J \cdot A = \frac{D \cdot A}{h} (C_s - C)$$

**Logic:** Mass transfer rate = flux × cross-sectional area through which molecules flow.

### Step 3: Sink Condition Simplification

Under perfect sink conditions, $C \ll C_s$, so:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} \approx \frac{D \cdot A \cdot C_s}{h}$$

**Logic:** If bulk concentration remains negligible (drug absorbed instantly or sink maintained), the equation simplifies to a constant dissolution rate.

### Significance for CR Design

- **Slowing release** → Increase $h$ (e.g., coating layer) or reduce $C_s$ (use sparingly soluble salt).
- **Steady release** → Maintain constant $A$ (spherical matrix) and $h$ (hydrodynamic boundary layer).
- **Accelerating release** → Reduce particle size (increases $A$), increase wetting.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Higuchi Square Root of Time Model (Matrix Diffusion)

### Step 1: Setup — Planar Matrix Geometry

A drug is homogeneously dispersed at initial concentration $C_0$ in an insoluble polymer matrix of thickness $L$. The matrix is exposed to a perfect sink on one side.

### Step 2: Fick's Second Law Application

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Logic:** Drug concentration within the matrix changes with time and position by diffusion.

### Step 3: Boundary and Initial Conditions

- $C(x, 0) = C_0$ for $0 < x < L$ (initial uniform loading)
- $C(0, t) = 0$ for $t > 0$ (perfect sink at surface)
- $C(L, t) = C_0$ (drug-rich interior acts as reservoir)

### Step 4: Higuchi's Assumption

If $C_0 \gg C_s$ (drug loading >> saturation solubility), then pseudo-steady state is reached. The fraction of drug released per unit area is derived as:

$$M_t = A \sqrt{D \cdot C_s \cdot (2C_0 - C_s) \cdot t}$$

**Logic:** Drug release from a matrix is proportional to the square root of time, not linear. This is the **hallmark of diffusion-controlled CR systems**.

### Step 5: Simplification (When $C_0 \gg C_s$)

$$M_t \approx A \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot C_s \cdot C_0 \cdot t}$$

**Logic:** Common in CR design — drug load vastly exceeds solubility, so the term $C_s$ inside the bracket can be neglected.

### Significance

- Plot of $M_t$ vs. $\sqrt{t}$ → straight line (Higuchi plot).
- Slope = $A \sqrt{2 D C_s C_0}$ → used to calculate $D$ experimentally.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Relationship Between Dose and Steady-State Plasma Concentration

### Step 1: Define Steady-State Condition

At steady state, drug input rate = drug elimination rate.

$$R_{in} = R_{out}$$

### Step 2: Express Input Rate from CR System

For a zero-order CR dosage form:

$$R_{in} = k_0 \quad \text{(mg/h)}$$

### Step 3: Express Output Rate from First-Order Elimination

$$R_{out} = CL \cdot C_{ss}$$

**Logic:** Clearance times steady-state concentration equals elimination rate.

### Step 4: Equate and Solve for $C_{ss}$

$$k_0 = CL \cdot C_{ss}$$

$$C_{ss} = \frac{k_0}{CL}$$

### Step 5: Relate $k_0$ to Dose and Dosing Interval

For a unit-dose CR system releasing over time $T$:

$$k_0 = \frac{F \cdot Dose}{T}$$

### Step 6: Final Equation

$$C_{ss} = \frac{F \cdot Dose}{CL \cdot T}$$

**Logic:** The steady-state plasma level depends directly on dose and inversely on clearance and dosing interval. This is the engineering equation for designing CR dosing regimens.

## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example — Full Design Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A pharmaceutical company wants to formulate a CR tablet of **Propranolol HCl** (MW = 295.8 Da, $C_s$ = 70 mg/mL, log P = 3.0, pKa = 9.45, t½ = 4 h, Vd = 270 L, CL = 50 L/h, F = 0.25, TI = wide). Calculate the dose required to maintain $C_{ss}$ of 50 ng/mL for 24 h dosing interval.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify target $C_{ss}$.

$$C_{ss} = 50 \, \text{ng/mL} = 0.05 \, \mu g/mL = 0.05 \, mg/L$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange the steady-state equation:

$$Dose = \frac{C_{ss} \cdot CL \cdot T}{F}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$Dose = \frac{0.05 \times 50 \times 24}{0.25} = \frac{60}{0.25} = 240 \, \text{mg}$$

**Step 4:** Verify half-life suitability.

Since t½ = 4 h, the natural duration of action is ≈ 4–6 h. A 24-h CR tablet extends this 4–6 fold — feasible and beneficial.

**Step 5:** Verify solubility.

$C_s$ = 70 mg/mL is high but acceptable if formulated as a reservoir or osmotic pump with controlled release membrane.

**Step 6:** Conclusion — Propranolol is an **ideal CR candidate** (wide TI, appropriate t½, high solubility, good permeability). The required dose of 240 mg in a CR tablet matches marketed products (e.g., Inderal LA 80–160 mg).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Property Criteria for CR Eligibility

| Property | Ideal Range for CR | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Aqueous Solubility ($C_s$) | 1–10 mg/mL | Allow controlled dissolution without dose dumping |
| log P | 1–3 | Balance solubility and permeability |
| Molecular Weight | < 500 Da | Permit diffusion through polymer matrices |
| pKa | 3–7 (for acids), 7–9 (for bases) | Ionization along GIT for absorption |
| Half-life ($t_{1/2}$) | 2–6 h | Frequent dosing in IR; CR reduces frequency |
| Therapeutic Index (TI) | > 10 | Wide safety margin tolerates CR fluctuations |
| Dose | < 500 mg | Larger doses need voluminous tablets |
| Bioavailability (F) | > 0.5 | Avoid excessive first-pass loss |
| Stability in GI fluids | Stable 8–24 h | Prevent degradation during extended release |
| Absorption mechanism | Passive diffusion | Active transport shows variable $F$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "controlled release" formulation. List any four physicochemical properties of drugs that are relevant to the design of CR formulations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A controlled release (CR) dosage form is one that releases one or more active ingredients at a controlled rate over an extended period, maintaining a therapeutically effective plasma concentration for a prolonged duration.

**Four Physicochemical Properties (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Aqueous Solubility ($C_s$):** Determines the dissolution rate and the polymer system's ability to retard release without dose dumping.
2. **Partition Coefficient (log P):** Governs permeability across biological membranes; ideal range 1–3 for CR.
3. **Molecular Weight (MW):** Influences diffusivity through polymer matrix; should be < 500 Da for diffusion-controlled CR.
4. **Acid Dissociation Constant (pKa):** Determines ionization at GI pH, affecting both solubility and absorption profile.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Explain why drugs with very short half-lives are preferred candidates for controlled release formulations. Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Explanation (2 marks):** Drugs with short plasma half-lives ($t_{1/2}$ < 2 h) require frequent dosing in conventional IR forms (e.g., 3–4 times daily), which causes poor patient compliance, fluctuating plasma levels (peak-trough), and increased side effects. Controlled release maintains steady plasma concentrations over 12–24 h, eliminating the need for repeated administration. Ideal $t_{1/2}$ for CR candidates: 2–6 h.

**Example (1 mark):** **Propranolol** ($t_{1/2}$ ≈ 4 h) — formulated as Inderal LA (long-acting) for once-daily dosing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

#### (a) Discuss in detail the physicochemical properties of drugs that govern the design of controlled release formulations. **[7 marks]**

#### (b) A drug with half-life 6 hours, volume of distribution 50 L, and clearance 5 L/h is to be formulated as a once-daily controlled release tablet. Calculate the steady-state plasma concentration and the loading dose. Assume bioavailability F = 0.8. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Detailed Discussion [7 marks]

The major physicochemical properties governing CR design are:

**(i) Aqueous Solubility (1.5 marks):** 
- Drug solubility dictates dissolution rate from the dosage form.
- Noyes–Whitney equation: $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = \dfrac{DA(C_s - C)}{h}$
- Very low solubility (< 0.1 mg/mL) → poor CR release (drug never dissolves).
- Very high solubility (> 10 mg/mL) → risk of dose dumping; requires coating/reservoir system.
- Ideal: 1–10 mg/mL (BCS Class I/II range).

**(ii) Partition Coefficient — log P (1 mark):**
- Determines membrane permeability and lipid solubility.
- log P 1–3 ideal; log P < 1 → poor permeability; log P > 5 → poor aqueous solubility and high protein binding.

**(iii) Molecular Weight and Diffusivity (1 mark):**
- Diffusion through polymer matrix is inversely related to molecular radius (Stokes–Einstein).
- MW > 500 Da restricts diffusion; MW < 400 Da is ideal.

**(iv) pKa and Ionization (1 mark):**
- Governs fraction of unionized drug available for membrane crossing.
- pH-dependent solubility (Henderson–Hasselbalch) affects CR along the GIT.

**(v) Drug Stability (0.5 mark):**
- Susceptibility to hydrolysis, oxidation, photolysis influences polymer and packaging choice.

**(vi) Polymorphism and Crystal Form (0.5 mark):**
- Different polymorphs have different solubilities → use stable form for reproducible CR release.

**(vii) Dose Size (0.5 mark):**
- Dose number $D_o$ calculated; very high doses are difficult to formulate as CR.

**(viii) Protein Binding (0.5 mark):**
- High protein binding reduces free fraction, prolongs effective half-life, affects CR pharmacokinetics.

**(ix) Biopharmaceutics Classification (1 mark):**
- BCS Class II drugs (low solubility, high permeability) are the **best CR candidates** because solubility enhancement combined with sustained release gives optimal oral delivery.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** $t_{1/2} = 6$ h, $V_d = 50$ L, $CL = 5$ L/h, $F = 0.8$, $T = 24$ h.

**Step 1: Verify Half-life Calculation (0.5 mark)**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693 \cdot V_d}{CL} = \frac{0.693 \times 50}{5} = \frac{34.65}{5} = 6.93 \, h$$

The given $t_{1/2} \approx 6$ h is consistent with these PK parameters.

**Step 2: Calculate Maintenance Dose (2 marks)**

Target $C_{ss}$ must be specified (assume therapeutic target $C_{ss} = 2$ mg/L).

$$Dose = \frac{C_{ss} \cdot CL \cdot T}{F} = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 24}{0.8} = \frac{240}{0.8} = 300 \, mg$$

**Step 3: Calculate Loading Dose (2 marks)**

$$LD = \frac{V_d \cdot C_{ss}}{F} = \frac{50 \times 2}{0.8} = \frac{100}{0.8} = 125 \, mg$$

**Step 4: Calculate Steady-State Plasma Concentration Using CR Input Rate (1.5 marks)**

For a CR tablet releasing the dose uniformly over 24 h:

$$k_0 = \frac{F \cdot Dose}{T} = \frac{0.8 \times 300}{24} = 10 \, mg/h$$

$$C_{ss} = \frac{k_0}{CL} = \frac{10}{5} = 2 \, mg/L$$

**Step 5: Discussion (0.5 mark)**

The CR formulation delivers 300 mg over 24 h, maintaining $C_{ss}$ = 2 mg/L with peak-to-trough ratio near unity — far superior to IR dosing 4 times daily.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

#### (a) Explain the biological properties of drugs that must be considered for designing controlled release formulations. **[7 marks]**

#### (b) Aspirin has pKa = 3.5. Calculate the percentage of ionized and unionized form at (i) stomach pH = 1.5 and (ii) intestinal pH = 6.5. Comment on the absorption in each region. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Biological Properties for CR Design [7 marks]

**(i) Absorption and Permeability (1.5 marks):**
- Drug must be efficiently absorbed from GI tract.
- High-permeability drugs (BCS I, II) are preferred.
- Site-specific absorption (e.g., vitamin B12 in ileum) limits CR design.

**(ii) Distribution — Volume of Distribution (1 mark):**
- Vd influences dose calculation; high Vd drugs require larger loading doses.

**(iii) Metabolism — Hepatic First-Pass (1.5 marks):**
- Drugs with high first-pass metabolism (e.g., nitroglycerin, propranolol) lose significant fraction orally.
- For such drugs, non-oral CR routes (transdermal, parenteral) are preferred.

**(iv) Elimination Half-Life (1.5 marks):**
- Ideal $t_{1/2}$: 2–6 h.
- Short $t_{1/2}$ → frequent dosing → CR beneficial.
- Long $t_{1/2}$ (> 8 h) → CR offers little advantage.

**(v) Therapeutic Index (1 mark):**
- Wide TI (TI > 10) → safe for CR; tolerates plasma fluctuations.
- Narrow TI (TI < 2) → risky; CR may push levels into toxic range.

**(vi) Bioavailability (0.5 mark):**
- F > 0.5 is preferred for oral CR; low F drugs may require alternative routes.

#### Part (b) — Aspirin Ionization Calculation [7 marks]

**Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation:**

$$pH = pK_a + \log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$$

For aspirin (weak acid): $pK_a = 3.5$.

**Case (i): Stomach pH = 1.5 (2 marks)**

$$1.5 = 3.5 + \log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$$

$$\log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} = 1.5 - 3.5 = -2.0$$

$$\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$$

So, total species = $[A^-] + [HA]$. Let $[HA] = 100$ units, $[A^-] = 1$ unit.

- **Unionized** = $\dfrac{100}{101} \times 100 = 99\%$ 
- **Ionized** = $\dfrac{1}{101} \times 100 = 0.99\%$

**Absorption comment:** In stomach, aspirin is ~99% unionized — readily absorbed across gastric mucosa. However, aspirin can also irritate the gastric lining.

**Case (ii): Intestinal pH = 6.5 (2 marks)**

$$6.5 = 3.5 + \log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$$

$$\log \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} = 6.5 - 3.5 = 3.0$$

$$\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} = 10^3 = 1000$$

So, $[A^-] = 1000 \cdot [HA]$. Let $[HA] = 1$ unit, $[A^-] = 1000$ units.

- **Unionized** = $\dfrac{1}{1001} \times 100 = 0.1\%$
- **Ionized** = $\dfrac{1000}{1001} \times 100 = 99.9\%$

**Absorption comment:** In intestine, aspirin is ~99.9% ionized — poorly absorbed directly. However, the huge absorptive surface area of the intestine compensates, and aspirin is also absorbed via paracellular and other pathways.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Despite pH-dependent ionization, aspirin is well absorbed from both stomach (high unionized fraction, small area) and intestine (low unionized fraction, large area). This dual-site absorption makes aspirin an interesting candidate for enteric-coated CR formulations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing log P with log D** — log D is pH-dependent (for ionizable drugs); log P is the intrinsic partition coefficient at neutral pH. Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Using CL in mg/h instead of L/h** — Units matter. A calculation with mixed units may yield incorrect answers and lose 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting Bioavailability (F) in loading dose equation** — $LD = V_d \cdot C_{ss} / F$, not $V_d \cdot C_{ss}$. This is a 1-mark deduction trap.
> 
> 4. **Wrong direction of Henderson–Hasselbalch ratio** — For acids: $\log([A^-]/[HA])$; for bases: $\log([BH^+]/[B])$ or $[B]/[BH^+]$ — students mix these up.
> 
> 5. **Neglecting the BCS classification** — Examiners expect BCS Class discussion for any drug property question. Omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Using "half-life" without units** — Always specify (h or min) in final answers.
> 
> 7. **Conflating "controlled release" with "sustained release"** — CR implies predictable zero-order kinetics; SR (sustained release) is broader (any prolonged release, often first-order). Examiners note this distinction strictly.
> 
> 8. **Skipping the "why"** — Writing properties as a list without explaining their relevance to CR design loses marks. Always link property → consequence in CR formulation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **CR Definition** — Release of API at controlled rate for extended duration, maintaining therapeutic plasma levels.
- [x] **Aqueous Solubility** — Ideal 1–10 mg/mL; Noyes–Whitney equation governs dissolution.
- [x] **log P (Partition Coefficient)** — Ideal 1–3 for oral CR; governs membrane permeability.
- [x] **Molecular Weight** — < 500 Da preferred; governs diffusivity through polymer.
- [x] **pKa** — Ionization pH-dependent via Henderson–Hasselbalch; only unionized form permeates.
- [x] **Dose Number** — $D_o = \dfrac{Dose}{C_s \cdot V_{GI}}$; > 1 indicates solubility-limited absorption.
- [x] **BCS Classification** — Class II (low sol, high perm) is **most suitable** for CR.
- [x] **Half-Life (t½)** — Ideal 2–6 h; calculated via $t_{1/2} = 0.693 V_d / CL$.
- [x] **Therapeutic Index** — Wide TI (TI > 10) preferred for CR safety.
- [x] **Bioavailability (F)** — $F = F_a \times F_g \times F_h$; preferred > 0.5 for oral CR.
- [x] **Volume of Distribution (V_d)** — Affects loading dose; $LD = V_d C_{ss} / F$.
- [x] **Clearance (CL)** — Determines steady-state concentration; $C_{ss} = k_0 / CL$.
- [x] **Higuchi Matrix Model** — $M_t \propto \sqrt{t}$; signature of diffusion-controlled CR.
- [x] **First-Pass Metabolism** — High $F_h$ loss drugs need non-oral CR routes.
- [x] **Polymorphism** — Stable polymorph used in CR for reproducible release.
- [x] **Stability** — Drug must remain stable 8–24 h in GI fluids.
- [x] **Protein Binding** — High binding prolongs effective half-life; affects CR pharmacodynamics.
- [x] **Steady-State Equation** — $C_{ss} = F \cdot Dose / (CL \cdot T)$ for design calculations.
- [x] **Common CR Drugs to Remember** — Propranolol, Theophylline, Metformin (XR), Nifedipine (GITS), Diclofenac, Diltiazem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB862 - DRUG DELIVERY PRINCIPLE & APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Pharmaceutical Sciences and Drug Delivery (KTU 2024 Scheme, Course Code PEBB862), **Classification** refers to the systematic, scientifically-rationalized categorization of pharmaceutical dosage forms, drug delivery systems (DDS), and routes of drug administration based on:
1. **Physical state** (solid, liquid, semi-solid, gaseous)
2. **Route of administration** (enteral, parenteral, topical, transdermal, inhalational)
3. **Site of action** (local vs. systemic)
4. **Mechanism of drug release** (immediate, modified, sustained, controlled, targeted, delayed)
5. **Generation/type of delivery system** (conventional → first generation → second generation → third generation → fourth generation smart systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** A student must be able to differentiate between the **four major drug release patterns**: *Immediate Release (IR)*, *Sustained Release (SR)*, *Controlled Release (CR)*, and *Targeted Release (TR)*. This classification is the foundational cognitive anchor for Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mail & Courier" Model

Imagine the human body as a **giant city**, and a drug molecule as a **letter or package** that needs to reach a specific recipient (the receptor site / diseased cell).

| Courier Concept | Pharmaceutical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Ordinary postal letter (delivered anytime) | **Conventional / Immediate Release** dosage form |
| Registered post with delivery confirmation | **Sustained Release** (prolonged, but rate can vary) |
| Speed post with timestamp tracking | **Controlled Release** (zero-order, predictable rate) |
| Courier addressed to a specific person/building only | **Targeted Delivery** (ligand-guided, site-specific) |
| Smart locker with code opening only at a fixed time | **Stimuli-Responsive / Triggered Release** |

This Mail & Courier analogy helps visualize why **classification is the first step in pharmaceutical design** — the formulator must first decide *how the drug should reach the destination* before engineering the dosage form.

## 1.3 Physical State Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **Plasma Half-life ($t_{1/2}$):** Time for drug concentration to fall to 50% of its peak. Typical range: **0.5 – 24 hours**.
> - **Bioavailability (F):** Fraction of administered drug reaching systemic circulation. Unit: **dimensionless % (0–100%)**.
> - **Therapeutic Index (TI):** Ratio $\frac{TD_{50}}{ED_{50}}$. **TI > 10** is generally considered "safe".
> - **Dosing Interval ($\tau$):** Time between successive doses, measured in **hours**.

## 1.4 Why Classification is the Gateway Module

Classification provides the **decision-tree framework** upon which all subsequent modules are built. Without a robust classification map, a pharmacist cannot rationally select an appropriate delivery system for a given drug, disease state, or patient population.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Tier Classification Framework

### **Tier 1 — Classification by Route of Administration**

| Sub-class | Definition | Common Dosage Forms |
|---|---|---|
| **Enteral** | Via GI tract (oral, sublingual, rectal) | Tablets, capsules, syrups, suppositories |
| **Parenteral** | Bypassing GI tract (injection, infusion) | IV, IM, SC, intradermal injections |
| **Topical** | Applied to skin/mucosa for local effect | Creams, ointments, gels, patches |
| **Transdermal** | Through skin for systemic effect | TDDS patches, microneedles |
| **Inhalational** | Via respiratory tract | MDIs, DPIs, nebulizers, nasal sprays |
| **Ophthalmic/Otic/Nasal** | Local delivery to specific organs | Eye/ear drops, nasal aerosols |

### **Tier 2 — Classification by Physical State**

- **Solid dosage forms:** Tablets, capsules, lozenges, powders, granules.
- **Liquid dosage forms:** Solutions, suspensions, emulsions, syrups, elixirs.
- **Semi-solid dosage forms:** Ointments, creams, gels, pastes.
- **Gaseous/aerosol forms:** Inhalers, pressurized metered-dose inhalers (pMDIs).
- **Novel forms:** Nanoparticles, liposomes, micelles, dendrimers.

### **Tier 3 — Classification by Mechanism of Drug Release**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The four release kinetics categories — the heart of Module 1:**

1. **Immediate Release (IR):** Drug released rapidly upon administration; peak plasma in < 1 hour.
2. **Modified Release (MR):** Umbrella term for non-conventional release.
   - *Sustained Release (SR):* Drug released over an extended period, but rate may decline over time.
   - *Controlled Release (CR):* Drug released at a *constant (zero-order)* or *predictable* rate.
   - *Extended Release (ER/XR):* Reduces dosing frequency (e.g., once-daily).
   - *Delayed Release (DR):* Release occurs at a specific time/site (e.g., enteric-coated).
3. **Targeted Release:** Drug delivered to a specific organ/tissue/cell.
   - *Passive Targeting:* Exploits EPR effect in tumors.
   - *Active Targeting:* Ligand-mediated (e.g., antibody-functionalized nanoparticles).
4. **Stimuli-Responsive / Triggered Release:** Release activated by pH, temperature, enzyme, light, magnetic field, or ultrasound.

### **Tier 4 — Classification by Site/Target**

- **Local Delivery:** Acts at site of application (e.g., topical creams).
- **Systemic Delivery:** Enters bloodstream → distributed throughout body.
- **Site-Specific/Targeted:** Reaches a specific organ/tissue (e.g., brain, tumor).

### **Tier 5 — Generation-Based Classification of DDS**

| Generation | Key Feature | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1st (1950s–70s) | Conventional tablets/capsules | Aspirin tablet |
| 2nd (1970s–90s) | Sustained/Controlled release | OROS tablets |
| 3rd (1990s–2010s) | Targeted & nanoparticles | Liposomal doxorubicin (Doxil®) |
| 4th (2010s–Present) | Smart/responsive systems | Insulin-responsive glucose-sensitive patch |
| 5th (Emerging) | Digital/closed-loop & bioelectronic | Ingestible sensor-pill combinations |

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Release Category

### **2.2.1 Sustained Release — The "Slow Drip" Principle**
- **Why?** To reduce dosing frequency, minimize peak-trough fluctuations, and improve patient compliance.
- **How?** The drug is embedded in a matrix (hydrophobic polymer like HPMC, ethylcellulose) that erodes or diffuses drug out over **6–24 hours**.

### **2.2.2 Controlled Release — The "Constant Flow" Principle**
- **Why?** To maintain a *constant plasma drug concentration* ($C_{ss}$) within the therapeutic window, avoiding sub-therapeutic or toxic levels.
- **How?** Osmotic pumps (e.g., OROS technology), reservoir systems with rate-controlling membranes, or erodible matrices engineered for zero-order kinetics.

### **2.2.3 Targeted Release — The "GPS-Guided Missile" Principle**
- **Why?** To concentrate drug at the disease site (e.g., tumor), sparing healthy tissues → **reduces side effects**.
- **How?** Two main strategies:
  - **Passive:** Nanoparticles of 50–200 nm accumulate in tumor interstitium due to the **Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect**.
  - **Active:** Surface-conjugated ligands (antibodies, peptides, folic acid) bind to over-expressed receptors on target cells.

### **2.2.4 Stimuli-Responsive — The "Smart Trigger" Principle**
- **Why?** Release on demand, only when disease-specific biomarker is detected.
- **How?** Polymers that undergo conformational change in response to:
  - **pH** (e.g., enteric polymers dissolve at pH > 6 in the intestine).
  - **Temperature** (e.g., PNIPAM shrinks above 32°C).
  - **Enzymes** (e.g., MMP-2 cleaves peptide linkers in tumors).
  - **Magnetic field** (magnetic nanoparticles + external magnet).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1 — Classification)

| Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{ss} = \frac{F \cdot D}{CL \cdot \tau}$ | Average steady-state plasma concentration | mg/L |
| $D_{loading} = \frac{V_d \cdot C_{target}}{F}$ | Loading dose to immediately achieve target concentration | mg |
| $D_{maintenance} = CL \cdot C_{ss} \cdot \tau / F$ | Maintenance dose to sustain $C_{ss}$ | mg |
| $\tau = \frac{\ln(2)}{k_e}$ | Dosing interval based on elimination rate constant | hours |
| $TI = \frac{TD_{50}}{ED_{50}}$ | Therapeutic index (safety margin) | dimensionless |
| $F_{rel} = 1 - e^{-k_e \cdot t}$ | Fraction of drug released by time $t$ (first-order) | dimensionless |
| $\frac{dM}{dt} = k_0$ (zero-order) | Constant drug release rate (CR systems) | mg/hr |
| $t_{max} = \frac{\ln(k_a/k_e)}{k_a - k_e}$ | Time of peak concentration | hours |
| $AUC = F \cdot D / CL$ | Area Under the Curve (bioavailability measure) | mg·hr/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** For *zero-order* controlled release, $\frac{dM}{dt}$ is **constant** and **independent** of drug concentration in the dosage form. For *first-order* sustained release, the rate is **proportional** to the residual drug concentration, i.e., $\frac{dM}{dt} = -k \cdot M$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This classification framework is the **backbone of the pharmaceutical R&D industry**. Every novel drug candidate entering clinical trials must first be matched to a delivery class:
- A **peptide drug** (poor oral bioavailability) → **parenteral / nanoparticulate** class.
- A **chronotherapeutic** indication (e.g., nocturnal asthma) → **delayed-release / pulsatile** class.
- A **cancer chemotherapeutic** (high toxicity) → **targeted / stimuli-responsive** class.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Zero-Order vs. First-Order Release Kinetics

### 3.1.1 First-Order Release (Sustained Release Systems)

A first-order release system releases drug at a rate proportional to the remaining drug concentration in the dosage unit.

**Step 1: Write the rate equation.**

The rate of drug leaving the dosage form is proportional to the drug amount remaining:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = -k \cdot M(t)$$

where:
- $M(t)$ = amount of drug remaining in the dosage form at time $t$
- $k$ = first-order release rate constant (units: $\text{hr}^{-1}$)
- $\frac{dM}{dt}$ = rate of drug release (negative because mass decreases)

**Step 2: Separate variables and integrate.**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dM}{M} = -k \cdot dt$$

Integrating both sides from $t = 0$ (when $M = M_0$) to $t = t$ (when $M = M(t)$):

$$\int_{M_0}^{M(t)} \frac{dM}{M} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

The left integral gives $\ln(M)$, so:

$$\ln(M(t)) - \ln(M_0) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 3: Solve for $M(t)$.**

$$\ln\left(\frac{M(t)}{M_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\boxed{M(t) = M_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}}$$

**Step 4: Express cumulative drug released $Q(t)$.**

The cumulative drug released by time $t$ is:

$$Q(t) = M_0 - M(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)$$

**Step 5: Derive the half-release time.**

Setting $M(t) = M_0 / 2$:

$$\frac{M_0}{2} = M_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}^{rel}}$$

$$e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}^{rel}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking natural log:

$$-k \cdot t_{1/2}^{rel} = -\ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2}^{rel} = \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}}$$

### 3.1.2 Zero-Order Release (Controlled Release Systems)

A zero-order release system releases drug at a **constant rate**, independent of remaining drug amount.

**Step 1: Write the rate equation.**

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = -k_0$$

where $k_0$ is the zero-order release rate constant (units: mg/hr).

**Step 2: Integrate.**

Integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$M(t) - M_0 = -k_0 \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{M(t) = M_0 - k_0 \cdot t}$$

**Step 3: Express cumulative drug released.**

$$Q(t) = k_0 \cdot t$$

This is a **linear function of time** — the defining feature of controlled-release systems.

**Step 4: Derive the time to complete release.**

Setting $M(t) = 0$:

$$0 = M_0 - k_0 \cdot t_{complete}$$

$$\boxed{t_{complete} = \frac{M_0}{k_0}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Release Kinetics

| Property | First-Order (Sustained) | Zero-Order (Controlled) |
|---|---|---|
| Rate equation | $\frac{dM}{dt} = -k M$ | $\frac{dM}{dt} = -k_0$ |
| Drug remaining $M(t)$ | $M_0 e^{-kt}$ | $M_0 - k_0 t$ |
| Cumulative release $Q(t)$ | $M_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ | $k_0 t$ (linear) |
| Plasma profile | Declining $C_p$ over time | Flat $C_p$ at steady state |
| Half-release time | $\frac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Not constant (depends on $M_0$) |
| Dose dumping risk | Lower | Higher if membrane ruptures |
| Patient compliance | Better than IR | Best (once-daily dosing) |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style Problem)

**Problem:** A sustained-release tablet contains $M_0 = 500$ mg of drug with first-order release constant $k = 0.15 \text{ hr}^{-1}$.

**(a) Calculate the amount of drug released after 4 hours.**

$$Q(4) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)$$

$$Q(4) = 500 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.15 \times 4}\right)$$

$$Q(4) = 500 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.60}\right)$$

Using $e^{-0.60} \approx 0.5488$:

$$Q(4) = 500 \times (1 - 0.5488)$$

$$Q(4) = 500 \times 0.4512$$

$$\boxed{Q(4) = 225.6 \text{ mg}}$$

**(b) Calculate the half-release time.**

$$t_{1/2}^{rel} = \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.15}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2}^{rel} = 4.62 \text{ hours}}$$

**(c) If the same tablet were formulated as zero-order with $k_0 = 50$ mg/hr, what is the drug released in 4 hours?**

$$Q(4) = k_0 \cdot t = 50 \times 4$$

$$\boxed{Q(4) = 200 \text{ mg}}$$

**Interpretation:** Even though the *amount* released is similar at 4 hours, the **temporal profile** differs — the zero-order system delivers a **uniform 200 mg over 4 hr (50 mg/hr)**, while the first-order delivers drug rapidly at first, then slowing down.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Modified Release dosage forms. List its sub-types.**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**
Modified Release (MR) dosage forms are those in which the rate or site of release of the active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) is different from that of the conventional immediate-release form. The release is intentionally altered by special formulation design or manufacturing method.

**Sub-types of Modified Release:**
1. **Sustained Release (SR)** — prolonged release with declining rate.
2. **Controlled Release (CR)** — release at constant (zero-order) rate.
3. **Extended Release (ER)** — prolonged duration, reduces dosing frequency.
4. **Delayed Release (DR)** — release triggered after a lag time (e.g., enteric-coated).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for sub-types)*

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Sustained Release and Controlled Release systems.**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sustained Release | Controlled Release |
|---|---|---|
| Release rate | Declines with time (first-order) | Constant (zero-order) |
| Plasma profile | Variable | Flat plateau at $C_{ss}$ |
| Mathematical form | $Q(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ | $Q(t) = k_0 t$ |
| Predictability | Moderate | High |
| Example | HPMC matrix tablet | OROS osmotic pump |

*(3 marks: tabular comparison with example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Classify drug delivery systems based on (i) route of administration, and (ii) mechanism of drug release. Give suitable examples for each category.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification by Route of Administration:**

| Route | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Enteral** | Via GI tract | Oral tablets, capsules, syrups |
| **Parenteral** | Bypassing GI tract | IV, IM, SC injections |
| **Topical** | Local skin/mucosal effect | Creams, ointments, gels |
| **Transdermal** | Systemic via skin | Nicotine patch, fentanyl patch |
| **Inhalational** | Via respiratory tract | Salbutamol MDI, insulin DPI |
| **Ophthalmic/Otic/Nasal** | Local organ delivery | Eye drops, ear drops, nasal spray |

**(ii) Classification by Mechanism of Drug Release:**

1. **Immediate Release (IR)** — Drug released within minutes (e.g., paracetamol tablet).
2. **Sustained Release (SR)** — Prolonged release with declining rate (e.g., theophylline SR tablet).
3. **Controlled Release (CR)** — Zero-order release at constant rate (e.g., OROS oxybutynin).
4. **Delayed Release (DR)** — Release after a lag time (e.g., enteric-coated aspirin).
5. **Targeted Release** — Site-specific delivery (e.g., liposomal doxorubicin / Doxil®).
6. **Stimuli-Responsive Release** — Triggered by pH/temperature/enzyme (e.g., glucose-sensitive insulin patch).

*(7 marks: 3 for route classification with examples + 4 for release mechanism with examples)*

**(b) Explain the Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect. How is it exploited in passive tumor targeting using nanoparticulate drug delivery systems?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **EPR effect** is a physiological phenomenon first described by **Yasuhiro Matsumura and Hiroshi Maeda (1986)**. It refers to the **abnormal vascular architecture and impaired lymphatic drainage** observed in solid tumor tissues.

**Key Features:**
1. **Hypervasculature:** Tumor tissues over-express pro-angiogenic factors (VEGF, bFGF), resulting in leaky, poorly-aligned blood vessels with gaps of **200 nm – 1.2 µm** between endothelial cells.
2. **Poor lymphatic drainage:** Tumor tissues lack functional lymphatic vessels, so interstitial fluid is not cleared efficiently.

**Exploitation in Nanoparticulate DDS:**

Nanoparticles of size **50–200 nm** (liposomes, polymeric NPs, micelles) are administered IV. Because they are:
- **Too large** to penetrate normal blood vessel walls (which have tight junctions),
- **Small enough** to squeeze through tumor endothelial gaps,
- **Retained** in tumor interstitium due to poor lymphatic clearance,

they accumulate selectively in tumor tissue, achieving **10–50× higher drug concentration** than in healthy tissue.

**Example:** Doxil® (liposomal doxorubicin) — a 100 nm PEGylated liposome that uses the EPR effect to deliver doxorubicin to Kaposi's sarcoma and ovarian carcinoma.

*(7 marks: 2 for EPR definition + 2 for mechanism + 2 for nano exploitation + 1 for example)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Define Targeted Drug Delivery. Differentiate between active and passive targeting with examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Targeted Drug Delivery (TDD):** A strategy in which the drug is delivered preferentially to a specific organ, tissue, cell type, or intracellular compartment, thereby maximizing therapeutic efficacy and minimizing off-target side effects.

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Passive Targeting | Active Targeting |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Relies on **physiological/pathological** differences (e.g., EPR effect) | Relies on **ligand-receptor** interactions |
| Ligand needed? | No | Yes (antibody, peptide, aptamer) |
| Selectivity | Moderate (tumor accumulation) | High (cell-specific) |
| Example | Doxil® (liposomal doxorubicin) | Trastuzumab-conjugated liposome for HER2+ breast cancer |

**Examples in detail:**
- **Passive:** Stealth (PEGylated) liposomes of ~100 nm accumulate in solid tumors via EPR effect.
- **Active:** Folic acid-conjugated nanoparticles bind to folate receptors over-expressed on many cancer cells.

*(7 marks: 1 for definition + 3 for comparison + 3 for examples)*

**(b) Describe the construction, working principle, and advantages of an Osmotic Pump (OROS) drug delivery system. How does it achieve zero-order release?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of Elementary Osmotic Pump (Oros®):**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│       Drug Compartment          │  ← solid drug core (M_0)
│       (with osmotic agent)      │
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│  Semipermeable Membrane         │  ← cellulose acetate
│  (with laser-drilled orifice)   │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

Components:
1. **Core tablet** containing the drug + osmotic agent (e.g., NaCl, KCl).
2. **Semipermeable membrane** of cellulose acetate surrounding the core.
3. **Delivery orifice** (typically 0.1–1 mm diameter) laser-drilled through the membrane.

**Working Principle:**

Step 1: When the pump contacts aqueous GI fluid, water is imbibed osmotically across the semipermeable membrane into the core due to the high osmotic pressure of the dissolved osmotic agent.

Step 2: The imbibed water dissolves the drug, generating a saturated solution inside the core.

Step 3: The hydrostatic pressure inside the core rises, forcing the saturated drug solution out through the delivery orifice.

Step 4: The rate of water influx, and hence drug efflux, is governed by **the surface area of the membrane, its permeability, and the osmotic pressure difference** — all of which are **constant**.

Step 5: Because the driving force (osmotic pressure) is held constant until the entire core is dissolved, drug release follows:

$$Q(t) = k_0 \cdot t \quad \text{(zero-order)}$$

**Why Zero-Order?**

The release rate is mathematically described by:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot L_w \cdot \sigma \cdot \Delta\pi}{h}$$

where:
- $A$ = membrane surface area (constant)
- $L_w$ = water permeability (constant)
- $\sigma$ = reflection coefficient (constant)
- $\Delta\pi$ = osmotic pressure difference (constant)
- $h$ = membrane thickness (constant)

Since all parameters are constants, $\frac{dM}{dt}$ is **constant**, achieving true **zero-order kinetics**.

**Advantages:**
1. True zero-order release, independent of GI pH and agitation.
2. Reduced dosing frequency → improved patient compliance.
3. Minimized peak-trough fluctuations → fewer side effects.
4. Predictable in vivo performance.

**Examples:** OROS® oxybutynin (Ditropan XL®), OROS® methylphenidate (Concerta®).

*(7 marks: 1 for diagram + 2 for working + 2 for zero-order derivation + 2 for advantages)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Modified Release" with "Controlled Release":** MR is the umbrella term; CR is a specific subtype with zero-order kinetics. Marks deducted if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the EPR effect's discoverers** (Matsumura and Maeda, 1986) in passive targeting questions — examiners specifically reward historical context.
> 3. **Skipping the units** in numerical derivations (e.g., writing $k = 0.15$ without $\text{hr}^{-1}$).
> 4. **Writing "zero-order" without stating the boundary condition** that the rate is independent of drug concentration — this is the *defining* property tested in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Not drawing the OROS diagram** when asked about osmotic pumps — a labeled schematic earns 1 mark minimum even if the rest is incomplete.
> 6. **Mixing up transdermal and topical** routes: transdermal = systemic; topical = local. A common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Classification is the **foundational Module 1 topic** — every other module builds on it.
- ✅ **Four release kinetics** to master: *Immediate, Sustained (1st order), Controlled (0 order), Targeted*.
- ✅ **Five routes** to differentiate: *Enteral, Parenteral, Topical, Transdermal, Inhalational*.
- ✅ **EPR effect** = backbone of passive tumor targeting (Matsumura & Maeda, 1986).
- ✅ **Zero-order** rate equation: $\frac{dM}{dt} = k_0$ → linear $Q(t) = k_0 t$.
- ✅ **First-order** rate equation: $\frac{dM}{dt} = -kM$ → exponential $M(t) = M_0 e^{-kt}$.
- ✅ **OROS osmotic pump** uses semipermeable membrane + osmotic agent + delivery orifice → true zero-order.
- ✅ **Targeted DDS** has two flavors: **Passive (EPR)** and **Active (ligand-receptor)**.
- ✅ **Stimuli-responsive** systems trigger release by pH, temperature, enzyme, or magnetic field.
- ✅ **Therapeutic Index (TI)** guides whether a drug *needs* targeted delivery (TI < 10).
- ✅ **Generation-based classification** (1st conventional → 5th digital/bioelectronic) is frequently asked as a 3-mark question.
- ✅ Always quote **examples** in long-answer questions: *Doxil®, OROS Concerta®, PEGylated liposomes*.
- ✅ Always state **units** in derivations; always **draw diagrams** for delivery systems (1 free mark).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
