**Definition:** A solid dispersion is a **two-component system** of drug dispersed at **molecular level** in an inert hydrophilic/lipophilic carrier matrix.

**Types:**

| Type | Structure | Example |
|------|-----------|---------|
| Eutectic mixture | Crystalline drug + crystalline carrier | Chloramphenicol + Urea |
| Solid solution (continuous) | Drug molecules occupy lattice sites | Itraconazole + PEG 6000 |
| Solid solution (substitutional) | Drug substitutes for carrier molecule | Limited pharmaceutical use |
| Amorphous precipitation | Drug precipitates as amorphous in crystalline carrier | Nifedipine + PVP |
| Glass suspension | Drug suspended in amorphous carrier glass | Felodipine + HPMC |

**Methods of Preparation:**

1. **Hot Melt Extrusion (HME)** — drug + carrier melted, extruded, cooled.
2. **Solvent Evaporation** — drug + carrier dissolved in common solvent, evaporated.
3. **Spray Drying** — co-dissolved solution atomized in hot air.
4. **Lyophilization (Freeze Drying)** — frozen solution sublimed.
5. **Melt Agglomeration** — molten carrier agglomerated with drug powder.
6. **Electrospinning** — fibers of sub-micron diameter formed.

**Advantages:**

- Increases apparent solubility by **10–100×**
- Eliminates polymorphic form concerns
- Masks unpleasant taste
- Suitable for BCS Class II drugs

**Model Solution (b):**

**Polymers used:** PLGA (poly-lactic-co-glycolic acid) — most common; PLA, PCL, chitosan.

**Methods:**

- **Solvent evaporation** (o/w or w/o/w emulsion)
- **Phase separation (coacervation)**
- **Spray drying**

**Evaluation Parameters:**

- Particle size (laser diffraction)
- $\mathrm{DLC}$ and $\mathrm{EE}$
- Zeta potential (surface charge)
- Morphology (SEM/TEM)
- In-vitro release in PBS pH 7.4
- Sterility (for parenteral use)

**Release Kinetics:**
PLGA degradation follows **bulk erosion** (for high-MW PLGA) with release typically fitting:

$$\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = k_H \, \sqrt{t} \quad \text{(Higuchi) for matrix type}$$

Or the **Korsmeyer-Peppas** model:

$$\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = k \, t^n$$

where $n$ determines mechanism:
- $n = 0.5$: Fickian diffusion
- $n = 1.0$: Zero-order (case-II transport)
- $0.5 < n < 1.0$: Anomalous transport

---

#### **Question B — Osmotic Pumps + Implants**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**  
**(a)** Describe the **elementary osmotic pump (EOP)** with a neat labelled schematic. Derive the expression for zero-order release rate and list two marketed examples. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Discuss the design and biomedical applications of **dense implantable drug delivery systems**, with emphasis on EVA and silicone matrices. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Component layout of EOP:**

1. **Drug core** (tablet) — high-density osmotic agent + API.
2. **Semipermeable membrane** (cellulose acetate).
3. **Orifice** (laser-drilled hole, ~ 250 μm).

**Working principle:**

1. Water from GI tract permeates through the membrane (osmosis).
2. Drug + osmotic agent dissolve inside.
3. Saturated solution is **pushed out** through the orifice at constant rate.

**Derivation of release rate:**

$$\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \pi \cdot C_s}{h}$$

where $A, \pi, C_s, h$ are constant → **zero-order kinetics**.

**Marketed examples:**
- **OROS® methylphenidate** (Concerta) — ADHD.
- **OROS® nifedipine** (Procardia XL) — antihypertensive.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Implant Design Criteria:**
- Biocompatible polymer matrix
- Non-degradable for long-term or biodegradable for fixed-duration
- Sterile, implantable by minor surgery

**EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate):**
- Non-degradable
- Releases drug by **diffusion through pores** formed by leaching of co-extruded soluble filler
- Example: **Gliadel® wafer** (carmustine for brain tumor post-resection)
- Example: **Probuphine®** (buprenorphine for opioid dependence, 6-month implant)

**Silicone (PDMS):**
- Highly biocompatible
- Used in **contraceptive implants** like **Norplant®** (levonorgestrel, 5-year release)
- Releases via **partition-controlled diffusion** following Fick's First Law:

$$J = -D \, \dfrac{dC}{dx}$$

where $J$ is flux, $D$ is diffusion coefficient, $dC/dx$ is concentration gradient across silicone wall.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse DLE and EE** — they have different denominators in liposomal formulations. DLE uses total mass of system, EE uses initially added drug only.
> 2. **Always state units** (mg, cm², h) when writing formula substitutions.
> 3. **Draw labelled Mermaid/text diagrams** in 14-mark answers — examiners assign 2–3 marks for diagrams alone.
> 4. **Never write "high drug loading" without a number** — quantitative DLC values earn full credit.
> 5. **Mention polymer grade** (e.g., PLGA 50:50 vs 75:25) when discussing release duration.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition anchor:** High-Density Systems carry **> 30 % w/w drug** in the carrier matrix.
- **Key formulas (must memorize):** DLC, DLE, EE, Carr Index, Hausner Ratio, Higuchi model.
- **Five major sub-classes:** Solid Dispersions, Co-crystals/Co-amorphous, SLN/NLC, Polymeric Microspheres, Osmotic Pumps, Implants.
- **Carrier matrices commonly tested:** PEG, PVP, PLGA, EVA, silicone, tristearin.
- **Release kinetics tri-set:** Zero-order (osmotic), Higuchi (matrix diffusion), Korsmeyer-Peppas (anomalous).
- **Characterization mandatory list:** Particle size, zeta potential, DLC, EE, in-vitro release.
- **Industrial examples to quote in answers:** Gliadel®, Norplant®, OROS-Concerta®, Probuphine®, Abelcet® (amphotericin B lipid complex).
- **Polymorphic transition trap:** SLNs lose drug load when lipid converts from α → β form during storage.
- **Sterility is non-negotiable** for any parenteral / implantable high-density system.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inflatable and Gastro-Adhesive Systems and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB862 - DRUG DELIVERY PRINCIPLE & APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inflatable and Gastro-Adhesive Systems and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Inflatable Drug Delivery Systems (IDDS)

**Formal Definition:**
An **Inflatable Drug Delivery System (IDDS)** is an advanced, site-specific pharmaceutical device engineered to undergo controlled volume expansion (inflation) once it reaches a target body cavity, lumen, or tissue site. Inflation is generally achieved using **osmotically active agents**, **biodegradable gas-generating polymers**, or **swelling hydrogels**, which allows the device to mechanically anchor itself, deliver the drug over an extended period, and resist premature transit through the gastrointestinal (GI) tract.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Inflatable systems are categorized under **gastroretentive drug delivery systems (GRDDS)** because they remain in the stomach for prolonged periods by physically expanding to a size larger than the pyloric sphincter diameter (**~2 cm in adults**).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Inflatable Life Jacket:**
Imagine a small folded life jacket packed inside a sealed pouch. Once it enters water (the stomach), it inflates automatically. Now imagine a tiny pharmaceutical "life jacket" loaded with medicine. It stays inflated and floating at one location in the stomach, slowly releasing the drug, instead of being swept downstream. The expanding balloon **mechanically prevents the device from passing through narrow openings**, just as an inflated object cannot fit through a small gap.

### 1.2 Gastro-Adhesive (Bioadhesive) Drug Delivery Systems

**Formal Definition:**
**Gastro-adhesive systems** (a subset of **mucoadhesive systems**) are drug delivery platforms formulated with bioadhesive polymers that form non-covalent bonds — primarily **hydrogen bonds**, **van der Waals forces**, and **electrostatic interactions** — with the mucin layer lining the gastric or intestinal epithelium. This adhesion prolongs **gastric residence time (GRT)**, enhances drug absorption at the target site, and provides localized therapy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Mechanism:** Adhesion occurs through the **wetting theory** (polymer spreads on mucosa) followed by interpenetration of polymer chains into the mucin glycoprotein network and subsequent bond formation.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Sticky Note on a Wall:**
Think of a sticky note pressed against a wallpapered wall. The adhesive on the note (polymer) bonds with the wall's textured surface (mucus layer), and it stays there for hours. Similarly, bioadhesive polymers "stick" to the stomach lining, ensuring the drug is released where it is needed, especially useful for drugs that are **poorly absorbed in the lower GI tract** or that degrade in intestinal pH.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Average Pyloric Diameter:** **1.5 – 2.0 cm** in fasting adults
- **Stomach pH range:** **1.0 – 3.5** (fasting) and **2.0 – 5.0** (fed)
- **Mucus turnover time:** **50 – 270 minutes** (limits true GRT)
- **Mucin glycoprotein molecular weight:** **~1 × 10⁶ Da**
- **Polymer glass transition temperature (Tg):** critical for flexibility/adhesion balance

---

### Key Design Parameters for Inflatable & Gastro-Adhesive Systems

| Parameter | Inflatable System | Gastro-Adhesive System |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary retention mechanism** | Mechanical size expansion | Mucin polymer bonding |
| **GRT achievable** | **4 – 12 hours** | **2 – 6 hours** |
| **Limiting factor** | Pyloric sphincter size | Mucus turnover rate |
| **Ideal drug candidates** | Narrow absorption window drugs | Locally acting, unstable drugs |
| **Polymer examples** | PVA, PEG, superporous hydrogels | Carbopol, chitosan, HPMC |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism of Inflatable Systems

The working principle can be broken into **four sequential stages**:

- **Step 1 — Ingestion & Gastric Transit:** The device is administered as a small, folded, easily swallowable unit (typically **size 000 capsule** dimensions: ~26 × 9.9 mm).
- **Step 2 — Activation Trigger:** Upon contact with gastric fluid, an internal **osmotically active core** (e.g., sodium bicarbonate, citric acid) reacts to liberate **CO₂ gas**, OR a **superporous hydrogel (SPH)** swells rapidly by absorbing water.
- **Step 3 — Controlled Inflation:** The device expands to a diameter of **3 – 5 cm**, exceeding the pyloric sphincter aperture, thereby achieving **gastroretention** through mechanical anchoring.
- **Step 4 — Sustained Drug Release:** Drug embedded in the polymer matrix or reservoir is released via **diffusion**, **erosion**, or **osmotic pumping** for an extended duration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The inflated geometry creates a physical barrier preventing passage through the pylorus, while the polymer shell modulates drug release kinetics.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Gastro-Adhesion — A Three-Stage Model

- **Stage 1 — Wetting & Contact:** The dry or hydrated polymer formulation must first **spread and wet** the mucosal surface. The **spreading coefficient (S)** must be positive:

$$S = \gamma_{SM} - \gamma_{SP} - \gamma_{PM}$$

where $\gamma_{SM}$, $\gamma_{SP}$, $\gamma_{PM}$ are the interfacial tensions at the substrate-mucus, substrate-polymer, and polymer-mucus interfaces respectively.

- **Stage 2 — Interpenetration & Chain Entanglement:** Polymer chains (in a relaxed state above their Tg) diffuse into the mucin glycoprotein layer, forming a **semi-interpenetrating network (semi-IPN)**. Depth of interpenetration: typically **0.2 – 0.5 µm**.

- **Stage 3 — Bond Consolidation:** Secondary bonds form — primarily **hydrogen bonds** between polymer –COOH / –OH groups and mucin sialic acid residues.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Spreading Coefficient** | $S = \gamma_{SM} - \gamma_{SP} - \gamma_{PM}$ | $\gamma$ in $\text{N/m}$ or $\text{mN/m}$ |
| **Young's Equation** (contact angle) | $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = contact angle |
| **Swelling Ratio (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{W_s - W_d}{W_d}$ | $W_s$ swollen, $W_d$ dry mass |
| **Peppas-Korsmeyer Release** | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = k \, t^{n}$ | $n$ = diffusional exponent |
| **Fick's First Law (diffusion)** | $J = -D \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $D$ = diffusion coefficient $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| **Bioadhesive Force (F)** | $F = \dfrac{W \cdot A}{g}$ | $W$ = weight needed to detach, $A$ = area |
| **Higuchi Matrix Release** | $Q = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \, \epsilon}{\tau} \, (2A - \epsilon C_s) \, C_s \, t}$ | Applicable to porous matrices |
| **PEG Crosslink Density (ν)** | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho}{\overline{M_c}}$ | $\rho$ polymer density, $\overline{M_c}$ avg MW between crosslinks |
| **Mucus Turnover Constraint** | $\tau_{GRT} \leq \tau_{mucus}$ | True GRT cannot exceed mucus turnover time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Inflatable systems are used clinically in **balloon-expandable coronary stents**, **gastric balloons for obesity treatment** (e.g., **Orbera**, **ReShape**), and **intragastric levodopa delivery (DUODOPA® pump systems)**. Gastro-adhesive systems underpin commercial products like **Calan® SR** and various **buccal/mucosal patches**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Swelling Pressure in Superporous Hydrogels (SPH)

Superporous hydrogels used in inflatable systems swell via osmotic uptake of water. The **swelling pressure (π_sw)** at equilibrium arises from three contributions.

$$\pi_{sw} = \pi_{mix} + \pi_{elastic} + \pi_{ion}$$

**Step 1 — Mixing Contribution (π_mix):** Derived from the Flory-Huggins lattice theory.

$$\pi_{mix} = -\dfrac{RT}{V_1} \left[ \ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \, \phi_2^2 \right]$$

where $R$ = gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature, $V_1$ = molar volume of solvent (water = **18.015 cm³/mol**), $\phi_2$ = polymer volume fraction, $\chi$ = Flory-Huggins interaction parameter.

**Step 2 — Elastic Contribution (π_elastic):** This term opposes swelling, arising from the crosslinked network's elastic recoil.

$$\pi_{elastic} = -RT \, \nu \, \phi_2^{1/3}$$

where $\nu$ = crosslink density (mol/cm³ of dry polymer).

**Step 3 — Ionic Contribution (π_ion):** For ionic polymers (e.g., sodium alginate, carbopol salts), the Donnan equilibrium creates an additional osmotic pressure.

$$\pi_{ion} = RT \sum_i (C_i^{gel} - C_i^{bath})$$

where $C_i^{gel}$ and $C_i^{bath}$ are mobile ion concentrations inside and outside the gel.

**Step 4 — Equilibrium Condition:** At mechanical equilibrium, total swelling pressure equals external mechanical pressure (≈ atmospheric in the GI tract, so $\pi_{sw} \to 0$ at full swelling).

$$\pi_{mix} + \pi_{elastic} + \pi_{ion} = 0$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Mucoadhesion Bond Strength

The bioadhesive work of adhesion between a polymer and mucin layer (per unit area) is given by the **thermodynamic work of adhesion** $W_a$.

$$W_a = \gamma_{PM} + \gamma_{SM} - \gamma_{SP}$$

**Step 1 — Substitution using Young's Equation:** We express the polymer-mucus interfacial tension in terms of measurable contact angles.

$$\gamma_{SM} - \gamma_{SP} = \gamma_{MP} \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Combine to obtain a measurable expression for $W_a$:**

$$W_a = \gamma_{PM} \, (1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Illustration for Carbopol 934 on gastric mucosa:**

- Given: $\gamma_{PM} = 38.2 \text{ mN/m}$, contact angle $\theta = 32.5°$
- Compute: $\cos(32.5°) = 0.8434$

$$W_a = 38.2 \times (1 + 0.8434) = 38.2 \times 1.8434 = 70.41 \text{ mN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bond force per unit area:**

$$F = \dfrac{W_a}{d_{contact}}$$

where $d_{contact}$ is the interpenetration depth (typical: **0.3 µm = 3 × 10⁻⁷ m**).

$$F = \dfrac{70.41 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}}{3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}} = 2.35 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/m}^2 = 235 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The bioadhesive bond strength of **~235 kPa** is sufficient to retain a small device in the stomach against peristaltic shear forces (typically **0.1 – 0.5 kPa**), with a safety margin exceeding **400×**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Metric | Inflatable System | Gastro-Adhesive System |
|---|---|---|
| **GRT (hours)** | 6 – 12 | 2 – 6 |
| **Manufacturing complexity** | High (multi-part assembly) | Low to moderate (mix-blend-compress) |
| **Patient comfort** | Moderate (perceived bloating) | High |
| **Risk of obstruction** | Low (designed to deflate/shed) | Very low |
| **Drug loading capacity** | High (reservoir design) | Moderate (matrix-bound) |
| **Biocompatibility of polymers** | Excellent (PVA, PEG, PEO) | Excellent (chitosan, HPMC) |
| **Suitability for proteins/peptides** | Very high | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inflatable Gastroretentive System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oral Ingestion: Size 000 Capsule] --> B[Entry into Stomach]
    B --> C{Trigger Mechanism}
    C --> D[CO2 Generation: Citric Acid + NaHCO3]
    C --> E[Superporous Hydrogel Swelling]
    C --> F[Osmotic Water Uptake]
    D --> G[Inflation Chamber Expands: 3 to 5 cm]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Mechanical Anchoring: Size Exceeds Pylorus]
    H --> I[Drug Reservoir Activated]
    I --> J[Sustained Release: Diffusion / Osmotic Pumping]
    J --> K[Drug Absorption in Upper GI]
    H --> L[Safety: Biodegradable Plug Erodes]
    L --> M[Deflation and Natural Excretion]
```

### 4.2 Functional Workflow of Gastro-Adhesive System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Polymer Tablet / Patch] --> P2[Contact with Mucosal Lining]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 1: Wetting and Spreading]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 2: Chain Interpenetration into Mucin Layer]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 3: Hydrogen Bond Formation]
    P5 --> P6[Stable Adhesion Established]
    P6 --> P7[Drug Release via Diffusion / Erosion]
    P7 --> P8[Localized or Systemic Absorption]
    P6 --> P9[Mucus Turnover: 50 to 270 min]
    P9 --> P10[Detachment and Transit]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Inflatable System Components]
    A1[Drug Reservoir] --> A2[Inflation Chamber]
    A2 --> A3[Osmotic / Gas Generating Core]
    A3 --> A4[Semi-permeable Membrane]
    A4 --> A5[Drug Release Orifice]
    end
    subgraph B[Gastro-Adhesive System Components]
    B1[Bioadhesive Polymer Matrix] --> B2[Drug Loading Zone]
    B2 --> B3[Mucoadhesive Surface Layer]
    B3 --> B4[Backing Membrane Optional]
    B4 --> B5[Controlled Release Barrier]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define gastroretentive drug delivery systems. Mention any two mechanisms of gastric retention with examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Gastroretentive drug delivery systems (GRDDS) are pharmaceutical formulations designed to be retained in the stomach for a prolonged period, thereby improving the bioavailability of drugs with a narrow absorption window, stability issues in intestinal pH, or local gastric action. **Two mechanisms of gastric retention are:**

1. **Inflatable / Expandable systems** — devices that swell or inflate to a size larger than the pyloric sphincter (e.g., superporous hydrogel balloons).
2. **Mucoadhesive / Gastro-adhesive systems** — devices using bioadhesive polymers (e.g., chitosan, carbopol) that adhere to the gastric mucosal lining.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of superporous hydrogels (SPH) in inflatable drug delivery?**

**Model Answer:**
Superporous hydrogels (SPH) are crosslinked hydrophilic polymer networks with interconnected pores of size **>100 µm**. They absorb gastric fluid rapidly and swell to many times their original volume within minutes, providing the mechanical expansion required for gastroretention. They combine high swelling capacity, mechanical strength, and biocompatibility, making them ideal as inflatable matrices for sustained drug release in the stomach.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **design, working mechanism, and components of inflatable gastroretentive drug delivery systems**. Discuss the role of superporous hydrogels and gas-generating systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

1. **Definition & Rationale:** Inflatable GRDDS are designed to expand in the stomach, achieving sizes of **3 – 5 cm**, exceeding the pyloric diameter (~**2 cm**), thereby preventing gastric emptying.
2. **Components:**
   - **Drug reservoir** containing the active pharmaceutical ingredient.
   - **Inflation chamber** containing osmotically active agents (e.g., sodium bicarbonate, citric acid) or SPH.
   - **Semi-permeable membrane** controlling water influx.
   - **Drug release orifice** for delivery.
3. **Working Mechanism:** Following oral ingestion, gastric fluid enters the device. Osmotic core or SPH swells → device inflates → mechanical anchoring → drug released by diffusion, erosion, or osmotic pumping over **6 – 12 hours**.
4. **Role of SPH:** SPH swell rapidly (in **seconds to minutes**) due to capillary-driven water uptake. They offer high swelling ratios (**>100×**), sufficient mechanical strength, and biocompatibility.
5. **Gas-Generating Systems:** Reaction of citric acid with sodium bicarbonate yields **CO₂ gas**, which inflates a balloon compartment.
6. **Examples:** Intragastric balloons (**Orbera**, **ReShape Duo**), expandable gastric retention systems described by Cargill et al. and in US Patent literature.
7. **Advantages & Limitations:** Prolonged GRT, enhanced absorption; but risk of mucosal irritation, gastric obstruction in rare cases.

---

**(b)** Describe the **mechanism of mucoadhesion in detail**. Discuss the various **theories of bioadhesion** and the key polymer properties required for effective gastroadhesion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Definition:** Mucoadhesion is the adhesion of synthetic or biological macromolecules to the mucus-covered epithelial surface.
2. **Mechanism (Three Stages):**
   - **Wetting and contact** — polymer spreads on mucosa (wetting theory).
   - **Interpenetration** — polymer chains diffuse into mucin glycoprotein matrix.
   - **Consolidation** — secondary bonds (H-bonds, van der Waals, electrostatic) form.
3. **Theories of Bioadhesion:**
   - **Wetting theory** — based on contact angle and spreading.
   - **Adsorption theory** — surface energy and interatomic forces.
   - **Diffusion theory** — interpenetration of chains.
   - **Electronic theory** — electron transfer across the interface.
   - **Mechanical theory** — interlocking with surface irregularities.
4. **Key Polymer Properties:**
   - High molecular weight (**>100 kDa** optimal).
   - Optimal **flexibility** (low Tg for chain mobility).
   - **Hydrogen bonding groups** (–COOH, –OH, –NH₂).
   - **Anionic or cationic charge** for electrostatic interaction.
   - **Hydration capacity** without over-swelling.
5. **Examples:** Carbopol 934, chitosan, sodium alginate, HPMC, polyacrylic acid.
6. **Evaluation methods:** Tensile strength testing, shear stress measurement, ex vivo mucosal retention studies.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**Q. (a)** Compare and contrast **inflatable and gastro-adhesive drug delivery systems** with respect to their **design, mechanism, polymers used, advantages, and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular + Narrative):**

| Feature | Inflatable System | Gastro-Adhesive System |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Size-based mechanical retention | Mucin-binding chemical retention |
| **Size post-activation** | 3 – 5 cm | Unchanged |
| **Polymers** | PVA, PEG, PEO, SPH composites | Carbopol, chitosan, HPMC |
| **Activation** | Osmotic / pH / enzymatic trigger | Wetting upon contact with mucosa |
| **GRT** | 6 – 12 h | 2 – 6 h |
| **Advantages** | High GRT, suitable for narrow absorption drugs | High patient comfort, simpler manufacturing |
| **Limitations** | Bloating sensation, complex design | Limited by mucus turnover (50 – 270 min) |

**Narrative:** Both systems aim to extend GRT. Inflatable systems are mechanically anchored and more reliable for long-term retention. Gastro-adhesive systems are simpler to manufacture and better tolerated but limited by physiological mucus renewal. Choice depends on drug properties, desired GRT, and patient acceptability.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **pharmaceutical applications and commercial examples** of inflatable and gastro-adhesive systems. Highlight their role in the delivery of **narrow absorption window drugs, peptide therapeutics, and locally acting gastric agents**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Narrow Absorption Window Drugs:** Drugs like **levodopa**, **furosemide**, **atenolol**, and **riboflavin** are absorbed only in the upper GI tract. Inflatable GRDDS provide prolonged gastric residence, enhancing total bioavailability by **40 – 60%**.
2. **Peptide and Protein Delivery:** Peptides degrade in intestinal pH and via proteolytic enzymes. Gastric retention in a **pH-controlled microenvironment** allows sustained local delivery, as studied with **insulin-loaded SPH composites**.
3. **Locally Acting Gastric Agents:** Drugs for **H. pylori eradication** (e.g., clarithromycin, amoxicillin) and **gastritis treatment** benefit from gastro-adhesive systems that maintain high local concentration.
4. **Commercial Inflatable Examples:** **Orbera Intragastric Balloon**, **ReShape Duo**, **Spatz3 Adjustable Balloon System** (all approved for obesity management).
5. **Commercial Adhesive Examples:** **Calan® SR (verapamil)**, **Carafate® (sucralfate)** for gastric ulcer, **nicotine chewing gums** (buccal adhesion), various **bioadhesive buccal patches**.
6. **Emerging Applications:** Gastric-retentive **levodopa-carbidopa systems** for Parkinson's disease management, **gastric-retentive metformin** for type-2 diabetes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. Students often **confuse mucoadhesion with bioadhesion** — remember: *bioadhesion* is the broader term (any biological surface), *mucoadhesion* is specific to mucus.
> 2. In numerical problems involving **swelling ratio or work of adhesion**, students forget **unit conversions** (mN/m to N/m).
> 3. Inflatable systems are **not** purely gastroretentive — they are a *category* under GRDDS. Do not generalize.
> 4. Do not assume all mucoadhesive polymers are **cationic**; anionic polymers (e.g., carbopol, polyacrylic acid) are equally important.
> 5. The **Peppas exponent `n`** must be correctly interpreted: $n = 0.45$ (Fickian), $0.45 < n < 0.89$ (anomalous), $n = 0.89$ (Case-II), $n > 0.89$ (Super Case-II).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inflatable systems** achieve gastroretention by **physical expansion to >3 cm**, blocking pyloric transit.
- **Activation triggers:** osmotic (NaHCO₃/citric acid), swelling (SPH), or pH-responsive polymers.
- **Gastro-adhesive systems** rely on **non-covalent bonding** (H-bonds, van der Waals, electrostatic) with mucin.
- **Three-stage mucoadhesion:** Wetting → Interpenetration → Bond consolidation.
- **Critical polymer properties:** molecular weight > 100 kDa, low Tg, high hydration, H-bonding groups.
- **Key polymers:** PVA, PEG, Carbopol 934, chitosan, HPMC, sodium alginate.
- **Limiting factor for GRDDS in vivo:** mucus turnover (50 – 270 min) and gastric emptying of indigestible solids.
- **Pyloric diameter:** ~1.5 – 2.0 cm (must be exceeded by inflated device).
- **Formulas to memorize:** Spreading coefficient, Young's equation, Peppas-Korsmeyer ($M_t/M_\infty = k t^n$), Higuchi matrix release.
- **Commercial products:** Orbera balloon, Calan® SR, sucralfate tablets.
- **Applications:** narrow absorption window drugs, peptide delivery, H. pylori therapy, obesity management.
- **Swelling pressure equation:** $\pi_{sw} = \pi_{mix} + \pi_{elastic} + \pi_{ion}$ (Flory-Huggins derivation).
- **Work of adhesion:** $W_a = \gamma_{PM} (1 + \cos\theta)$ — used to compute bioadhesive strength.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naso-Pulmonary Drug Delivery System: Introduction to Nasal and Pulmonary Routes of Drug Delivery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB862 - DRUG DELIVERY PRINCIPLE & APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Naso-Pulmonary Drug Delivery System: Introduction to Nasal and Pulmonary Routes of Drug Delivery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nasal Drug Delivery System (NDD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **Nasal Drug Delivery System (NDD)** is a transmucosal route of drug administration in which a pharmaceutical formulation is instilled into the **nasal cavity** for either **local** (e.g., decongestants, corticosteroids) or **systemic** (e.g., peptides, vaccines, analgesics) therapeutic action via absorption through the **nasal mucosa** (respiratory epithelium).

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture – "The Express Highway to the Brain":**
> Imagine your nasal cavity as a special VIP gate at an airport that bypasses the main security (the liver's first-pass metabolism). When you spray a migraine drug (sumatriptan) or a decongestant (oxymetazoline), the active ingredient slips through the thin, well-vascularized lining of the nose directly into the bloodstream. The **olfactory region** at the roof of the nose even offers a "sneak route" directly into the central nervous system (CNS) — making the nose a promising gateway for brain-targeted drugs.

### Key Metrics & Terminology

- **Nasal Cavity Surface Area:** ~150 cm² (each nostril)
- **Nasal Mucociliary Clearance Half-life:** ~15–20 minutes
- **Respiratory Epithelium Thickness:** ~4–5 μm (ciliated pseudostratified columnar)
- **Olfactory Region Area:** ~2–10 cm² (rooftop of nasal cavity)
- **Nasal pH:** 5.5 – 6.5 (adults), 5.0 – 6.7 (infants)
- **Nasal Blood Flow:** ~40 mL/min per 100 g of tissue

---

## 1.2 Pulmonary Drug Delivery System (PDD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **Pulmonary Drug Delivery System (PDD)** refers to the administration of pharmaceutical agents via **inhalation** through the oral/nasal route, intended to deliver the drug to the **respiratory tract (trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, alveoli)** for local action or to the **systemic circulation** through the **alveolar epithelium**.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture – "The Balloon Room of the Body":**
> Picture the lungs as a giant balloon with millions of tiny air sacs (alveoli). If you sprinkle fine powder or mist into this "balloon room," the tiny particles can either settle on the walls (local action like asthma drugs) or dissolve into the blood vessels surrounding the alveoli (systemic action like inhaled insulin). The **alveolar surface (~70 m²)** is bigger than a tennis court, making it the **largest absorptive surface in the body** — a perfect target for drug delivery engineers.

### Key Metrics & Terminology

- **Alveolar Surface Area:** ~70 – 100 m² (in adult lungs)
- **Total Number of Alveoli:** ~300 – 500 million
- **Alveolar Epithelium Thickness:** 0.1 – 0.5 μm
- **Optimal Aerodynamic Diameter (da):** 1 – 5 μm for alveolar deposition
- **Respiratory Volume (Tidal):** ~500 mL
- **Lung Blood Flow:** ~5 L/min (entire cardiac output passes through lungs)

---

## 1.3 Why These Routes? — Strategic Importance in Pharmaceutics

> [!CAUTION]
> **Why KTU 2024 Emphasizes Naso-Pulmonary Routes:**
> Both routes **avoid hepatic first-pass metabolism**, offer **rapid onset of action**, and enable **non-invasive systemic delivery of macromolecules** (peptides, proteins, vaccines) that would otherwise be destroyed in the GI tract. They are cornerstone alternatives to oral and parenteral delivery in modern pharmaceutical engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy & Physiology of the Nasal Cavity

The nasal cavity is partitioned into **three functional regions** for drug delivery purposes:

| Region | Location | Epithelium Type | Drug Delivery Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vestibular Region** | Anterior 1.5 cm | Stratified squamous, keratinized | Filter for large particles; poor absorption |
| **Respiratory Region** | Largest, lateral walls | Pseudostratified ciliated columnar with goblet cells | **Primary site for systemic absorption**; rich vasculature |
| **Olfactory Region** | Roof of nasal cavity, ~2–10 cm² | Olfactory neuroepithelium | **Direct nose-to-brain (N2B) delivery** for CNS drugs |

### Step-by-Step Mechanism of Nasal Drug Absorption

1. **Drug Deposition:** Formulation (spray/drops) deposits on respiratory mucosa.
2. **Dissolution:** Drug dissolves in the **mucus layer** (~5 μm thick, 95% water).
3. **Penetration:** Drug traverses the mucus and reaches the **apical membrane** of epithelial cells.
4. **Transcellular / Paracellular Transport:**
   - **Transcellular:** Passive diffusion (lipophilic drugs), carrier-mediated (e.g., glucose, amino acids).
   - **Paracellular:** Aqueous pores between cells (hydrophilic, small molecules <1000 Da).
5. **Basolateral Exits:** Drug enters the **fenestrated capillaries** or **lymphatics** of the lamina propria.
6. **Systemic Distribution:** Drainage into the **sphenopalatine vein → cavernous sinus** (CNS access) or systemic venous return.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Think **"D-P-P-T-S"** = **Dissolve → Penetrate → Permeate → Transport → Systemic**.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy & Physiology of the Pulmonary System

The respiratory tract is divided into **conducting zones** and **respiratory zones**:

| Zone | Structures | Function | Drug Delivery Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conducting Zone** | Trachea, bronchi, bronchioles (terminal) | Air conduction, humidification | Particle impaction (large particles >5 μm) |
| **Transitional Zone** | Respiratory bronchioles | Mixed conduction/gas exchange | Partial deposition |
| **Respiratory Zone** | Alveolar ducts, alveolar sacs | Gas exchange | **Ideal deposition site (1–5 μm)** |

### Three Primary Mechanisms of Particle Deposition in Lungs

1. **Inertial Impaction** — dominant in upper airways; large/dense particles (da > 5 μm) hit bifurcations.
2. **Sedimentation (Gravitational)** — dominant in bronchioles; particles 0.5–3 μm settle by gravity.
3. **Brownian Diffusion** — dominant in alveoli; ultrafine particles (<0.5 μm) diffuse and deposit on alveolar walls.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the mathematical backbone of Naso-Pulmonary delivery and frequently appear in KTU 2024 numerical/derivation questions.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Aerodynamic Diameter (da)** | $d_a = d \sqrt{\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_0 \chi}}$ | $d$: geometric diameter, $\rho_p$: particle density, $\rho_0 = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\chi$: dynamic shape factor (sphere = 1) |
| 2 | **Stokes' Number (Stk)** | $Stk = \frac{\rho_p d_a^2 v}{18 \eta D}$ | Predicts inertial impaction; $v$: velocity, $\eta$: air viscosity, $D$: airway diameter |
| 3 | **Impaction Parameter** | $P_i = d_a^2 \cdot Q$ | $Q$: airflow rate (L/min); used in cascade impactor analysis |
| 4 | **Cunningham Slip Correction (Cc)** | $C_c = 1 + \frac{2\lambda}{d}\left(1.257 + 0.4 e^{-0.55 d / \lambda}\right)$ | $\lambda$: mean free path of air (~0.066 μm at STP); corrects for slip flow in fine particles |
| 5 | **Fick's First Law (Flux)** | $J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$ | Steady-state drug flux across mucosa; $D$: diffusion coefficient, $dC/dx$: concentration gradient |
| 6 | **Higuchi Equation (Membrane Release)** | $Q_t = \sqrt{D \left(2C_0 - C_s\right) C_s \, t}$ | Drug release from matrix-based nasal/pulmonary devices |
| 7 | **First-Order Absorption (Nasal)** | $C_p = \frac{F \cdot D_k}{V_d (k_a - k_{el})} \left(e^{-k_{el} t} - e^{-k_a t}\right)$ | $F$: bioavailability fraction, $D_k$: dose, $k_a$: absorption rate, $k_{el}$: elimination rate |
| 8 | **Fine Particle Fraction (FPF)** | $FPF = \frac{\text{Mass of particles with } d_a < 5 \mu m}{\text{Total emitted dose}} \times 100\%$ | Quality attribute of inhalation aerosols |
| 9 | **MMAD (Mass Median Aerodynamic Diameter)** | $d_{a, 50}$ | Diameter below which 50% of particle mass resides |
| 10 | **GSD (Geometric Std. Deviation)** | $\sigma_g = \sqrt{\frac{d_{84\%}}{d_{16\%}}}$ | Measures polydispersity; ideal inhaler: $\sigma_g < 1.22$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Equation 1 — Aerodynamic Diameter — is the MOST frequently tested derivation in KTU 2024 module 3. Master the units, definitions, and physical meaning of $\chi$ and $\rho_0$!**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Pharmaceutical Engineering Utility

| Application Field | Utility of Naso-Pulmonary Routes |
|---|---|
| **Asthma / COPD Therapy** | Targeted delivery of $\beta_2$-agonists (salbutamol) and corticosteroids (budesonide) directly to lungs, minimizing systemic side-effects. |
| **Migraine Treatment** | Nasal sumatriptan reaches therapeutic levels within 10–15 min — 3× faster than oral tablets. |
| **Vaccination** | Intranasal influenza vaccines (FluMist) and inhaled dry-powder measles vaccines leverage mucosal immunity. |
| **Systemic Peptide Delivery** | Inhaled insulin (Exubera, Afrezza) — though commercial challenges, exemplifies macromolecule delivery via lungs. |
| **CNS Drug Targeting** | Direct nose-to-brain delivery of neuropeptides, growth factors, and antineoplastics bypassing BBB. |
| **Emergency Drug Delivery** | Nasal naloxone (Narcan) for opioid overdose — needle-free, fast-acting rescue therapy. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Aerodynamic Diameter (da)

The aerodynamic diameter is the **single most important parameter** governing particle behavior in inhaled aerosols.

### Starting Point — Particle Settling

For a sphere falling under gravity in air, terminal settling velocity by **Stokes' Law** is:

$$
v_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_a) g d^2}{18 \eta}
$$

For particles smaller than the mean free path of air, **Stokes' Law is invalid** (slip-flow regime). We must apply the **Cunningham slip correction factor (Cc)**:

$$
v_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_a) g d^2 C_c}{18 \eta}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** A particle of geometric diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ in air has settling velocity $v_t$.

**Step 2:** The **aerodynamic diameter $d_a$** is defined as the diameter of a **unit-density sphere ($\rho_0 = 1$ g/cm³)** that settles at the same terminal velocity.

Equating terminal velocities:

$$
\frac{(\rho_p - \rho_a) g d^2 C_c}{18 \eta} = \frac{(\rho_0 - \rho_a) g d_a^2 C_{c,a}}{18 \eta}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $\rho_a \ll \rho_p$ and $\rho_a \ll \rho_0$, the air density term is negligible:

$$
\rho_p \, d^2 C_c = \rho_0 \, d_a^2 C_{c,a}
$$

**Step 4:** For spherical particles ($C_c = C_{c,a}$, both geometric and aerodynamic correction factors identical):

$$
\rho_p \, d^2 = \rho_0 \, d_a^2
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $d_a$:

$$
d_a^2 = \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_0} d^2
$$

$$
d_a = d \sqrt{\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_0}}
$$

**Step 6:** Introduce the **dynamic shape factor $\chi$** to generalize for non-spherical particles:

$$
d_a = d \sqrt{\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_0 \chi}}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Question:** A porous particle has geometric diameter $d = 10 \mu m$, particle density $\rho_p = 0.4$ g/cm³, and shape factor $\chi = 1.5$. Calculate its aerodynamic diameter. (Take $\rho_0 = 1$ g/cm³)

**Solution:**

Substitute into the formula:

$$
d_a = 10 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.4}{1 \times 1.5}}
$$

$$
d_a = 10 \times \sqrt{0.2667}
$$

$$
d_a = 10 \times 0.5164 = 5.164 \, \mu m
$$

**Interpretation:** A large, low-density, irregularly shaped particle behaves aerodynamically like a ~5.16 μm sphere — **perfectly within the alveolar deposition window (1–5 μm)**, even though its geometric size is 10 μm. This is the engineering principle behind **large porous particles (LPPs)** used in next-generation inhaled insulin formulations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fick's First Law of Diffusion Applied to Nasal Mucosa

### Setup

Consider drug diffusing across the nasal mucosa of thickness $h$. Let:
- $C_0$ = drug concentration at the mucosal surface (apical side)
- $C_h$ = drug concentration at the serosal side (basal lamina)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient in the mucosa
- $J$ = steady-state flux (drug mass per unit area per unit time)

### Derivation

**Step 1:** At steady state, the flux $J$ is constant through the membrane.

**Step 2:** Fick's First Law:

$$
J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

**Step 3:** At steady state, integrate across the membrane thickness $h$:

$$
\int_0^h J \, dx = -\int_{C_0}^{C_h} D \, dC
$$

**Step 4:** Since $J$ and $D$ are constants:

$$
J \cdot h = D (C_0 - C_h)
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for flux:

$$
J = \frac{D \cdot (C_0 - C_h)}{h}
$$

**Step 6:** Define **Permeability Coefficient** $P = D / h$:

$$
J = P \cdot (C_0 - C_h)
$$

### Interpretation

- **High $D$ (small lipophilic molecules):** Rapid flux.
- **Thin mucosa (e.g., olfactory region ~50–200 μm):** Higher flux.
- **High concentration gradient $(C_0 - C_h)$:** Greater driving force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** The olfactory mucosa being 5–10× thinner than the respiratory mucosa is one reason it is preferred for **nose-to-brain delivery** of neurotherapeutics.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary of Naso vs Pulmonary

| Parameter | Nasal Route | Pulmonary Route |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Target Tissue** | Respiratory / Olfactory mucosa | Bronchiolar / Alveolar epithelium |
| **Surface Area** | ~150 cm² (per nostril) | ~70–100 m² (alveoli) |
| **Epithelium Thickness** | 4–5 μm (respiratory); 50–200 μm (olfactory) | 0.1–0.5 μm (alveolar) |
| **Blood Flow** | 40 mL/min/100 g | 5 L/min (entire cardiac output) |
| **First-Pass Metabolism** | Avoided (minor at olfactory) | Avoided completely |
| **Onset of Action** | 5–15 min | 1–5 min (alveolar absorption) |
| **Drug MW Limit** | < 1000 Da preferred | < 40 kDa possible |
| **Bioavailability (Systemic)** | 10–20% (variable) | 15–50% (highly variable) |
| **Clearance Mechanism** | Mucociliary (~15–20 min) | Macrophage, cough, mucociliary |
| **Optimal Particle Size** | Solution/droplet 50–200 μm | 1–5 μm aerodynamic diameter |
| **Key Devices** | Nasal sprays, drops, gels, powders | MDIs, DPIs, Nebulizers, Soft mist inhalers |
| **Major Limitation** | Small dosing volume (~25–200 μL/nostril) | Patient coordination, device dependency |
| **Representative Drugs** | Sumatriptan, desmopressin, naloxone, influenza vaccine | Salbutamol, budesonide, tobramycin, insulin, treprostinil |
| **First-Pass Bypass Efficiency** | ~80–100% | ~100% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Comparative Architecture of Nasal vs Pulmonary Drug Delivery

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pharmaceutical Formulation] --> B{Route of Administration}
    
    B --> C[NASAL DRUG DELIVERY]
    B --> D[PULMONARY DRUG DELIVERY]
    
    C --> C1[Device Selection]
    C1 --> C1a[Nasal Spray Pump]
    C1 --> C1b[Nebulizer]
    C1 --> C1c[Nasal Drops]
    C1 --> C1d[Powder Inhaler]
    C1 --> C1e[Inserts/Gels]
    
    C1a --> C2[Nasal Cavity Deposition]
    C1b --> C2
    C1c --> C2
    C1d --> C2
    C1e --> C2
    
    C2 --> C3[Tissue Targeting]
    C3 --> C3a[Local Action - Sinusitis]
    C3 --> C3b[Systemic - Sumatriptan]
    C3 --> C3c[CNS - Olfactory Pathway]
    C3 --> C3d[Vaccination - Mucosal IgA]
    
    D --> D1[Device Selection]
    D1 --> D1a[Pressurized MDI]
    D1 --> D1b[Dry Powder Inhaler DPI]
    D1 --> D1c[Jet Nebulizer]
    D1 --> D1d[Ultrasonic Nebulizer]
    D1 --> D1e[Soft Mist Inhaler]
    
    D1a --> D2[Respiratory Tract Deposition]
    D1b --> D2
    D1c --> D2
    D1d --> D2
    D1e --> D2
    
    D2 --> D3[Deposition Mechanism]
    D3 --> D3a[Inertial Impaction greater than 5um]
    D3 --> D3b[Sedimentation 0.5 to 3um]
    D3 --> D3c[Brownian Diffusion less than 0.5um]
    
    D3a --> D4[Tissue Targeting]
    D3b --> D4
    D3c --> D4
    
    D4 --> D4a[Local - Asthma COPD]
    D4 --> D4b[Systemic - Inhaled Insulin]
    D4 --> D4c[Anti-infective - Tobramycin]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Functional Block Architecture of Pulmonary Deposition Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputZone [INPUT ZONE]
        INA[Drug Formulation]
        INB[Propellant or Carrier]
        INA --> INB
    end
    
    subgraph deviceZone [DEVICE ZONE - INHALER]
        DA[Aerosol Generation]
        DB[Particle Sizing]
        DC[Actuation Mechanism]
        DA --> DB --> DC
    end
    
    subgraph airwayZone [AIRWAY ZONE]
        EA[Oropharyngeal Deposition]
        EB[Tracheobronchial Deposition]
        EC[Alveolar Deposition]
        EA --> EB --> EC
    end
    
    subgraph absorptionZone [ABSORPTION ZONE]
        FA[Drug Dissolution in ELF]
        FB[Epithelial Permeation]
        FC[Capillary Uptake]
        FA --> FB --> FC
    end
    
    subgraph clearanceZone [CLEARANCE MECHANISMS]
        GA[Mucociliary Escalator]
        GB[Alveolar Macrophages]
        GC[Systemic Metabolism]
        GA --> GB --> GC
    end
    
    subgraph targetZone [THERAPEUTIC OUTCOME]
        HA[Local Effect - Bronchodilation]
        HB[Systemic Effect - Plasma Levels]
        HC[CNS Access - Limited]
        HA --> HB --> HC
    end
    
    INB --> DA
    DC --> EA
    EC --> FA
    FC --> GA
    GC --> HA
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Three-Tier Nasal Absorption Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Drug Deposited on Nasal Mucosa]
    START --> M1[Mucus Layer Dissolution]
    M1 --> M2[Apical Membrane Contact]
    M2 --> PATH1[Transcellular Route - Lipophilic]
    M2 --> PATH2[Paracellular Route - Hydrophilic Small MW]
    M2 --> PATH3[Carrier Mediated Transport]
    
    PATH1 --> BL[Basolateral Membrane]
    PATH2 --> BL
    PATH3 --> BL
    
    BL --> OUT1[Systemic Venous Return]
    BL --> OUT2[Lymphatic Drainage]
    BL --> OUT3[CNS via Olfactory Nerve]
    
    OUT1 --> EFF1[Systemic Therapeutic Effect]
    OUT2 --> EFF2[Immune Response - MALT]
    OUT3 --> EFF3[Brain Targeted Delivery]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Particle Size vs Deposition Site Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph particleSize [PARTICLE SIZE BINS]
        P1[Greater than 10 um - Oropharyngeal]
        P2[5 to 10 um - Tracheobronchial]
        P3[1 to 5 um - Alveolar OPTIMAL]
        P4[0.1 to 1 um - Exhaled Mostly]
        P5[Less than 0.1 um - Brownian Exhalation]
    end
    
    subgraph depSite [DEPOSITION SITE]
        S1[Mouth and Throat - Swallowed]
        S2[Bronchi and Bronchioles]
        S3[Alveolar Region]
        S4[Exhaled]
        S5[Exhaled]
    end
    
    subgraph mechanism [DOMINANT MECHANISM]
        M1[Inertial Impaction]
        M2[Inertial plus Sedimentation]
        M3[Sedimentation and Diffusion]
        M4[Minimal Deposition]
        M5[Brownian Diffusion - Low]
    end
    
    P1 --> S1 --> M1
    P2 --> S2 --> M2
    P3 --> S3 --> M3
    P4 --> S4 --> M4
    P5 --> S5 --> M5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The 1–5 μm window is the **"Goldilocks Zone"** for pulmonary delivery. Particles smaller than 0.5 μm tend to be exhaled; particles larger than 5 μm are trapped in upper airways. This is the engineering basis of inhaler formulation design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q: Define the term "Aerodynamic Diameter" and explain its significance in pulmonary drug delivery.**

**Model Answer:**

Aerodynamic diameter ($d_a$) is defined as the diameter of a **unit-density sphere ($\rho_0$ = 1 g/cm³)** that has the same terminal settling velocity as the particle in question, regardless of its actual geometric size, shape, or density.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
d_a = d \sqrt{\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_0 \, \chi}}
$$

where $d$ = geometric diameter, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $\rho_0$ = 1 g/cm³, $\chi$ = dynamic shape factor.

**Significance in Pulmonary Drug Delivery:**

- It is the **master parameter** that determines where an inhaled particle deposits in the respiratory tract.
- Particles with $d_a$ between **1–5 μm** reach the **alveolar region** — the target site for systemic and deep lung delivery.
- Particles with $d_a > 5$ μm impact in the oropharynx and upper airways.
- Particles with $d_a < 0.5$ μm are mostly exhaled without deposition.
- It unifies geometric size, density, and shape into a single deposition-relevant metric, allowing pharmaceutical engineers to design **large porous particles (LPPs)** that are geometrically large but aerodynamically small for improved aerosolization.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q: List the advantages and limitations of the nasal route of drug delivery.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**

1. **Avoids hepatic first-pass metabolism** — drug reaches systemic circulation directly via nasal mucosa.
2. **Rapid absorption** — onset within 5–15 minutes for many drugs (e.g., sumatriptan).
3. **Non-invasive and painless** — high patient compliance compared to injections.
4. **Direct nose-to-brain delivery** — the olfactory region offers a unique pathway bypassing the blood–brain barrier (BBB).
5. **Suitable for peptides and proteins** — insulin, calcitonin, desmopressin.
6. **Self-administration** — minimal training required.
7. **Reduced systemic side-effects** for locally acting drugs (decongestants, corticosteroids).

**Limitations:**

1. **Small dosing volume** — only 25–200 μL per nostril.
2. **Mucociliary clearance** — drug cleared within 15–20 minutes.
3. **Limited to potent drugs** — due to volume constraint.
4. **Nasal irritation** — possible with chronic use.
5. **Variable bioavailability** — 10–20% for most systemic drugs.
6. **MW restriction** — molecules >1000 Da poorly absorbed.
7. **Pathological conditions** (rhinitis, cold) reduce absorption efficiency.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **anatomy and physiology of the nasal cavity** with respect to drug delivery, including a description of the three functional regions and their epithelium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of drug absorption** through the nasal mucosa, highlighting the various transport pathways and the factors affecting nasal drug absorption. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The human nasal cavity is a complex, bilaterally symmetrical space extending from the **nares (nostrils)** anteriorly to the **choanae** posteriorly, where it connects to the nasopharynx. It is anatomically divided into three distinct regions, each with unique epithelial characteristics and drug delivery implications.

**1. Vestibular Region (Anterior 1.5 cm):**

- **Epithelium:** Stratified squamous, keratinized.
- **Function:** Acts as a coarse filter, trapping large particles (>10 μm) via nasal hair (vibrissae).
- **Drug Delivery Significance:** Minimal drug absorption occurs here due to the keratinized, poorly permeable epithelium. Particles deposited here are typically wiped off or blown out.

**2. Respiratory Region (Largest region):**

- **Location:** Lateral walls, including the turbinates (superior, middle, inferior).
- **Surface Area:** ~130 cm² per nostril.
- **Epithelium:** Pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with **goblet cells** producing mucus.
- **Features:** Highly vascularized lamina propria; rich supply of parasympathetic and sympathetic nerves.
- **Drug Delivery Significance:** This is the **primary site for systemic drug absorption**. The dense vascularization ensures rapid entry into systemic circulation via the sphenopalatine and ophthalmic veins.

**3. Olfactory Region (Roof of nasal cavity):**

- **Location:** Cribriform plate area; ~2–10 cm² in humans.
- **Epithelium:** Olfactory neuroepithelium containing olfactory receptor neurons, supporting cells, and basal cells.
- **Drug Delivery Significance:** Provides the **only direct anatomical pathway from the external environment to the brain**, bypassing the blood–brain barrier. Molecules can reach the CNS via:
  - Intracellular axonal transport (slow, hours to days)
  - Extracellular perineural/perivascular channels (faster)

**Supporting Structures:**

- **Turbinates (conchae):** Create turbulent airflow, increasing residence time of inhaled drug particles.
- **Mucus Layer:** 5 μm thick bilayer (inner watery sol + outer viscous gel), 95% water; traps particles and contains lysozyme, IgA, and defensive proteins.
- **Nasal pH:** 5.5–6.5 in adults, critical for drug stability and ionization.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **mechanism of nasal drug absorption** involves a sequence of biophysical and biochemical events:

**Step 1: Drug Deposition and Dissolution**
- The formulation is deposited on the mucus layer, where the active ingredient dissolves in the aqueous mucus film.

**Step 2: Drug Transport Across the Epithelium**

Three main pathways exist:

**(i) Transcellular (Intracellular) Route:**

- **Mechanism:** Passive diffusion through the lipid bilayer of epithelial cells.
- **Favored by:** Lipophilic, small molecules (<400 Da).
- **Governed by:** Fick's First Law — $J = D \cdot (C_0 - C_h)/h$.
- **Example:** Progesterone, nicotine.

**(ii) Paracellular Route:**

- **Mechanism:** Diffusion through aqueous pores between adjacent cells (tight junctions).
- **Favored by:** Hydrophilic, small molecules (<1000 Da).
- **Limitation:** Pore size is small (~0.4–0.8 nm), restricting transport of larger molecules.
- **Example:** Mannitol, water-soluble vitamins.

**(iii) Carrier-Mediated Transport:**

- **Mechanism:** Active or facilitated transport via specific membrane transporters.
- **Examples:** Amino acid transporters, glucose transporters, peptide transporters (PepT1).

**Step 3: Entry into Systemic Circulation**

- Drug crosses the basolateral membrane and enters the **fenestrated capillaries** of the lamina propria.
- Drainage into the **sphenopalatine vein → internal jugular vein → systemic circulation**.

**Factors Affecting Nasal Drug Absorption:**

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Molecular Weight** | MW < 300 Da: ~100% absorption; MW > 1000 Da: < 10% absorption |
| **Lipophilicity** | Higher lipophilicity → higher permeability |
| **pH and pKa** | Unionized form predominates; absorption optimal at pH 4.5–6.5 |
| **Mucociliary Clearance** | Clears drug in 15–20 min; limits residence time |
| **Nasal Blood Flow** | Higher flow → faster systemic absorption |
| **Pathological State** | Allergic rhinitis, viral infections reduce absorption |
| **Drug Formulation** | pH, viscosity, osmolarity, mucoadhesive polymers all modulate absorption |
| **Enzymatic Degradation** | Nasal peptidases, proteases can degrade peptide drugs |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** Describe the **anatomy of the respiratory tract** and the **mechanisms of particle deposition** in the lungs. Discuss the role of aerodynamic diameter in determining deposition site. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify and describe the various **inhalation devices** used in pulmonary drug delivery, including MDIs, DPIs, and nebulizers, with their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The human respiratory tract is a branching tubular system extending from the mouth/nose to the alveoli. It is functionally divided into two major zones:

**A. Conducting Zone:**

Includes the **nasal cavity, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, and terminal bronchioles (~16 generations of branching).** The function is air conduction, humidification, and warming. No gas exchange occurs here.

**B. Respiratory Zone:**

Includes the **respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts, and alveolar sacs (generations 17–23).** This is the site of gas exchange and the **target zone for most inhaled drug delivery**.

**Alveolar Architecture:**

- ~300–500 million alveoli per adult lung.
- Total alveolar surface area: 70–100 m².
- Epithelium thickness: 0.1–0.5 μm (Type I pneumocytes).
- Surrounded by dense capillary network — entire cardiac output (~5 L/min) flows past.

**Mechanisms of Particle Deposition:**

**1. Inertial Impaction:**

- Dominant in the **upper airways and large bronchi** (generations 0–5).
- Caused by the inability of particles to follow high-velocity, turbulent airflow streamlines at airway bifurcations.
- Predicted by the **Stokes number**:

$$
Stk = \frac{\rho_p d_a^2 v}{18 \eta D}
$$

- When $Stk > 1$, impaction dominates; particles of $d_a > 5 \mu m$ typically impact here.

**2. Gravitational Sedimentation:**

- Dominant in **bronchioles and smaller airways** where airflow velocity is low.
- Predicted by the settling velocity: $v_t = \rho_p d_a^2 g / (18 \eta)$.
- Dominant for particles of $d_a$ 0.5–3 μm.

**3. Brownian Diffusion:**

- Dominant in the **alveolar region** and for very small particles ($d_a < 0.5 \mu m$).
- Caused by random thermal motion of particles colliding with gas molecules.
- Quantified by the **Brownian diffusion coefficient**: $D_B = k_B T / (3 \pi \eta d)$.

**Role of Aerodynamic Diameter in Deposition:**

The aerodynamic diameter is the **master variable** for predicting deposition site. The correlation is summarized below:

| Aerodynamic Diameter ($d_a$) | Predominant Deposition Site | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| > 10 μm | Oropharynx, nasal cavity | Inertial impaction |
| 5–10 μm | Trachea, main bronchi | Inertial impaction |
| 1–5 μm | Bronchioles, alveoli | Sedimentation + diffusion |
| 0.1–1 μm | Minimal deposition (exhaled) | Negligible |
| < 0.1 μm | Exhaled | Brownian diffusion (low mass) |

**Formulation Principle:** To target the alveoli, formulators engineer aerosols with **MMAD between 1 and 5 μm** and **GSD < 1.22** (monodisperse).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Pulmonary drug delivery relies on specialized devices that generate aerosols of the right particle size. There are **three primary categories**:

**1. Pressurized Metered Dose Inhalers (pMDIs):**

- **Construction:** Aluminum canister containing drug suspension/solution in a liquefied propellant (HFA-134a, HFA-227), fitted with a metering valve and actuator mouthpiece.
- **Working:** Actuation releases a metered volume (25–100 μL) of propellant that atomizes into droplets upon expansion.
- **Advantages:**
  - Portable, compact, multi-dose.
  - Rapid delivery, dose reproducible.
  - No contamination (sealed canister).
- **Limitations:**
  - Requires hand–lung coordination.
  - Cold Freon effect (cold aerosol may cause cough).
  - Propellant environmental concerns (CFC phase-out → HFA reformulation).
  - High oropharyngeal deposition (~70%).

**2. Dry Powder Inhalers (DPIs):**

- **Construction:** Device containing the drug as a dry powder (often blended with a coarse carrier like lactose), actuated by patient inspiration.
- **Working:** Patient's inspiratory airflow de-agglomerates the powder and disperses fine particles (<5 μm).
- **Types:**
  - **Single-dose** (e.g., HandiHaler — tiotropium)
  - **Multi-dose** (e.g., Diskus — salmeterol/fluticasone; Turbuhaler — budesonide)
- **Advantages:**
  - Breath-actuated (no coordination needed).
  - Propellant-free (eco-friendly).
  - Stable formulation (dry state).
- **Limitations:**
  - Requires adequate inspiratory flow (>30 L/min) — may be problematic in children/elderly.
  - Sensitive to humidity.
  - Dose depends on inspiratory effort.

**3. Nebulizers:**

Convert drug solutions or suspensions into a fine mist for inhalation via mouthpiece or mask. Two main types:

**(i) Jet (Pneumatic) Nebulizers:**

- **Construction:** Compressor-driven; high-velocity gas stream through a narrow nozzle creates a pressure drop (Venturi effect) drawing liquid up and shattering it into droplets.
- **Advantages:** No coordination needed; high-dose delivery possible; suitable for acute and pediatric use.
- **Limitations:** Bulky, noisy, long treatment time (5–15 min); device-dependent aerosol characteristics.

**(ii) Ultrasonic Nebulizers:**

- **Construction:** Piezoelectric crystal vibrates at high frequency (1–3 MHz), generating droplets from the solution.
- **Advantages:** Faster, quieter than jet nebulizers.
- **Limitations:** Inefficient for suspensions; device heating may degrade thermolabile drugs.

**(iii) Mesh (Vibrating Mesh) Nebulizers:**

- **Construction:** Drug forced through a vibrating mesh with 3–5 μm apertures.
- **Advantages:** Highly efficient, low residual volume, silent, portable.
- **Limitations:** Expensive; mesh requires cleaning.

**4. Soft Mist Inhalers (SMIs) — Advanced:**

- **Example:** Respimat.
- **Mechanism:** Mechanical spring forces drug solution through a fine nozzle, generating a slow-moving (10 mph) aerosol with longer lung deposition time.
- **Advantages:** Higher lung deposition (~50%) than MDIs; no propellant; less coordination needed.
- **Limitations:** Single/multi-dose replacement cartridges; mechanical complexity.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Device | Particle Size (μm) | Lung Deposition | Coordination | Patient Population |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **pMDI** | 1–5 | 10–20% | Required | Adults, adolescents |
| **DPI** | 1–5 | 15–30% | Not required | Adults with adequate inspiratory flow |
| **Jet Nebulizer** | 1–5 | 10–15% | Not required | Infants, elderly, acute settings |
| **Soft Mist Inhaler** | 1–5 | 40–50% | Minimal | Broad, including elderly |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting $\rho_0 = 1$ g/cm³ in aerodynamic diameter formula.** Always state this explicitly. Examiners deduct 1 mark if omitted.
> 
> 2. **Confusing geometric diameter ($d$) with aerodynamic diameter ($d_a$).** They are NOT the same. Aerodynamic diameter accounts for shape and density; geometric diameter does not.
> 
> 3. **Writing "Fick's Law" without mentioning the steady-state assumption.** The equation $J = D \cdot \Delta C / h$ applies only at **steady state**. Always state this.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the role of mucociliary clearance.** Nasal drugs are cleared within 15–20 min. If a question asks about "limitations of nasal delivery," mentioning mucociliary clearance is a must.
> 
> 5. **In pulmonary diagrams, NOT labeling the three deposition mechanisms (impaction, sedimentation, diffusion).** Examiners specifically look for these labels.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to classify devices:** MDIs, DPIs, and Nebulizers are the three pillars. Missing even one costs a full sub-question.
> 
> 7. **Confusing FPF and MMAD:** FPF is a fraction (% of particles <5 μm), MMAD is a diameter value (μm). Never interchange them.
> 
> 8. **Omitting the role of the olfactory region** in nose-to-brain delivery. This is a KTU 2024 high-weightage "favorite" question topic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read This 30 Minutes Before Your Exam:**

- ✅ **Aerodynamic diameter** is the **single most important parameter** in pulmonary delivery. Formula: $d_a = d \sqrt{\rho_p / (\rho_0 \chi)}$.
- ✅ **Optimal alveolar deposition:** $d_a = 1$ – $5 \mu m$ (MMAD in this range).
- ✅ **Oropharyngeal deposition** dominates for $d_a > 5 \mu m$ (inertial impaction).
- ✅ **Exhalation** dominates for $d_a < 0.5 \mu m$ (insufficient mass).
- ✅ **Three deposition mechanisms:** Inertial Impaction (upper airways), Sedimentation (bronchioles), Brownian Diffusion (alveoli).
- ✅ **Nasal cavity has three regions:** Vestibular (filter), Respiratory (systemic absorption), Olfactory (CNS access).
- ✅ **Nasal mucosa thickness:** Respiratory ~4–5 μm; Olfactory ~50–200 μm.
- ✅ **Alveolar surface area:** ~70–100 m² — **largest absorptive surface in the body**.
- ✅ **Mucociliary clearance half-life:** 15–20 minutes — limits nasal drug residence.
- ✅ **Nasal bioavailability (systemic):** 10–20% (variable).
- ✅ **MW limit for nasal absorption:** < 1000 Da preferred.
- ✅ **Both routes avoid first-pass metabolism.**
- ✅ **Inhalation device triad:** pMDI (propellant, coordination needed), DPI (breath-actuated, inspiratory flow needed), Nebulizer (no coordination, suitable for acute/pediatric).
- ✅ **Soft mist inhalers** offer highest lung deposition (~50%) without propellant.
- ✅ **FPF (% particles <5 μm)** is the standard quality attribute for inhalation aerosols.
- ✅ **GSD < 1.22** indicates a monodisperse (good) aerosol.
- ✅ **Cascade impactor (Anderson, NGI)** is the standard apparatus to measure MMAD, GSD, and FPF.
- ✅ **MALT (Mucosa-Associated Lymphoid Tissue)** in nasal and bronchial regions enables **vaccine delivery** (e.g., FluMist).
- ✅ **Nose-to-brain delivery** exploits the olfactory neuroepithelium to bypass the BBB.
- ✅ **Key nasal drugs:** Sumatriptan, desmopressin, naloxone, calcitonin, estradiol.
- ✅ **Key pulmonary drugs:** Salbutamol, budesonide, salmeterol, fluticasone, tobramycin, treprostinil.
- ✅ **Fick's First Law at steady state:** $J = D \cdot \Delta C / h$.
- ✅ **The Stoke's number** ($Stk > 1$) predicts inertial impaction dominance.
- ✅ **CFC propellants** have been phased out and replaced by **HFA-134a and HFA-227** for environmental safety.
- ✅ **Large Porous Particles (LPPs)** and **Trojan Particles** are emerging formulation strategies to engineer large particles with low aerodynamic diameters.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 3: Naso-Pulmonary Drug Delivery System**
> **Total Marks Weightage (KTU 2024 ESE): ~14–20 marks per module**
> **Recommended Study Time: 6–8 hours for mastery**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulation of Inhalers (Dry Powder and Metered Dose)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBB862 - DRUG DELIVERY PRINCIPLE & APPLICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Formulation of Inhalers (Dry Powder and Metered Dose) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition: Pulmonary Drug Delivery & Inhalers **Pulmonary drug delivery** is the targeted administration of pharmaceutically active ingredients via inhalation, where the formulation is aerosolized and deposited in the respiratory tract — typically the bronchi, bronchioles, and alveolar region — for either **local action** (e.g., asthma, COPD) or **systemic absorption** (e.g., insulin, vaccines). An **inhaler** is a medical device that converts a drug formulation (solution, suspension, or dry powder) into a fine **aerosol** with an aerodynamic diameter small enough to navigate the upper airways and reach the deep lung. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Inhalers are classified as **Pressurized Metered Dose Inhalers (pMDIs)**, **Dry Powder Inhalers (DPIs)**, and **Nebulizers** based on the physical state of the formulation and the energy source used to generate the aerosol. ### 1.2 The Two Major Inhaler Classes | Class | Full Form | State of Drug | Energy Source | |---|---|---|---| | **MDI** | Metered Dose Inhaler | Solution or suspension in propellant | Pressurized liquefied propellant (HFA) | | **DPI** | Dry Powder Inhaler | Micronized dry powder blend | Patient's inspiratory airflow | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Bicycle vs. Motorcycle" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Pump — Think of it this way:** > - An **MDI is like a fire extinguisher**: it stores its own "energy" (compressed propellant) and pushes a precise dose out when the trigger is pressed. The patient only needs to coordinate their breath with the spray. > - A **DPI is like a bicycle**: the patient supplies all the "energy" by inhaling forcefully. The harder and faster you inhale, the more the powder is dispersed and carried deep into the lungs. > - A **nebulizer is like a garden hose with a spray nozzle**: it continuously converts a liquid reservoir into a mist for slow, tidal breathing (good for children and the elderly). This single analogy captures the entire mechanical philosophy of inhaler design: **where does the aerosolizing energy come from?** ### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Examinable) - **MMAD (Mass Median Aerodynamic Diameter):** The diameter below which **50%** of the aerosol mass resides. Optimal alveolar deposition: **1–5 μm**. Coarse particles >5 μm deposit in the oropharynx; ultra-fine particles <1 μm tend to be exhaled. - **FPF (Fine Particle Fraction):** The percentage of the emitted dose contained in particles **<5 μm** (or <3 μm in some standards). Reported as **% FPF**. - **ED (Emitted Dose):** The total mass of drug leaving the device per actuation, measured in **micrograms (μg)**. - **GSD (Geometric Standard Deviation):** A measure of particle size distribution width; a value close to **1.0** indicates a monodisperse aerosol, while **>1.5** is polydisperse. - **DDU (Dose Delivered to the User):** The dose leaving the mouthpiece, distinct from the *metered dose* inside the canister. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Metered Dose Inhaler (MDI) — Deep Theory #### 2.1.1 MDI Anatomy An MDI is a closed pressurized system comprising three functional units: 1. **Canister (Container):** Aluminum or stainless steel, holds the formulation under pressure (~3–5 bar for HFA systems). 2. **Metering Valve:** A precision-engineered component that delivers a fixed volume (typically **25 μL, 50 μL, or 63 μL**) per actuation. Contains the metering chamber, ferrule, stem, and gasket. 3. **Actuator (Mouthpiece):** A plastic L-shaped or T-shaped piece that channels the spray and acts as a **primary atomizing nozzle** with an orifice diameter of ~0.3–0.5 mm. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The metering valve is the *heart* of the MDI. It must be chemically inert, resist cold-welding under pressure, and deliver a reproducible volume of **±10%** of the label claim across the full canister life. #### 2.1.2 MDI Formulation Components | Component | Function | Example | Typical % (w/w) | |---|---|---|---| | **Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API)** | Therapeutic agent | Salbutamol sulfate, Beclomethasone dipropionate | 0.1–2 % | | **Propellant** | Solvent + atomization energy | HFA-134a, HFA-227 | 70–99 % | | **Co-solvent** | Solubilization aid for poorly soluble drugs | Ethanol, PEG 400 | 0–30 % | | **Surfactant / Dispersing agent** | Suspension stability, valve lubrication | Oleic acid, Lecithin, Sorbitan trioleate | 0.01–0.1 % | | **Suspending agent / Bulking agent** | Suspension stabilization | Sorbitan trioleate, mineral oil | Trace | #### 2.1.3 Propellant Evolution — The CFCs-to-HFAs Transition **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)** — CFC-11, CFC-12, CFC-114 — were the original propellants. They were **phased out under the Montreal Protocol (1987)** because they depleted the stratospheric ozone layer. They are now **completely banned** in pharmaceutical aerosols. **Hydrofluoroalkanes (HFAs)** — HFA-134a (1,1,1,2-tetrafluoroethane) and HFA-227 (1,1,1,2,3,3,3-heptafluoropropane) — replaced CFCs. They are: - **Zero ODP** (Ozone Depletion Potential) - **Zero VOC** (Volatile Organic Compound) classification issues - **Lower GWP** than CFC-12 - **Higher vapor pressure** and different solvent properties than CFCs (require reformulation with co-solvents and surfactants) #### 2.1.4 Two MDI Formulation Strategies - **Suspension MDI (s-MDI):** Micronized drug particles suspended in propellant. Easier to manufacture. Risk of **particle growth (Ostwald ripening)**, **creaming**, and **valve clogging**. Requires **low-shear** valves and **electropolished** canisters. - **Solution MDI:** Drug dissolved in a co-solvent/propellant mixture. Produces **finer aerosols** (MMAD ~1.2–1.8 μm). Risk of **chemical degradation** and **re-crystallization** on actuator. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight — Why does solution MDI give smaller particles?** > In a suspension MDI, the drug is already a solid; propellant evaporation just shrinks the droplet to the size of the original solid. In a solution MDI, the drug precipitates as the propellant flashes off, producing a near-monodisperse sub-micron aerosol. This is why solution MDIs (e.g., QVAR, beclomethasone dipropionate in HFA-134a) have superior alveolar deposition. ### 2.2 Dry Powder Inhaler (DPI) — Deep Theory #### 2.2.1 DPI Anatomy A DPI is a passive, breath-actuated device containing: 1. **Powder reservoir / blister strip / capsule** — stores unit doses. 2. **Dispersion mechanism** — capsules must be pierced (e.g., HandiHaler), blisters peeled (e.g., Diskus), or the powder reservoir metered (e.g., Turbuhaler). 3. **Air-flow path / cyclone / de-agglomerator** — converts bulk powder into an aerosol using patient inspiratory effort. 4. **Mouthpiece** — delivers aerosol to the patient. #### 2.2.2 DPI Formulation Components | Component | Function | Typical Example | Size | |---|---|---|---| | **Active drug** | Therapeutic effect | Budesonide, Tiotropium | MMAD 1–5 μm (micronized) | | **Carrier (coarse)** | Bulk flowability, de-agglomeration | α-Lactose monohydrate | 50–200 μm | | **Fines (fine excipient)** | Adhesion modulator | Fine α-lactose (<5 μm) | 1–5 μm | | **Tertiary agent (force control agent)** | Reduce drug-carrier adhesion | Magnesium stearate, leucine, dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC) | 1–5 μm | | **Anti-static / desiccant** | Powder stability | Silica gel sachet | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Classic Question — The "Carrier Problem":** > Micronized drug (1–5 μm) is *too cohesive* to flow freely. It must be **adhered to a coarse carrier** (lactose, 50–200 μm) for flow. However, it must also **detach** from the carrier during inspiration. This is the **fundamental formulation paradox** of DPI design: **adhere in the device, disperse in the lung.** #### 2.2.3 The Order of Mixing Principle 1. **Pre-mixing:** Coarse lactose + fine lactose (10–20 min low-shear blending). 2. **Drug addition:** Micronized drug layered onto pre-mixed carrier for 30–60 min. 3. **Tertiary addition:** Mg stearate (1% w/w) blended in last for 15–30 min to occupy high-energy adhesion sites. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Aerodynamic Diameter** | $d_{ae} = d_{v}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_0}\chi}$ | $d_v$ = geometric diameter, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $\chi$ = dynamic shape factor, $\rho_0$ = 1 g/cm³ | | **MMAD definition** | Mass fraction of particles $\le$ MMAD = 0.5 | μm | | **Stokes Number (inhaler)** | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, U}{18 \, \mu \, D}$ | Predicts particle deposition; $U$ = air velocity, $D$ = airway diameter | | **Cunningham Slip Correction** | $C_c = 1 + \dfrac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left(A_1 + A_2 \, e^{-A_3 d_p / (2\lambda)}\right)$ | Corrects drag for particles $< 5$ μm | | **Cutoff Diameter (impactor)** | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \, \mu \, L \, \ln(Stk_{50})}{\rho_p \, U}}$ | Used in cascade impactor theory | | **Emitted Dose (ED)** | $ED = \dfrac{\text{Drug mass leaving device}}{\text{Total drug loaded}} \times 100$ | % | | **Fine Particle Fraction (FPF)** | $FPF = \dfrac{\text{Drug in particles} < 5\,\mu m}{\text{Total emitted dose}} \times 100$ | % | | **Cascade Impactor Mass Balance** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = \text{RD} + \text{ED}$ | $m_i$ = mass on stage $i$ | | **Inhalation Flow Rate Targets (DPI)** | 30–60 L/min for most devices | Higher = better de-agglomeration but more oropharyngeal deposition | | **Breath-hold rule** | $t_{hold} = 10\,\text{s}$ | Allows sedimentation in alveoli | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Table Safety:** The vertical pipe inside formulas like $\vert x \vert$ has been replaced with `$\vert x \vert$` notation. For absolute values in tables, use `$\vert$ x $\vert$` or simply describe the quantity in words. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmacological impact:** A well-formulated DPI with FPF > 30% can deliver ~3–5× more drug to the lungs than a poorly formulated one at the same nominal dose. - **Patient compliance:** DPIs do not require hand–breath coordination (unlike MDIs), making them suitable for pediatric and geriatric populations. - **Global market:** The inhaled drug delivery market is valued at ~**USD 35–40 billion** (2024 estimate), with DPI products like Advair Diskus, Spiriva HandiHaler, and Symbicort Turbuhaler leading the segment. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Aerodynamic Diameter The **aerodynamic diameter ($d_{ae}$)** is the cornerstone of aerosol science. It is defined as the diameter of a *unit-density sphere* (1 g/cm³) that has the same terminal settling velocity as the particle in question. > **Step 1 — Terminal settling velocity by Stokes' Law (for $Re < 0.1$):** > > $$v_t = \dfrac{(\rho_p - \rho_{air}) \, g \, d_v^2}{18 \, \mu} \cdot \chi^{-1}$$ > > where $\rho_p$ is the particle density, $d_v$ is the volume-equivalent diameter, $\mu$ is the gas viscosity, and $\chi$ is the dynamic shape factor. > **Step 2 — Define reference sphere (1 g/cm³) with diameter $d_{ae}$:** > > $$v_t^{ref} = \dfrac{(1 - \rho_{air}) \, g \, d_{ae}^2}{18 \, \mu}$$ > **Step 3 — Equate the two terminal velocities ($v_t = v_t^{ref}$), neglect $\rho_{air}$ in comparison to $\rho_p$:** > > $$\dfrac{\rho_p \, d_v^2}{18 \, \mu \, \chi} = \dfrac{d_{ae}^2}{18 \, \mu}$$ > **Step 4 — Solve for $d_{ae}$:** > > $$d_{ae} = d_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_p}{\chi}}$$ > > For a sphere $\chi = 1$, simplifying to: > > $$\boxed{d_{ae} = d_v \, \sqrt{\rho_p}}$$ > > with $\rho_0 = 1$ g/cm³ absorbed into the definition. **Interpretation:** A porous particle of geometric diameter 5 μm but density 0.2 g/cm³ has $d_{ae} \approx 5\sqrt{0.2} = 2.24\,\mu m$ — this is the rationale behind **large porous particles (PulmoSpheres, $d_v \approx 5$ μm, $\rho \approx 0.1$ g/cm³)** achieving alveolar deposition with reduced phagocytic clearance. ### 3.2 Derivation: Cascade Impactor Cutoff Diameter A **cascade impactor** (e.g., Andersen Cascade Impactor, ACI; Next Generation Impactor, NGI) is the gold-standard instrument for *in vitro* aerosol characterization. Each stage is a set of jets with progressively smaller orifices, accelerating the air to successively higher velocities.
