Particles above a cutoff diameter impact; smaller particles follow the air to the next stage. > **Step 1 — Reynolds number at the jet:** > > $$Re_j = \dfrac{\rho_{air} \, U_j \, W}{\mu}$$ > > where $U_j$ = jet velocity, $W$ = jet width (orifice diameter). > **Step 2 — Particle relaxation time ($\tau$):** > > $$\tau = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, C_c}{18 \, \mu}$$ > > Cunningham correction $C_c \approx 1$ for $d_p > 5$ μm; $C_c > 1$ for smaller. > **Step 3 — Stokes number at the stage:** > > $$Stk_j = \dfrac{\tau \, U_j}{W/2} = \dfrac{2 \, \tau \, U_j}{W}$$ > **Step 4 — 50% cutoff occurs at $Stk_{50} \approx 0.22$ for round jets in the NGI (empirically calibrated).** Setting $Stk_j = Stk_{50}$ and solving for $d_{50}$: > > $$Stk_{50} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_{50}^2 \, C_c \, U_j}{9 \, \mu \, W}$$ > > Solving for $d_{50}$: > > $$\boxed{d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \, \mu \, W \, Stk_{50}}{\rho_p \, C_c \, U_j}}}$$ **Practical use:** Knowing the jet geometry and flow rate, one predicts which stage a given particle lands on. The cumulative mass below a given $d_{50}$ yields the FPF. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DPI De-Agglomeration Trade-Off This matrix guides a formulator in selecting carrier properties: | Carrier Property | Effect on Flowability | Effect on Dispersibility | Optimal Range | |---|---|---|---| | ↑ Particle size | ↑ Flow | ↓ Dispersibility | 50–200 μm | | ↑ Surface roughness | ↓ Adhesion strength | ↑ Dispersibility | $R_a > 1$ μm | | ↑ Fine lactose content | ↓ Flow | ↑ Dispersibility (saturation) | 5–15% w/w | | ↑ Mg stearate | ↑ Flow + ↓ Adhesion | ↑ Dispersibility | 1–2% w/w | | ↑ Humidity | ↓ Flow + ↑ Adhesion | ↓ Dispersibility | <50% RH storage | **Reading:** A higher Mg stearate concentration *appears* universally beneficial, but at concentrations > 2% it can form a *continuous film* on the lactose, trapping the drug in a *second-order aggregate* and *reducing* FPF. ### 3.4 Step-by-Step: MDI Actuation Sequence > **Step 1** — Patient shakes canister (for suspensions) to re-disperse drug. > **Step 2** — Pressing the canister drives the stem into the actuator, opening the metering chamber. > **Step 3** — The liquefied propellant in the chamber is exposed to atmospheric pressure and **flashes** (boils) to a high-velocity gas. > **Step 4** — The shear at the actuator orifice breaks the propellant stream into a **primary droplet** spray of MMAD 8–30 μm. > **Step 5** — Propellant evaporation during flight shrinks droplets; the final aerosol has MMAD 1.5–4 μm at the mouthpiece. > **Step 6** — Patient inhales within 1–2 seconds of firing; a slow, deep breath + 10-s breath-hold maximizes alveolar deposition. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 MDI Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Drug Substance
Micronized 1-5 um] --> B[Container Closure System] C[Propellant
HFA-134a or HFA-227] --> B D[Co-solvent
Ethanol] --> B E[Surfactant
Oleic Acid] --> B B --> F[Pressurized Canister
3-5 bar] F --> G[Metering Valve
25-100 uL] G --> H[Actuator Orifice
0.3-0.5 mm] H --> I[Primary Spray
MMAD 8-30 um] I --> J[Evaporation in Flight] J --> K[Final Aerosol
MMAD 1.5-4 um] K --> L[Oropharyngeal Deposition] K --> M[Bronchial Deposition] K --> N[Alveolar Deposition] ``` ### 4.2 DPI Dispersion Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Drug API
1-5 um] --> M1[Order of Mixing] P2[Coarse Lactose
50-200 um] --> M1 P3[Fine Lactose
1-5 um] --> M1 P4[Tertiary Agent
Mg Stearate 1-2%] --> M1 M1 --> R[Final Powder Blend] R --> S[Device Reservoir or Capsule] S --> T[Patient Inspiratory Effort
30-60 L/min] T --> U[De-agglomeration Stage
Cyclone or Mesh] U --> V[Detached Drug Aerosol] U --> W[Recycled Carrier
Impacts Throat] V --> X[Deep Lung Deposition] ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree: Choosing MDI vs. DPI ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient Inhaler Selection] --> B{Patient Coordination?} B -- Poor --> C{Drug Solubility in HFA?} B -- Good --> D[Consider MDI] C -- Soluble --> E[Solution MDI] C -- Insoluble --> F{Dose Size?} F -- High > 1 mg --> G[DPI Preferred] F -- Low < 200 ug --> H[Suspension MDI] D --> I{Sustainability Goal?} I -- Yes --> J[DPI No Propellant] I -- No --> K[Spacer Compatible MDI] ``` ### 4.4 Cascade Impactor Stage Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Induction Port
Mimics Throat] --> B[Stage 0
Cutoff 9.0 um] B --> C[Stage 1
Cutoff 5.8 um] C --> D[Stage 2
Cutoff 4.7 um] D --> E[Stage 3
Cutoff 3.3 um] E --> F[Stage 4
Cutoff 2.1 um] F --> G[Stage 5
Cutoff 1.1 um] G --> H[Stage 6
Cutoff 0.7 um] H --> I[Filter
Captures sub-0.7 um] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** The cascade impactor architecture maps directly to human respiratory deposition — Stage 0–1 corresponds to oropharyngeal, Stages 2–4 to bronchial, Stages 5–6 to alveolar, and filter to exhaled fraction. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between solution and suspension MDI formulations. Give one marketed example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Solution MDI | Suspension MDI | |---|---|---| | Drug state | Dissolved in propellant ± co-solvent | Dispersed as micronized particles | | Particle formation | Drug precipitates on propellant flash | Pre-formed solid shrinks on flash | | Aerosol MMAD | 1.2–1.8 μm (smaller) | 2.5–4.0 μm (larger) | | FPF | Higher | Lower | | Stability issue | Re-crystallization | Creaming, agglomeration, valve clogging | | Example | QVAR (beclomethasone in HFA-134a) | Ventolin HFA (salbutamol in HFA-134a) | #### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **What is the role of magnesium stearate as a "ternary agent" in DPI formulations?** **Model Answer:** Magnesium stearate (1–2% w/w) is a *force control agent* (FCA) added as a ternary component. It preferentially occupies the high-energy adhesion sites on coarse lactose surfaces during blending, thereby **(a)** reducing the drug–carrier adhesion force and **(b)** improving powder flowability. The net effect is a significant increase in FPF at low-to-moderate inspiratory flow rates (30–60 L/min), with no major impact on chemical stability. However, over-concentration (> 2%) causes film formation and a paradoxical reduction in dispersibility. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the formulation components and manufacturing of a **pressurized Metered Dose Inhaler (pMDI)**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Components of a pMDI:** A pMDI consists of three integrated subsystems: - **Container (Canister):** Aluminum or tin-plated steel. Holds formulation at 3–5 bar. Internal surface is electropolished to prevent drug–wall interaction and particle adhesion. - **Metering Valve:** Stainless steel or polymer body with: (i) metering chamber (25, 50, or 63 μL), (ii) stem with axial orifice, (iii) gasket (Buna, EPDM, or neoprene), (iv) ferrule. Tolerances are ±10% of label claim. - **Actuator (Mouthpiece):** Injection-molded plastic (polypropylene, polyethylene) with an expansion chamber and nozzle orifice of 0.3–0.5 mm. **2. Formulation composition:** | Component | Function | Example | % w/w | |---|---|---|---| | API | Therapeutic | Salbutamol sulfate | 0.1–0.5 | | Propellant | Solvent + driving force | HFA-134a | 95–99 | | Co-solvent | Solubility enhancer | Ethanol | 0–30 | | Surfactant | Suspension stability / valve lubrication | Oleic acid | 0.01–0.05 | **3. Manufacturing steps:** 1. **Drug micronization:** Jet-milling or spray-drying to achieve MMAD 1–5 μm. 2. **Pre-mixing of excipients:** Ethanol + oleic acid + drug in a high-shear mixer. 3. **Pressure filling:** Propellant is cold-filled (–50 °C) or pressure-filled (preferred) into the canister through the valve crimping machine. 4. **Valve crimping:** The metering valve is crimped onto the canister under nitrogen. 5. **Actuator assembly + leak testing:** Each unit tested for pressure integrity and valve actuation force. 6. **Quality control:** Assay, leak rate, plume geometry, shot weight, content uniformity, FPF by ACI. --- **b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of replacing CFCs with HFAs** in pMDI formulations. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Reasons for CFC replacement (Montreal Protocol, 1987):** CFC-11, CFC-12, and CFC-114 were identified as **stratospheric ozone-depleting substances (ODP > 0)**. The Montreal Protocol mandated a global phase-out, fully implemented in pharmaceutical aerosols by **2008**. **Advantages of HFAs:** 1. **Zero ODP** — environmentally compliant. 2. **Lower GWP** than CFC-12 (HFA-134a GWP ≈ 1430 vs. CFC-12 GWP ≈ 10,900). 3. **Higher vapor pressure** at 25 °C (~5.7 bar for HFA-134a) — improved spray dynamics. 4. **Reduced flammability** vs. hydrocarbons. 5. **Superior lung deposition** — solution MDIs in HFA can achieve MMAD 1.2–1.8 μm, vs. ~3.5 μm for CFC suspension MDIs (e.g., QVAR vs. Beclovent). 6. **Reduced "cold-Freon" effect** — HFA-134a is less cold on actuation, improving patient comfort. **Disadvantages / Challenges:** 1. **Different solvent properties** — HFA-134a is a poor solvent for many APIs. Requires co-solvents (ethanol up to 30%) and novel surfactants. 2. **Reformulation burden** — entire stability and bioequivalence programs had to be repeated; >USD 1 billion industry investment. 3. **Compatibility issues** with traditional elastomers (Buna, neoprene) and gaskets — required EPDM/butyl redesigns. 4. **Higher cost** of propellant (3–5× more expensive than CFC at the time of transition). 5. **Potential environmental concern** — HFA-134a is a weak greenhouse gas (GWP = 1430), still under EPA voluntary reduction programs. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — "Why not just keep using CFCs?"** > Many students lose 2 marks by answering this with vague "environmental" statements. You **must** explicitly mention: (i) the **Montreal Protocol (1987)**, (ii) **stratospheric ODP = 0** for HFAs, and (iii) one quantitative comparison (e.g., GWP value or vapor pressure). --- #### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **a)** Explain the **principle and components of Dry Powder Inhaler (DPI) formulation**. Why are carrier particles essential? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Principle of DPI:** A DPI is a **passive, breath-actuated** device that delivers a dry powder formulation to the lungs. The patient's inspiratory airflow provides the **de-agglomeration energy** — the kinetic energy of the inhaled air overcomes the interparticle adhesion forces holding the drug to the carrier, dispersing the fine drug particles into an inhalable aerosol (MMAD 1–5 μm). **Components of a DPI Formulation:** 1. **Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API):** Micronized drug (geometric size 1–5 μm). Examples: budesonide, fluticasone propionate, salmeterol xinafoate, tiotropium bromide. 2. **Coarse Carrier Particles:** α-Lactose monohydrate, 50–200 μm. Provides bulk, flowability, and dose metering. 3. **Fine Excipient (Fines):** α-Lactose, 1–5 μm, 5–15% w/w. Saturates high-energy sites, allowing more drug to occupy low-energy sites that detach easily. 4. **Tertiary Agent (Force Control Agent):** Mg stearate, leucine, or DPPC, 1–2% w/w. Further reduces drug–carrier adhesion. 5. **Anti-Static / Desiccant:** Silica gel sachet for moisture control. **Why are carrier particles essential?** Micronized drug particles (1–5 μm) are **highly cohesive** — they have a surface-area-to-mass ratio of ~1 m²/g and experience dominant **van der Waals** and **electrostatic** forces. Consequences: - They **cannot flow** through device metering mechanisms (Hopper problems). - They **agglomerate** irreversibly into clusters >50 μm that impact in the oropharynx. To solve this, the micronized drug is **adhered onto a coarse carrier (lactose, 50–200 μm)**. The carrier: - Provides **flowability** for reproducible dose metering. - Acts as a **diluent** to improve content uniformity. - Carries the drug through the device into the inspiratory airflow. - The drug is **designed to detach** at high shear in the device's de-agglomeration zone, leaving the heavy carrier to impact the throat. This is the **fundamental paradox** of DPI: the drug must *adhere strongly enough* to survive handling and metering, but *detach easily* during inspiration. --- **b)** With suitable equations, describe the **role of aerodynamic diameter in pulmonary deposition** and how it influences DPI/MDI design. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The **aerodynamic diameter ($d_{ae}$)** is the diameter of a unit-density sphere (1 g/cm³) that has the same terminal settling velocity as the particle in question: $$d_{ae} = d_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_p}{\chi \cdot \rho_0}}$$ For a sphere ($\chi = 1$, $\rho_0 = 1$ g/cm³): $$\boxed{d_{ae} = d_v \, \sqrt{\rho_p}}$$ **Deposition zones as a function of $d_{ae}$:** | $d_{ae}$ range | Deposition zone | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | > 10 μm | Oropharynx (mouth, throat) | Inertial impaction | | 5–10 μm | Trachea, large bronchi | Inertial impaction + sedimentation | | 1–5 μm | Bronchioles, alveoli | Sedimentation + diffusion (optimal) | | 0.1–1 μm | Alveoli, but exhalation loss | Brownian diffusion | | < 0.1 μm | Exhaled | No deposition | **Implications for DPI design:** 1. **Drug particles must be 1–5 μm** — this is the "respirable fraction" range. 2. The carrier lactose (50–200 μm) is **intentionally oversized** so it impacts the throat and is swallowed (or expectorated). 3. The device's **cyclone/mixing channels** must generate turbulence such that **Stokes number** at the throat is < 0.1 for the drug, but > 1 for the carrier — this enables selective detachment and separation. **Implications for MDI design:** 1. **Solution MDIs** generate MMAD 1.2–1.8 μm by precipitating drug during propellant flash — superior alveolar deposition. 2. **Suspension MDIs** generate MMAD 2.5–4.0 μm, limited by the size of the original micronized drug. 3. **Spacer/valve holding chambers** (VHC) reduce the oropharyngeal deposition of large particles (5–30 μm primary droplets) by allowing them to settle or impact on the chamber walls. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common marks lost on this question:** > 1. Forgetting to define $d_{ae}$ in terms of the *unit-density sphere reference*. Many students write $d_{ae} = d_v$ — this loses 2 marks. > 2. Confusing **aerodynamic** with **geometric** diameter. The two are equal *only* for spheres of density 1 g/cm³. > 3. Failing to give a numerical deposition range. The 1–5 μm range is the KTU board's *expected* answer. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **MMAD** is the single most cited metric in inhaler testing; **1–5 μm** is the target for alveolar deposition. - **CFCs are gone; HFAs are mandatory** in all MDIs since 2008 (Montreal Protocol). - **Solution MDI > Suspension MDI** in terms of fine particle generation, but formulation stability is harder. - **DPI formulation paradox:** drug must adhere to the carrier in the device, but detach in the lung. - **α-Lactose monohydrate** is the gold-standard DPI carrier; **Mg stearate** is the prototypical tertiary agent. - **Cascade impactor** stages map directly to human respiratory zones — Stage 0 = oropharynx, Stages 4–5 = alveoli. - **Breath-hold for 10 s** post-inhalation maximizes sedimentation in alveoli. - **Hand–breath coordination** is required for MDIs (use a spacer for children/elderly); DPIs do not require coordination. - **Inspiratory flow rate** for DPIs: target **30–60 L/min** (some modern devices accept > 20 L/min). - **Stokes number** governs impaction in airways and impactor stages: $Stk_{50} \approx 0.22$ for round jets. > [!TIP] > **One-Liner KTU Revision Summary:** > *MDIs use HFA propellant + 25 μL metering valve + actuator nozzle; DPIs use coarse lactose carrier + micronized drug + Mg stearate ternary agent. Target MMAD = 1–5 μm, FPF > 30%, ED tested by cascade impactor.*

topics.title: Nasal Sprays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB862 - DRUG DELIVERY PRINCIPLE & APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nasal Sprays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Nasal Sprays

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **nasal spray** is a pharmaceutical device–product combination that delivers a metered, atomized dose of an active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) — in solution, suspension, or emulsion form — into the nasal cavity, primarily for **local therapeutic action** (e.g., decongestants, corticosteroids, antihistamines) or **systemic absorption** (e.g., nasal vaccines, peptide hormones, analgesics) via the highly vascularized respiratory and olfactory mucosa.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A nasal spray is a *non-pressurized, metered-dose dispensing system* that uses a mechanical pump to generate a liquid aerosol of controlled droplet size (typically **10–200 μm**) for deposition on the nasal mucosa.

The system is officially classified by the **USFDA (2002 Guidance for Industry)**, **EMA Guideline on Pharmaceutical Development of Inhalation and Nasal Products (2012)**, and the **Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP 2022)** as a *metered-dose nasal spray (MDNS)*, distinguished from nasal drops (gravitational deposition), nasal powders (active dispersion), and pressurized metered-dose inhalers (propellant-driven).

## 1.2 Key Engineering & Pharmacological Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Spray Volume per actuation** | **50–140 μL** (most common 100 μL) | Dose metering accuracy |
| **Plume Geometry** | **Full cone angle: 30°–75°** | Determines deposition zone |
| **Droplet Size Distribution (DSD)** | **Dv50 = 30–120 μm** | Site of deposition (anterior vs. posterior) |
| **Spray Force / Impaction Force** | **< 50 mN (ideal < 35 mN)** | Patient comfort, anterior deposition |
| **Shot Weight / Dose Weight** | **0.08–0.14 g** | Pump consistency |
| **Dose Content Uniformity (DCU)** | Acceptance Value (AV) ≤ **15** | USP <905> compliance |
| **Pump-to-Pump Variability** | RSD < **± 5%** | Quality control |
| **Preservative System** | Benzalkonium chloride (BKC) **0.01–0.02% w/v** | Microbial stability |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **"The Garden Hose Nozzle Analogy":** Imagine holding a garden hose with your thumb partially blocking the opening. The water exits in a *fine, controlled cone* — neither a single jet (too forceful) nor a drizzle (too dispersed). The *amount of water* corresponds to the **dose**, the *spread of the cone* to the **plume geometry**, and the *fineness of droplets* to the **droplet size distribution**. A nasal spray is essentially a *miniaturized, precision-engineered version* of this concept, designed so the "water" lands exactly on the nasal walls — not down your throat, not out of your nose, but precisely on the absorptive mucosa. The pump replaces your thumb; the actuator nozzle replaces the hose tip; and the formulation replaces the water.

## 1.4 Classification of Nasal Delivery Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Relevant Classification Triad:**
> 1. **Liquid Nasal Sprays (Aqueous)** — Pump-based; solutions, suspensions, emulsions. *Most clinically common.*
> 2. **Nasal Powders** — Device-driven (breath-actuated); freeze-dried or spray-dried API. *Better stability, no preservatives.*
> 3. **Pressurized Nasal Aerosols (PNA)** — Propellant-based (HFA). *Newer, more reproducible DSD.*

> [!NOTE]
> Within **Liquid Nasal Sprays**, the FDA further sub-classifies:
> - **Metered Spray Pump** (MDPI-Nasal analogue) — most widely marketed.
> - **Single-Dose / Bi-dose Dispensers** — for vaccines, emergency drugs.
> - **Squeeze Bottles** — over-the-counter decongestants (less reproducible).

## 1.5 Anatomical Targets Within the Nasal Cavity

The nasal cavity (volume ≈ **15–20 mL**, surface area ≈ **150–180 cm²**) is divided into functional zones:

- **Vestibule** (~0.6 cm²): Stratified squamous epithelium; filters particles **> 10 μm** by impaction.
- **Respiratory Region** (turbinates): Pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium; **prime absorption zone** for systemic drugs.
- **Olfactory Region** (~2–5 cm²): Direct nose-to-brain pathway; **target for CNS drugs** (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's).
- **Nasopharynx**: Cilia sweep mucus toward throat (mucociliary clearance rate ≈ **5–6 mm/min**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — How a Metered-Dose Nasal Spray Works

A nasal spray is a **four-stage engineered system**. KTU examiners frequently test the *causal sequence*:

### Stage 1: Actuation (Mechanical Energy Input)
The patient presses the actuator downward. This compresses the **return spring** and pushes the **piston** into the **metering chamber** (a precisely machined cylinder of fixed volume, typically **50 μL or 100 μL**).

### Stage 2: Metering (Dose Quantification)
The piston expels the **pre-metered volume** of formulation from the chamber into the **dip tube**, which draws liquid from the reservoir bottle (typically amber glass or HDPE).

### Stage 3: Atomization (Aerosol Generation)
The liquid is forced under pressure (**P ≈ 2–6 bar**) through the **micro-orifice nozzle** (diameter ≈ **150–500 μm**) and the swirl chamber, where it undergoes:
- **Shear-induced break-up** (viscous forces > surface tension forces)
- **Centrifugal acceleration** in the swirl chamber
- **Rayleigh or Bagley break-up regime** depending on Weber number

### Stage 4: Plume Formation & Deposition
The atomized droplets travel in a defined **plume geometry** (cone angle 30°–75°) and impact the nasal mucosa. Deposition efficiency depends on **aerodynamic diameter**, **inspiratory flow rate**, and **spray insertion angle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Two-Filter" Deposition Concept (Heyder 1986, KTU-favorite):**
> - **Filter 1 — Impaction filter:** Droplets > 10 μm are trapped in the **anterior nasal region (vestibule)**.
> - **Filter 2 — Sedimentation filter:** Droplets 5–10 μm settle in the **turbinate region** (respiratory zone).
> - Droplets **< 1 μm** behave like gas and are exhaled — *useless for nasal therapy*.

## 2.2 The "Why & How" — Core Physical Chemistry

| Question | Engineering "Why" | Physicochemical "How" |
|---|---|---|
| Why droplet size matters? | Determines **aerosol stability** and **deposition site** | Governed by **Weber number** $We = \frac{\rho v^2 d}{\sigma}$ |
| Why viscosity must be controlled? | Affects **spray pattern** and **droplet velocity** | Newton's law of viscous flow; rheology (Newtonian vs. non-Newtonian) |
| Why pH is critical? | Mucosal tolerance requires pH **4.5–6.5**; ionization affects absorption | Henderson-Hasselbalch: $pH = pK_a + \log\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ |
| Why tonicity matters? | Isotonic (~**300 mOsm/kg**) prevents ciliary toxicity | Osmotic balance across nasal epithelium |
| Why surface tension? | Low σ (≈ **30–50 mN/m**) improves wetting and atomization | Laplace pressure: $\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma}{r}$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize and Practice These 10 Equations for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, d}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds number** — flow regime in nozzle | $\rho$: density (kg/m³), $v$: velocity (m/s), $d$: nozzle diameter (m), $\mu$: viscosity (Pa·s) | Predicts laminar vs. turbulent break-up |
| 2 | $We = \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2} \, d}{\sigma}$ | **Weber number** — surface tension vs. inertia | $\sigma$: surface tension (N/m) | If $We > We_{crit} \approx 12$, atomization occurs |
| 3 | $Oh = \dfrac{\mu_{l}}{\sqrt{\rho_{l} \, \sigma \, d}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{We}{Re}}$ | **Ohnesorge number** — viscous dissipation | $\mu_l$: liquid viscosity, $\rho_l$: liquid density | Independent of velocity; used in jet stability |
| 4 | $v_{s} = \dfrac{d^{2} \, g \, (\rho_{p} - \rho)}{18 \, \mu}$ | **Stokes' settling velocity** | $d$: droplet diameter, $g$: 9.81 m/s², $\rho_p$: particle density | Predicts gravitational deposition in posterior cavity |
| 5 | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_{p} \, d^{2} \, v}{18 \, \mu \, D}$ | **Stokes number** — inertial impaction | $D$: characteristic nasal airway diameter (~3 mm) | $Stk > 1$ ⇒ impaction dominates |
| 6 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4} \, \Delta P}{128 \, \mu \, L}$ | **Hagen-Poiseuille flow** in dip tube/nozzle | $L$: tube length, $\Delta P$: pressure drop | Pump flow rate design |
| 7 | $r_{D} = \dfrac{R_{h}}{k_{a}}$ | **Damköhler ratio** — reaction vs. transport | $R_h$: reaction rate, $k_a$: mass transfer coeff. | Mucosal absorption kinetics |
| 8 | $J = \dfrac{D \cdot (C_0 - C_{b})}{h}$ | **Fick's first law — drug flux across mucosa** | $D$: diffusion coefficient, $h$: mucus thickness (~5–10 μm) | Transmucosal absorption rate |
| 9 | $MMD \approx d_{0} \, (1 + 3.4 \, Oh)$ | **Median Mass Diameter** of droplet | $d_0$: orifice diameter | Empirical — relates geometry to droplet size |
| 10 | $F_{spray} = \rho \, Q \, v_{exit}$ | **Momentum flux / spray impact force** | $Q$: volumetric flow rate | Patient comfort threshold < **50 mN** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmacokinetics:** Equation (8) directly drives bioavailability calculations for systemic nasal drugs.
- **Device Design:** Equations (1)–(3) govern the nozzle/swirl chamber engineering in pumps manufactured by **Aptar Pharma, Gerresheimer (Bormioli), Nemera**.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** USP <601> "Inhalation and Nasal Products" uses equations (5) and (9) as quality gate criteria.
- **Patient Compliance:** Equation (10) is used in human-factor studies to ensure sprays do not trigger the sneeze reflex.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Critical Weber Number for Atomization

The **Weber number** determines whether a liquid jet emerging from a nozzle will remain coherent or break up into droplets. KTU may ask: *"Derive the relationship between Weber number and atomization regime."*

### Step 1: Define the Weber Number

The Weber number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to surface tension forces acting on a liquid element at the nozzle exit.

$$
We = \frac{\text{Inertial Force}}{\text{Surface Tension Force}} = \frac{\rho \, v^{2} \, d}{\sigma}
$$

### Step 2: Energy Balance on a Liquid Cylinder Element

Consider a cylindrical liquid jet of diameter $d$ moving with velocity $v$. The kinetic energy per unit volume is $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$. The surface energy per unit area (which resists break-up) is $\sigma$.

For the jet to break up, kinetic energy must overcome surface energy:

$$
\frac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} \ge \frac{2\sigma}{d}
$$

### Step 3: Rearrange for Critical Velocity

$$
v_{crit}^{2} \ge \frac{4\sigma}{\rho \, d}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute into Weber Number Definition

$$
We_{crit} = \frac{\rho \, v_{crit}^{2} \, d}{\sigma} = \frac{\rho \, d}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{4\sigma}{\rho \, d} = 4
$$

### Step 5: Refine Using Experimental Correction

Empirical work (Rayleigh, Weber, Ohnesorge) shows that a more accurate value, accounting for the most unstable perturbation wavelength ($\lambda \approx 4.51\,d$), yields:

$$
We_{crit} \approx 12
$$

### Step 6: Interpret for Nasal Spray Design

$$
We_{crit} \approx 12
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For a typical nasal spray formulation ($\rho \approx 1000$ kg/m³, $\sigma \approx 40 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, nozzle $d = 0.3$ mm), the minimum velocity required is:

$$
v_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{We_{crit} \cdot \sigma}{\rho \, d}} = \sqrt{\frac{12 \times 0.04}{1000 \times 3 \times 10^{-4}}} \approx 1.26 \text{ m/s}
$$

This is a small fraction of pump exit velocity (~10–30 m/s), confirming that **atomization is easily achieved** in practice — but the resulting droplet sizes are large, in the **respirable-but-not-inhalable** range appropriate for nasal deposition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Median Droplet Diameter (MMD) from Nozzle Geometry

KTU frequently asks for the relationship between **droplet size and orifice diameter** in a swirl-chamber pump.

### Step 1: Start from the Empirical Correlation

The volume median diameter of droplets in a pressure-atomized spray follows:

$$
MMD = K \cdot d_{0}^{a} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_{l}}{\rho_{a}}\right)^{b} \cdot \left(\frac{\mu_{l}}{\mu_{a}}\right)^{c} \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho_{l}}\right)^{e} \cdot \Delta P^{f}
$$

For most pharmaceutical sprays with low viscosity and similar air/liquid densities, a simplified form applies:

$$
MMD \approx C \cdot d_{0} \cdot Oh^{0.4}
$$

where $Oh$ is the **Ohnesorge number**.

### Step 2: Expand the Ohnesorge Number

$$
Oh = \frac{\mu_{l}}{\sqrt{\rho_{l} \, \sigma \, d_{0}}}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Explicit Values for a Typical Nasal Formulation

Let:
- $d_0 = 250 \, \mu m = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, m$
- $\mu_l = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$ (water-like)
- $\rho_l = 1000 \, kg/m^{3}$
- $\sigma = 45 \times 10^{-3} \, N/m$

### Step 4: Calculate Ohnesorge Number

$$
Oh = \frac{10^{-3}}{\sqrt{1000 \times 0.045 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}}}
$$

$$
Oh = \frac{10^{-3}}{\sqrt{1.125 \times 10^{-2}}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{0.1061} = 9.43 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate MMD

Using the empirical constant $C \approx 3.4$:

$$
MMD \approx 3.4 \times d_{0} \times (1 + 3.4 \cdot Oh) = 3.4 \times 250 \times (1 + 3.4 \times 9.43 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
MMD \approx 850 \times (1.032) \approx 877 \, \mu m \approx 88 \, \mu m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **88 μm MMD** lies within the optimal nasal deposition window (50–120 μm), confirming the **engineering validity** of typical pump designs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Dose Content Uniformity (USP <905> Acceptance Value)

The **Acceptance Value (AV)** is the regulatory metric KTU students must be able to compute.

### Step 1: Definition

$$
AV = \vert \bar{X} - T \vert + ks
$$

where:
- $\bar{X}$ = sample mean dose
- $T$ = target dose (label claim)
- $s$ = sample standard deviation
- $k$ = acceptability constant (depends on $n$; for $n=10$, $k=2.4$)

### Step 2: Worked Example

A nasal spray delivers 10 doses with the following weights (mg): 100.1, 99.8, 100.5, 99.7, 100.3, 100.0, 99.9, 100.2, 100.4, 99.6.

**Compute mean:**

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{100.1 + 99.8 + 100.5 + 99.7 + 100.3 + 100.0 + 99.9 + 100.2 + 100.4 + 99.6}{10} = \frac{1000.5}{10} = 100.05 \text{ mg}
$$

**Compute standard deviation:**

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}
$$

Squared deviations: 0.0025, 0.0625, 0.2025, 0.1225, 0.0625, 0.0025, 0.0225, 0.0225, 0.1225, 0.2025. Sum = 0.825.

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{0.825}{9}} = \sqrt{0.0917} = 0.303 \, mg
$$

**Compute AV (T = 100, k = 2.4 for n=10):**

$$
AV = \vert 100.05 - 100 \vert + 2.4 \times 0.303 = 0.05 + 0.727 = 0.777
$$

### Step 3: Regulatory Conclusion

$$
AV = 0.777 \le 15 \text{ (USP limit)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sample passes USP <905>.** In KTU board problems, students are expected to:
> 1. Compute $\bar{X}$ and $s$ explicitly (showing every squared deviation).
> 2. State the $k$ value used and why.
> 3. Conclude compliance or non-compliance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Droplet Size vs. Deposition Site

| Droplet Size (μm) | Aerodynamic Behavior | Deposition Zone in Nose | Therapeutic Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **> 150** | High inertia; ballistic | Vestibule / anterior turbinate | Local, surface-acting drugs |
| **50 – 120** | **Optimum impaction** | Middle / superior turbinate (respiratory) | **Most nasal drugs (steroids, antihistamines)** |
| **10 – 50** | Sedimentation-dominated | Posterior cavity / nasopharynx | Systemic peptide delivery |
| **< 5** | Diffusive / exhaled | **Mostly exhaled** | Inefficient — *avoid* |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Confusing "aerodynamic diameter" with "geometric diameter." For non-spherical particles, $d_{ae} = d_{g} \sqrt{\rho_p / \rho_0 \chi}$ where $\chi$ is the dynamic shape factor. Drops are typically *spherical* ($\chi \approx 1$) so $d_{ae} \approx d_g \sqrt{\rho_p}$.
