<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liposomes** are spherical, self-assembled, closed vesicular structures composed of one or more concentric **phospholipid bilayers** (lamellae) that enclose an equal number of aqueous compartments. They range in size from **20 nm to several micrometers** and serve as biocompatible carriers for the targeted and controlled delivery of hydrophilic, lipophilic, and amphiphilic drug molecules.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Pharmaceutical / Biomedical Engineering domain), liposomes are categorized under **"Vesicular Drug Delivery Systems"** — a subclass of **Particulate / Carrier-mediated Drug Delivery**. The structural backbone is the **phospholipid molecule**, which possesses a hydrophilic polar head group and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails, conferring **amphiphilic** character.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Soap Bubble Inside a Water Balloon":**
> Imagine a tiny water balloon 🎈. Now imagine the rubber of the balloon is not a single sheet, but a *double-layered film* — the inner surface loves water (hydrophilic) and the middle (between the two films) hates water (hydrophobic). If you drop a water-soluble drug inside the balloon's water core, and a fat-soluble drug into the double-film layer, **both** get carried safely through the bloodstream. The outer "film" also shields the drug from stomach acid, enzymes, and the body's immune surveillance — releasing the payload only at the target tissue. That double-film water balloon is essentially a **liposome**.

## 1.3 Historical Origin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discovery:** Liposomes were first described by British hematologist **Dr. Alec D. Bangham** in **1961** at the Babraham Institute, Cambridge, UK. Initially observed while studying phospholipids and blood clotting, Bangham noticed that phospholipids spontaneously formed closed bilayer structures when hydrated — laying the foundation for modern vesicular drug delivery.

## 1.4 Key Structural Metrics

- **Diameter range:** 20 nm – 10 µm (clinical liposomal products typically 50–500 nm)
- **Bilayer thickness:** approximately **3–4 nm**
- **Aqueous core volume:** proportional to the cube of the radius
- **Surface charge:** governed by the choice of phospholipid (e.g., neutral PC, anionic PA/PS, cationic DOTAP)

## 1.5 Salient Features of Liposomes

| Feature | Functional Significance |
|---|---|
| Biocompatible & biodegradable | Composed of naturally occurring lipids (phosphatidylcholine) → safe *in vivo* |
| Amphiphilic loading capacity | Hydrophilic drugs in aqueous core; lipophilic drugs in bilayer |
| Reduced drug toxicity | Encapsulation shields healthy tissues from cytotoxic drugs |
| Improved pharmacokinetics | Prolonged circulation time via PEGylation ("stealth" liposomes) |
| Targeted delivery | Surface conjugation with ligands (antibodies, peptides) |
| Versatile administration | IV, oral, ocular, pulmonary, transdermal, intranasal |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of Liposomes

A liposome is built from three primary chemical constituents, each contributing distinct physicochemical properties:

1. **Phospholipids (Main Structural Component)**
   - Most common: **Phosphatidylcholine (PC / Lecithin)**
   - Others: Phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), Phosphatidylserine (PS), Phosphatidylglycerol (PG), Phosphatidic acid (PA)
   - Synthetic alternatives: **DSPC** (1,2-Distearoyl-*sn*-glycero-3-phosphocholine), **DOPC**, **DPPC**
   - Cationic lipid for gene delivery: **DOTAP**, **DOTMA**

2. **Cholesterol (Membrane Stabilizer)**
   - Inserted into the bilayer at molar ratios of **20–50 mol %**
   - Function: modulates membrane fluidity, reduces permeability to water-soluble drugs, enhances *in vivo* stability.

3. **Charge-Modifying / Stealth Lipids**
   - Anionic: Phosphatidylglycerol, Dicetyl phosphate (DCP)
   - Cationic: Stearylamine, DOTAP
   - Steric stabilizer: **DSPE-PEG2000** (1,2-Distearoyl-*sn*-Glycero-3-Phosphoethanolamine-N-[Methoxy(Polyethylene glycol)-2000])

## 2.2 Why Liposomes Self-Assemble — The Thermodynamic Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The driving force for liposome formation is the **hydrophobic effect** — a thermodynamic phenomenon wherein non-polar tails of lipids aggregate to minimize their unfavorable contact with water. This is quantified by the concept of the **critical packing parameter (CPP)**.

### 2.2.1 The Critical Packing Parameter (CPP)

$$ \text{CPP} \;=\; \frac{v}{a_{0} \cdot l_{c}} $$

where:
- $v$ = volume of the hydrophobic tail group
- $a_{0}$ = optimal cross-sectional area of the head group at the lipid–water interface
- $l_{c}$ = critical length of the hydrophobic tail

> **Geometrical interpretation of CPP:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{CPP} < \tfrac{1}{2} &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Micelles (cone-shaped lipids)} \\[4pt]
\tfrac{1}{2} \le \text{CPP} \le 1 &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Bilayers / Liposomes (cylindrical lipids)} \\[4pt]
\text{CPP} > 1 &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Inverted Hexagonal Phase}
\end{aligned} $$

## 2.3 Encapsulation Efficiency (E%) and Drug Loading (DL%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two metrics are **high-yield KTU numerical questions**. Master them thoroughly.

$$ E\% \;=\; \frac{\text{Amount of drug encapsulated}}{\text{Total amount of drug added}} \times 100 $$

$$ DL\% \;=\; \frac{\text{Amount of drug encapsulated}}{\text{Total lipid mass} + \text{Drug mass}} \times 100 $$

> **Key Insight:** Lipophilic drugs have high DL% but variable E%; hydrophilic drugs have high E% but are released rapidly into the aqueous core.

## 2.4 Phase Transition Temperature (Tm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every phospholipid has a characteristic **gel-to-liquid crystalline phase transition temperature ($T_m$)**, below which the bilayer is rigid ("gel state") and above which it is fluid ("liquid-ordered" state).

$$ T_{m,\text{mix}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i} \, T_{m,i} $$

where $x_i$ is the mole fraction of the $i^{\text{th}}$ lipid component. This weighted-average equation is examined frequently in KTU derivations.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Critical Packing Parameter | $v / (a_0 \cdot l_c)$ | dimensionless | Predicts self-assembly geometry |
| 2 | Encapsulation Efficiency | $E\% = (\text{Encapsulated} / \text{Total}) \times 100$ | % | Drug loading performance |
| 3 | Drug Loading Capacity | $DL\% = (\text{Encapsulated} / \text{Total mass}) \times 100$ | % | Carrier capacity |
| 4 | Phase Transition (mixture) | $T_m = \sum x_i T_{m,i}$ | K or °C | Bilayer fluidity |
| 5 | Vesicle Surface Area | $A = 4 \pi r^2$ | nm² | Bilayer surface |
| 6 | Aqueous Core Volume | $V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | nm³ | Hydrophilic drug volume |
| 7 | Number of Lipid Molecules per Vesicle | $N_{\text{lipid}} = \dfrac{4 \pi (r + d)^2}{a_{\text{head}}}$ | dimensionless | Stoichiometric calculations |
| 8 | Zeta Potential threshold | $\vert \zeta \vert > 30$ mV | mV | Colloidal stability indicator |
| 9 | Partition Coefficient (log P) | $\log P = \log \dfrac{[C]_{\text{octanol}}}{[C]_{\text{water}}}$ | dimensionless | Predicts liposomal compartment |
| 10 | Release Kinetics (zero-order) | $Q_t = Q_0 + k_0 t$ | mass vs. time | Sustained release design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations are essential in pharmaceutical R&D labs for designing liposomal formulations of anticancer drugs (Doxil®, AmBisome®, DaunoXome®), mRNA vaccines (Pfizer-BioNTech, Moderna COVID-19 vaccines), and gene-therapy vectors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Encapsulation Efficiency from First Principles

> **Problem context:** A researcher prepares a liposomal formulation by hydrating a thin lipid film with **10 mg** of a water-soluble drug dissolved in **5 mL** of buffer. After extrusion and purification (Sephadex G-50 column), the eluted liposome fraction is lysed with Triton X-100, and the released drug is quantified by UV-Vis spectrophotometry at $\lambda_{\max} = 274$ nm. The absorbance corresponds to **6.8 mg** of drug. Compute $E\%$.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Walk-through

**Step 1 — Identify the system parameters.**

The total drug mass added during the hydration step is the **input** to the system. The drug mass recovered from inside the liposomes (after removing unentrapped free drug) is the **output**.

$$ M_{\text{total,added}} = 10 \text{ mg} $$

**Step 2 — Identify the entrapped drug mass.**

After column chromatography, only the liposome-encapsulated drug is collected. UV-Vis quantification gives the drug released upon lysis.

$$ M_{\text{entrapped}} = 6.8 \text{ mg} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the encapsulation efficiency definition.**

By definition, encapsulation efficiency is the ratio of entrapped drug to total drug added, expressed as a percentage.

$$ E\% \;=\; \frac{M_{\text{entrapped}}}{M_{\text{total,added}}} \times 100 $$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
E\% &= \frac{6.8 \text{ mg}}{10 \text{ mg}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.68 \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 68 \%
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

> A 68% E% indicates a moderately efficient formulation. For hydrophilic drugs, values of 60–80% are considered good; for hydrophilic drugs in small unilamellar vesicles (SUVs), values can drop to 20–30% due to limited aqueous core volume. To improve E%, a formulator would (i) increase the lipid-to-drug ratio, (ii) use the **reverse-phase evaporation** or **pH-gradient active-loading** method, or (iii) increase the number of bilayers (multilamellar vesicles, MLVs).

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Packing Parameter for Phosphatidylcholine

> **Problem context:** For 1,2-Dioleoyl-*sn*-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DOPC), the following parameters have been reported: $v = 1.063 \text{ nm}^3$, $a_0 = 0.72 \text{ nm}^2$, $l_c = 1.7 \text{ nm}$. Determine the predicted self-assembly geometry.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Computation

**Step 1 — Write the CPP formula.**

$$ \text{CPP} \;=\; \frac{v}{a_{0} \cdot l_{c}} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
a_{0} \cdot l_{c} &= 0.72 \text{ nm}^2 \times 1.7 \text{ nm} \\[4pt]
&= 1.224 \text{ nm}^3
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{CPP} &= \frac{1.063 \text{ nm}^3}{1.224 \text{ nm}^3} \\[4pt]
&= 0.868
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the geometric rule.**

$$ 0.5 \le \text{CPP} = 0.868 \le 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Bilayer (Liposome)} $$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

> Since CPP lies in the range $[0.5, 1]$, DOPC self-assembles into **bilayer structures** (liposomes / vesicles). This is consistent with the well-known experimental behavior of unsaturated phosphatidylcholines and is a high-yield KTU conceptual link.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Liposomes

| Category | Full Name | Size Range | Lamellarity | Drug-Compartment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SUV** | Small Unilamellar Vesicles | 20–100 nm | Unilamellar | Aqueous core |
| **LUV** | Large Unilamellar Vesicles | 100–400 nm | Unilamellar | Aqueous core (high volume) |
| **GUV** | Giant Unilamellar Vesicles | > 1 µm | Unilamellar | Aqueous core (very high) |
| **MLV** | Multilamellar Vesicles | 0.4–10 µm | Multilamellar (≥ 5 bilayers) | Aqueous core + multiple bilayers |
| **OLV** | Oligolamellar Vesicles | 0.1–1 µm | 2–5 bilayers | Aqueous + bilayers |
| **MVV** | Multivesicular Vesicles | 1–10 µm | Multiple internal vesicles | Nested aqueous cores |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "SLuG-MOM"**
> **S**UV → **Lu**V → **G**UV → **M**LV → **O**LV → **MVV**
> (Ascending size & complexity order — frequently asked in 3-mark questions.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic 1 — Cross-Section of a Liposome (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph bilayer["PHOSPHOLIPID BILAYER MEMBRANE"]
        direction TB
        HEAD1["HYDROPHILIC HEAD GROUP - OUTER LEAFLET"]
        TAIL1A["HYDROPHOBIC TAIL - OUTER"]
        TAIL1B["HYDROPHOBIC TAIL - INNER"]
        HEAD2["HYDROPHILIC HEAD GROUP - INNER LEAFLET"]
        CHOL["CHOLESTEROL MOLECULE - MEMBRANE STABILIZER"]
        HEAD1 --> TAIL1A
        TAIL1A --> TAIL1B
        TAIL1B --> HEAD2
        CHOL -.->|"intercalated"| TAIL1A
    end
    AQUEOUS_CORE["AQUEOUS CORE - HYDROPHILIC DRUG HOUSING"]
    BILAYER -->|"encloses"| AQUEOUS_CORE
    LIPOPHILIC_PAYLOAD["LIPOPHILIC DRUG - EMBEDDED IN BILAYER"]
    BILAYER -.->|"hosts"| LIPOPHILIC_PAYLOAD
    TARGETING_LIGAND["SURFACE-ATTACHED LIGAND - ANTIBODY OR PEPTIDE"]
    HEAD1 -.->|"conjugated"| TARGETING_LIGAND
```

## 4.2 Schematic 2 — Functional Block Architecture of Liposomal Drug Delivery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DRUG COMPOUND"] --> B{"PARTITION COEFFICIENT LOG P"}
    B -->|"log P less than 1 hydrophilic"| C["ENTRAPMENT IN AQUEOUS CORE"]
    B -->|"log P greater than 1 lipophilic"| D["ENTRAPMENT IN BILAYER"]
    B -->|"log P approx 1 amphiphilic"| E["INTERFACIAL LOADING"]
    C --> F["LIPOSOME FORMULATION"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["CHARACTERIZATION SIZE ZETA ENCAPSULATION"]
    G --> H["IN VIVO ADMINISTRATION"]
    H --> I["TARGETED RELEASE PASSIVE OR ACTIVE"]
    I --> J["THERAPEUTIC EFFECT"]
    F --> K["PEG COATING - STEALTH LIPOSOME"]
    K --> H
```

## 4.3 Schematic 3 — Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["LIPOSOME PRECURSOR LIPID FILM"] --> HYDRATE["HYDRATION STEP"]
    HYDRATE --> MLVraw["MLV - 0.4 to 10 MICRON"]
    MLVraw -->|"sonication"| SUV["SUV - 20 to 100 NM"]
    MLVraw -->|"extrusion through polycarbonate membrane"| LUV["LUV - 100 to 400 NM"]
    MLVraw -->|"electroformation or gentle swelling"| GUV["GUV - greater than 1 MICRON"]
    MLVraw -->|"reverse phase evaporation"| REV["REV LIPOSOMES"]
    MLVraw -->|"freeze thaw cycling"| FTV["FREEZE THAW VESICLES"]
    FTV -->|"extrusion"| DEEPCYC["DEHYDRATION RHEOLOGY VESICLES"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The cross-section schematic shows how a single lipid bilayer (with cholesterol embedded) creates a sandwich-like architecture that can host both water-soluble drugs (in the central aqueous core) and fat-soluble drugs (within the bilayer). The functional block diagram traces the engineering workflow from drug selection → partitioning logic → formulation → *in vivo* administration → therapeutic effect. The classification tree illustrates how different preparation methods (sonication, extrusion, electroformation) yield different liposome subtypes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
> **Define liposomes. Mention any two advantages of liposomal drug delivery systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Liposomes are spherical, self-closed vesicular carriers composed of one or more phospholipid bilayers enclosing an aqueous compartment. They are formed spontaneously when amphiphilic phospholipids (e.g., phosphatidylcholine) are hydrated in aqueous media.

> **Advantages (any two):**
> 1. **Dual-loading capacity** — both hydrophilic (in aqueous core) and lipophilic (in bilayer) drugs can be entrapped simultaneously.
> 2. **Biocompatibility and biodegradability** — phospholipids are natural body constituents, ensuring low immunogenicity and toxicity.
> 3. **Improved pharmacokinetics** — PEGylated ("stealth") liposomes evade the reticuloendothelial system, prolonging circulation half-life.
> 4. **Targeted delivery** — surface conjugation with ligands enables site-specific drug release.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> **Differentiate between MLVs, SUVs, and LUVs.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **MLV** (Multilamellar) | **SUV** (Small Unilamellar) | **LUV** (Large Unilamellar) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Size | 0.4 – 10 µm | 20 – 100 nm | 100 – 400 nm |
| Lamellarity | Multiple (≥ 5) bilayers | Single bilayer | Single bilayer |
| Aqueous volume | Low per compartment | Very low | High |
| Encapsulation efficiency | Moderate (for hydrophilic) | Low | High |
| Preparation | Thin-film hydration | Sonication of MLV | Extrusion / REV |
| Stability | Highest | Least (curvature strain) | Moderate |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice (a) OR (b)**. Below we provide both alternatives with full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Module-4 ESE)]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the structural components of liposomes, with emphasis on the role of phospholipids and cholesterol. Mention how the choice of lipid affects the **phase transition temperature (Tm)** and **encapsulation efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define the **Critical Packing Parameter (CPP)**. With a suitable diagram, explain how CPP governs the self-assembly geometry of amphiphilic lipids. Compute the CPP for a lipid with $v = 0.95 \text{ nm}^3$, $a_0 = 0.65 \text{ nm}^2$, $l_c = 1.5 \text{ nm}$, and predict the self-assembly structure. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Structural Components:**

**1. Phospholipids (e.g., Phosphatidylcholine — PC):**
Phospholipids are the principal bilayer-forming amphiphiles. Each molecule has:
- A **hydrophilic head** (phosphorylcholine group) — water-loving.
- Two **hydrophobic fatty acyl tails** — water-repelling.

When hydrated, the tails aggregate to escape water, and the heads face the aqueous phase → bilayer → liposome.

> **Effect of head-group charge:** Neutral PC → neutral liposomes; PG/PS → anionic; DOTAP → cationic (used in gene delivery).

**2. Cholesterol:**
Cholesterol inserts into the bilayer with its hydroxyl group near the phospholipid head and its steroid ring intercalated between the acyl tails. At **20–50 mol %**, it:
- Reduces bilayer permeability to water-soluble drugs.
- Abolishes the sharp Tm (broadens the phase transition).
- Modulates mechanical stability and rigidity.

**3. Effect on Tm:**

Saturated long-chain lipids (e.g., DSPC, C18) have **high Tm** (~55 °C) → rigid gel-state bilayers at body temperature → sustained release.
Unsaturated short-chain lipids (e.g., DOPC) have **low Tm** (~−20 °C) → fluid bilayers at body temperature → rapid release.

**4. Effect on Encapsulation Efficiency:**
- High Tm (rigid) → high E% for hydrophilic drugs (less leakage during storage).
- Low Tm (fluid) → better bilayer loading for lipophilic drugs.
- Cholesterol ↑ → more rigid, sustained release, stable E% during shelf life.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition of CPP:**
The Critical Packing Parameter is a dimensionless geometric descriptor defined by Israelachvili as:

$$ \text{CPP} \;=\; \frac{v}{a_{0} \cdot l_{c}} $$

> **Geometrical Rule:**

| CPP range | Self-Assembly Structure |
|---|---|
| $< 0.5$ | Spherical micelles (cone shape) |
| $0.5$ to $1.0$ | Bilayers / liposomes (cylinder) |
| $> 1.0$ | Inverted hexagonal (inverse cone) |

**Numerical Computation:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\text{CPP} &= \frac{v}{a_0 \cdot l_c} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.95 \text{ nm}^3}{(0.65 \text{ nm}^2)(1.5 \text{ nm})} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.95}{0.975} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.974
\end{aligned} $$

**Predicted Geometry:** Since $0.5 \le 0.974 \le 1.0$, the lipid self-assembles into a **bilayer (liposome)**.

> The CPP value of 0.974 is very close to 1, indicating a "near-inverse-cone" geometry — typical of saturated phosphatidylcholines that form robust, low-curvature vesicles.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Module-4 ESE)]**
> **(a)** Classify liposomes based on **size and lamellarity**. Prepare a comparative table and describe any **two methods of preparation** of liposomes in detail. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a detailed note on the **applications of liposomes** in drug delivery, citing at least **three marketed liposomal products** with their therapeutic indications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification of Liposomes (Size & Lamellarity):**
*(See Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3)*

| Type | Size | Lamellarity |
|---|---|---|
| SUV | 20–100 nm | Unilamellar |
| LUV | 100–400 nm | Unilamellar |
| GUV | > 1 µm | Unilamellar |
| OLV | 0.1–1 µm | 2–5 bilayers |
| MLV | 0.4–10 µm | ≥ 5 bilayers |
| MVV | 1–10 µm | Multiple internal vesicles |

**Method 1 — Thin-Film Hydration (Bangham Method):**
1. Dissolve phospholipid + cholesterol in a round-bottom flask using an organic solvent (chloroform:methanol, 2:1 v/v).
2. Evaporate the solvent under reduced pressure (rotary evaporator) → a thin dry lipid film coats the flask wall.
3. Hydrate the film with aqueous buffer (above Tm) → multilamellar vesicles (MLVs) form spontaneously.
4. Optionally downsize by sonication → SUVs, or by extrusion → LUVs.

**Method 2 — Reverse-Phase Evaporation (REV):**
1. Dissolve lipids in an organic solvent (diethyl ether or isopropyl ether).
2. Add aqueous phase → form water-in-oil (w/o) emulsion by sonication/bath.
3. Evaporate the organic solvent under reduced pressure → the inverted micelles collapse into a viscous gel, then into a colloidal dispersion of LUVs.
4. Advantage: high encapsulation efficiency for water-soluble drugs (40–80%).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Applications of Liposomes in Drug Delivery:**

> **1. Anticancer Therapy (Chemotherapy):**
> Liposomal doxorubicin (**Doxil®/Caelyx®**) encapsulates the cytotoxic drug in PEGylated stealth liposomes (~85 nm). It is approved for Kaposi's sarcoma, ovarian cancer, and multiple myeloma. PEGylation prolongs circulation time and exploits the **EPR effect (Enhanced Permeability and Retention)** — passive targeting of tumor vasculature.

> **2. Antifungal Therapy:**
> Liposomal amphotericin B (**AmBisome®**) is used for systemic fungal infections (e.g., cryptococcal meningitis in immunocompromised patients). Encapsulation dramatically reduces the nephrotoxicity of free amphotericin B while maintaining antifungal efficacy.

> **3. Vaccine Delivery (mRNA):**
> The Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna COVID-19 vaccines use **ionizable cationic lipid nanoparticles (LNPs)** — a structural cousin of liposomes — to deliver mRNA encoding the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein. This application revolutionized the pharmaceutical industry post-2020.

> **4. Additional Applications:**
> - **Ocular delivery** (e.g., liposomal cyclosporine) for dry-eye disease.
> - **Pulmonary delivery** of liposomal ciprofloxacin for cystic fibrosis lung infections.
> - **Gene therapy** using cationic liposomes (lipoplexes) for DNA/siRNA delivery.
> - **Transdermal delivery** via deformable (elastic) liposomes — Transfersomes®.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Definition accuracy:** Writing "liposomes are fat bubbles" or "lipid-coated drug particles" is **wrong** — they are *closed bilayer vesicles* with a distinct aqueous core. Lose up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing MLV with SUV preparation:** Students often claim SUVs form directly by hydration. **Incorrect.** SUVs are *always* derived from MLVs via sonication/extrusion. Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **CPP computation error:** Confusing $a_0$ (nm²) with $a_0 \cdot l_c$ (nm³). The denominator carries units of **volume**, not area. Lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting cholesterol's role:** Cholesterol is *not* a bilayer former — it is a **modulator of fluidity and stability**. Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing marketed-product names:** Always cite at least one commercial product (Doxil®, AmBisome®, etc.) for full marks on application questions.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Introduction to Liposomes"**

- ☐ **Definition:** Spherical vesicles of one or more phospholipid bilayers enclosing aqueous core(s).
- ☐ **Discoverer:** **Alec D. Bangham, 1961** (Babraham Institute, UK).
- ☐ **Building blocks:** Phospholipid (PC) + **Cholesterol (20–50 mol %)** + optional charge/PEG lipid.
- ☐ **Driving force of self-assembly:** **Hydrophobic effect** + van der Waals interactions between tails.
- ☐ **CPP rule:** $< 0.5$ → micelle; $0.5$–$1.0$ → **bilayer/liposome**; $> 1$ → inverted hexagonal.
- ☐ **Classification (SLuG-MOM):** SUV → LUV → GUV → OLV → MLV → MVV.
- ☐ **E% formula:** $E\% = (M_{\text{entrapped}} / M_{\text{total}}) \times 100$.
- ☐ **DL% formula:** $DL\% = (M_{\text{entrapped}} / (M_{\text{lipid}} + M_{\text{drug}})) \times 100$.
- ☐ **Phase transition (Tm):** Mixture rule $T_m = \sum x_i T_{m,i}$.
- ☐ **Drug loading logic:** Hydrophilic → aqueous core; Lipophilic → bilayer; Amphiphilic → interface.
- ☐ **Methods:** Thin-film hydration (MLVs), sonication (SUVs), extrusion (LUVs), REV (high E% for hydrophilic).
- ☐ **Stealth liposomes:** PEGylated → evade RES → prolonged circulation.
- ☐ **EPR effect:** Basis of passive tumor targeting by nanoliposomes.
- ☐ **Marketed products:** Doxil® (cancer), AmBisome® (fungal), DaunoXome® (cancer), Visudyne® (PDT), Onpattro® (RNAi), mRNA-LNP vaccines.
- ☐ **Stability indicator:** $\vert \zeta \text{-potential} \vert > 30$ mV.
- ☐ **KTU high-yield keywords:** amphiphilicity, bilayer curvature, liposomal doxorubicin, PEGylation, active vs. passive targeting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Niosomes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBB862 - DRUG DELIVERY PRINCIPLE & APPLICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Niosomes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Niosomes (Non-ionic Surfactant Vesicles):** Niosomes are bilayered, spherical, lamellar (multilamellar/unilamellar) vesicular nanostructures composed primarily of **non-ionic surfactants** and **cholesterol** (or its derivatives) that self-assemble in aqueous media to encapsulate both **hydrophilic** (water-soluble) and **lipophilic** (fat-soluble) drug molecules for controlled and targeted drug delivery. Chemically, niosomes are an advanced category of **vesicular drug delivery systems** that closely resemble **liposomes** in their structural morphology, but are synthesized using synthetic, non-phospholipid amphiphiles. They consist of an aqueous core trapped within one or more concentric bilayers formed by hydrated surfactant molecules. **Key Structural Components:** - **Surfactant** (e.g., Span 40, Span 60, Tween 20, Tween 80) – forms the bilayer membrane - **Cholesterol** – imparts rigidity, stability, and entrapment efficiency - **Charge inducers** (e.g., dicetyl phosphate, stearylamine) – provides surface charge - **Hydration medium** (aqueous phase, usually phosphate buffer pH 7.4) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Soap Bubble Inside a Soap Bubble** > > Imagine you are blowing a **giant soap bubble** in the air. Now imagine wrapping that bubble inside **another bubble**, and then another. The thin film (the bubble wall) is made of soap molecules that have a "water-loving" head facing inward (toward the water in the center) and a "water-fearing" tail facing outward. Now, drop a small amount of oil in the middle — the oil gets trapped inside the aqueous center, while water-soluble stuff sits in the wall layers. > > **Niosomes work exactly like this!** The surfactant molecules are the "soap," cholesterol makes the bubble wall stiffer, and the trapped water bubble can carry both fat-soluble and water-soluble drugs simultaneously. They are essentially **artificial, drug-carrying nanobubbles** made of synthetic soap instead of natural phospholipids (like liposomes use). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Vesicle Size Range:** 10 nm to 3000 nm (typically 100–500 nm) - **Bilayer Thickness:** ~5–7 nm per bilayer - **Cholesterol-to-Surfactant Molar Ratio (CSMR):** Typically 1:1 to 1:2 (optimal for entrapment) - **Hydration Temperature:** Above the **Phase Transition Temperature (Tc)** of the surfactant - **Critical Packing Parameter (CPP):** 0.5 to 1.0 (required for vesicle formation) ### 1.4 Historical Context First reported by **Vanlerberghe and Handjani-Vila in 1972** (L'Oréal, France), originally developed for cosmetic applications before being adapted for pharmaceutical drug delivery in the late 1970s and 1980s. --- > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Niosomes are a high-yield topic frequently asked in **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition-based question and in **Part B (14 marks)** as comparative analytical questions (often paired with liposomes, ethosomes, or transferosomes). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Architecture of Niosomes The bilayer of a niosome is formed by the **self-assembly of amphiphilic non-ionic surfactants** in aqueous media. The orientation of these molecules is driven by the **hydrophobic effect**. - The **hydrophilic head groups** (e.g., –OH, –O–) orient toward the aqueous phase (both inside and outside). - The **hydrophobic alkyl chains** (tails) orient inward, facing each other, forming the **hydrophobic core of the bilayer**. > [!NOTE] > **Why Bilayer and Not Micelle?** > > This depends on the **Critical Packing Parameter (CPP)**, also called the **Surfactant Number** or **Israelachvili's Packing Parameter**, given by: > > $$CPP = \frac{v}{l_c \cdot a_0}$$ > > Where: > - $v$ = volume of the hydrophobic tail > - $l_c$ = critical length of the hydrophobic tail > - $a_0$ = cross-sectional area of the hydrophilic head group > > - If **CPP < 0.5** → spherical micelles > - If **0.5 ≤ CPP ≤ 1.0** → **bilayer vesicles (niosomes form here)** > - If **CPP > 1.0** → inverted micelles > > For vesicle (niosome) formation, the surfactants used (Span, Tween class) have CPP values typically between **0.5 and 1.0**, which is why they form bilayers and not micelles. ### 2.2 Role of Cholesterol in Niosomes Cholesterol is a **rigid, planar, lipophilic steroid** that intercalates between the surfactant molecules in the bilayer. Its roles include: - **Increases membrane rigidity** by filling gaps between surfactant monomers - **Reduces permeability** of entrapped drug (decreases leakage) - **Improves entrapment efficiency** for hydrophilic drugs - **Stabilizes the bilayer** against pH and temperature fluctuations - **Modulates the phase transition temperature (Tc)** — usually increases it > [!IMPORTANT] > **Excess cholesterol** (>50 mol%) disrupts the bilayer packing and **decreases** entrapment efficiency. The optimal molar ratio must be determined experimentally. ### 2.3 Types of Niosomes Based on **vesicle size and number of bilayers**, niosomes are classified as: | Type | Full Name | Size Range | Bilayers | |------|-----------|------------|----------| | **MLV** | Multilamellar Large Vesicles | 400 nm – 3500 nm | Multiple concentric | | **LUV** | Large Unilamellar Vesicles | 200 nm – 400 nm | Single bilayer | | **SUV** | Small Unilamellar Vesicles | 20 nm – 100 nm | Single bilayer | | **GUV** | Giant Unilamellar Vesicles | >1000 nm | Single bilayer | ### 2.4 Comparison: Niosomes vs Liposomes | Parameter | Niosomes | Liposomes | |-----------|----------|-----------| | Main component | Non-ionic surfactants (synthetic) | Phospholipids (natural, e.g., lecithin) | | Stability | **High** (chemical + storage) | Low (oxidation of phospholipids) | | Cost | **Low** (synthetic, scalable) | High (purification of natural lipids) | | Toxicity | **Low** | Variable (phospholipid impurities) | | Shelf-life | **Long** (months to years) | Short (weeks) | | Reproducibility | **High** | Lower | | Immunogenicity | Low | Can be higher | | Encapsulation efficiency | Moderate to high | High | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question compares niosomes with liposomes, emphasize **stability, cost, and synthetic reproducibility** as the three key advantages of niosomes. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes the **high-yield mathematical and empirical relationships** for niosome characterization — frequently tested in numerical and short-answer questions. | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Purpose | |-----------|---------------------|-------|---------| | **Critical Packing Parameter (CPP)** | $CPP = \dfrac{v}{l_c \cdot a_0}$ | Dimensionless | Predicts vesicle formation | | **Entrapment Efficiency (%EE)** | $\%EE = \dfrac{D_{entrapped}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Drug loading capacity | | **Drug Loading Capacity (%DL)** | $\%DL = \dfrac{D_{entrapped}}{W_{lipid}} \times 100$ | % | Drug-to-lipid mass ratio | | **Zeta Potential (ζ)** | $\zeta = \dfrac{4\pi \eta v}{\varepsilon}$ | mV | Surface charge & stability | | **Vesicle Size (Hydrodynamic, $D_h$)** | $D_h = \dfrac{k_B T}{3\pi \eta D}$ | nm | Size by DLS | | **Release Kinetics (Zero Order)** | $Q_t = Q_0 + k_0 t$ | Concentration vs time | Constant release | | **Release Kinetics (Higuchi)** | $Q_t = k_H \sqrt{t}$ | Cumulative release | Diffusion-controlled | | **Release Kinetics (First Order)** | $\ln Q_t = \ln Q_0 - k_1 t$ | log concentration vs time | Concentration-dependent release | | **Permeability Coefficient (P)** | $P = \dfrac{J}{\Delta C}$ | cm/s | Membrane permeability | | **Phase Transition Temp (Tc)** | Determined by DSC | °C | Bilayer fluidity | | **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** | $PDI = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{(D)^2}$ | Dimensionless | Size distribution width | | **Surface Area per Lipid ($a_0$)** | Determined by Langmuir trough | nm² / molecule | Critical for CPP | > **Note on units:** $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s), $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant (1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K), $\eta$ = viscosity (Pa·s), $\varepsilon$ = dielectric constant, $v$ = electrophoretic mobility (m²/V·s). ### 2.6 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications Niosomes have been exploited in pharmaceutical engineering for: - **Targeted cancer therapy** (doxorubicin, methotrexate loaded niosomes) - **Ocular drug delivery** (timolol, acetazolamide — sustained release in eye) - **Transdermal delivery** (enhanced skin penetration for antifungal/anti-inflammatory) - **Gene delivery** (siRNA and DNA complexation) - **Vaccine adjuvant systems** (antigen encapsulation) - **Cosmeceuticals** (anti-aging, sunscreen encapsulation) - **Oral peptide delivery** (protection from GI enzymes) - **Brain-targeted delivery** (via surface functionalization with lactoferrin/Tf) > [!TIP] > **Why are niosomes "engineered" vesicles?** Unlike natural lipid vesicles, the synthetic surfactant composition can be **finely tuned** (alkyl chain length, HLB value, CPP) to achieve desired release rates, size, and surface properties — making them a true **engineered drug delivery platform**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Packing Parameter (CPP) and Its Application The CPP is derived from geometric packing considerations of amphiphiles at an air-water interface. We start from the fundamental geometry of a single amphiphile: **Step 1 — Define the geometric parameters of a single amphiphile:** Let: - $v$ = volume occupied by the hydrocarbon tail(s) (nm³) - $l_c$ = maximum effective length of the hydrocarbon tail in its fully extended conformation (nm) - $a_0$ = optimal cross-sectional area per molecule at the hydrocarbon-water interface (nm²) **Step 2 — Establish the geometric constraint for bilayer formation:** For an amphiphile to form a **bilayer vesicle**, the hydrophilic head area and the hydrophobic tail volume must be balanced such that the molecule is "wedge-shaped" with a head area approximately equal to the tail cross-section projected onto a plane. The maximum volume of the hydrophobic core that can be packed without exposing chains to water is: $$V_{core} = v \cdot N$$ where $N$ = number of amphiphiles in one leaflet. **Step 3 — Derive the critical packing parameter:** The total cross-sectional area of one leaflet = $N \cdot a_0$. The bilayer must close upon itself to form a closed vesicle (to avoid exposure of hydrophobic tails to water). The geometric condition for closure is: $$v \leq l_c \cdot a_0$$ Rearranging to define the critical packing parameter: $$CPP = \frac{v}{l_c \cdot a_0}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the CPP rule:** - If $CPP < 1/3$ → the molecule is cone-shaped → forms **spherical micelles** - If $1/3 \leq CPP < 1/2$ → forms **rod-like / cylindrical micelles** - If $1/2 \leq CPP \leq 1$ → forms **bilayers (planar or vesicles)** ← **Niosomes** - If $CPP > 1$ → forms **inverted (reverse) micelles** > [!NOTE] > **For vesicle (niosome) formation, the absolute mathematical condition is:** > > $$\frac{1}{2} \leq CPP = \frac{v}{l_c \cdot a_0} \leq 1$$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:** Consider **Span 60 (sorbitan monostearate)**, a common niosome-forming surfactant. Given: - Molecular formula: C₂₄H₄₆O₆ - $v \approx 0.46$ nm³ (hydrophobic tail volume) - $l_c \approx 1.7$ nm (C16 chain length) - $a_0 \approx 0.45$ nm² Calculate CPP: $$CPP = \frac{0.46}{1.7 \times 0.45} = \frac{0.46}{0.765} \approx 0.601$$ **Interpretation:** Since $0.5 \leq 0.601 \leq 1.0$, Span 60 lies in the **vesicle-forming region** — confirming that it is suitable for niosome formation. ✓ ### 3.2 Derivation: Entrapment Efficiency (%EE) and Drug Loading (%DL) These are the **two most frequently asked** niosome parameters in KTU examinations. **Step 1 — Definitions:** - $D_{total}$ = total amount of drug added during preparation (mg) - $D_{free}$ = amount of un-entrapped (free) drug recovered in the supernatant after separation (mg) - $D_{entrapped} = D_{total} - D_{free}$ (mg) - $W_{lipid}$ = total weight of lipid/surfactant used in the formulation (mg) **Step 2 — Entrapment Efficiency formula:** $$\%EE = \left(\frac{D_{entrapped}}{D_{total}}\right) \times 100$$ $$\%EE = \left(\frac{D_{total} - D_{free}}{D_{total}}\right) \times 100$$ **Step 3 — Drug Loading Capacity formula:** $$\%DL = \left(\frac{D_{entrapped}}{W_{lipid}}\right) \times 100$$ **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:** **Problem:** A niosomal formulation is prepared using 50 mg of drug and 200 mg of Span 60:Cholesterol (1:1). After ultracentrifugation, the supernatant contained 12 mg of free (un-entrapped) drug. Calculate %EE and %DL. **Solution:** Given: - $D_{total} = 50$ mg - $D_{free} = 12$ mg - $W_{lipid} = 200$ mg Calculate $D_{entrapped}$: $$D_{entrapped} = 50 - 12 = 38 \text{ mg}$$ Calculate %EE: $$\%EE = \left(\frac{38}{50}\right) \times 100 = 76\%$$ Calculate %DL: $$\%DL = \left(\frac{38}{200}\right) \times 100 = 19\%$$ **Interpretation:** The formulation has a **76% entrapment efficiency** and **19% drug loading** — these are considered **acceptable values** for a topical/transdermal niosomal formulation. ### 3.3 Derivation: Higuchi Release Kinetics from Niosomes Niosomes are diffusion-controlled reservoir systems. Drug release from a niosomal bilayer follows the **Higuchi square-root-of-time equation**. **Step 1 — Higuchi's assumptions:** - The drug is uniformly dispersed in the bilayer (or matrix) - The system is a planar geometry (or pseudo-planar, valid for spherical vesicles when $\sqrt{t}$ approximation holds) - Sink conditions are maintained in the release medium - Drug diffusivity $D$ is constant **Step 2 — Fick's Second Law of Diffusion:** $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 3 — Apply pseudo-steady-state (Higuchi) approximation:** The cumulative amount of drug released per unit area at time $t$ is: $$Q_t = A \cdot \sqrt{D \cdot C_s \cdot \epsilon \cdot t}$$ Simplifying the constants into a single Higuchi rate constant $k_H$: $$Q_t = k_H \cdot \sqrt{t}$$ where: - $k_H$ = Higuchi dissolution constant (mg·cm⁻²·min⁻½) - $C_s$ = drug solubility in the matrix - $\epsilon$ = porosity of the matrix - $A$ = surface area **Step 4 — Worked Example (Cumulative Drug Release):** **Problem:** A niosomal gel shows Higuchi release kinetics with $k_H = 8.5$ mg/cm²·h^½. Calculate the cumulative drug released at $t = 4$ hours. **Solution:** $$Q_{4h} = 8.5 \times \sqrt{4} = 8.5 \times 2 = 17 \text{ mg/cm}^2$$ **At t = 9 h:** $$Q_{9h} = 8.5 \times \sqrt{9} = 8.5 \times 3 = 25.5 \text{ mg/cm}^2$$ **Interpretation:** The cumulative release increases as $\sqrt{t}$ — characteristic of **diffusion-controlled Fickian transport** through a niosomal bilayer. A plot of $Q_t$ vs $\sqrt{t}$ yields a straight line, with slope = $k_H$ (used to confirm Higuchi kinetics experimentally). ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Method Selection for Niosome Preparation | Method | Resulting Vesicle Type | Size Range | Drug Solubility Preference | Cost | |--------|------------------------|------------|---------------------------|------| | **Thin-Film Hydration (TFH)** | MLV | 400–3500 nm | Both (mostly lipophilic) | Low | | **Sonication** | SUV | 20–100 nm | Hydrophilic | Low | | **Extrusion (through polycarbonate membrane)** | LUV | 200–400 nm | Both | Medium | | **Reverse-Phase Evaporation (REV)** | LUV | 200–1000 nm | Hydrophilic | Medium | | **Ether Injection** | SUV / LUV | 50–500 nm | Both | Low | | **Microfluidization** | SUV / LUV | 50–200 nm | Both | High | | **Transmembrane pH Gradient** | LUV (high EE for weak acids) | 200–400 nm | Weak acids/bases | Medium | | **Supercritical CO₂** | SUV | 50–200 nm | Both | High (industrial) | | **Bubble Method** | LUV | 100–500 nm | Hydrophilic | Low (no organic solvent) | | **Heating Method** | MLV → SUV | 100–500 nm | Both | Very low (green) | | **Mozafari Method** | MLV / LUV | 100–800 nm | Both | Low (no organic solvent) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For hydrophilic drugs (e.g., doxorubicin, 5-FU):** Use **Reverse-Phase Evaporation (REV)** — gives the highest aqueous entrapment. > > **For lipophilic drugs (e.g., retinoic acid, cyclosporine):** Use **Thin-Film Hydration (TFH)** — favors partitioning into the bilayer. > > **For green/industrial scale-up:** Use **Mozafari method** or **heating method** — eliminates toxic organic solvents. ### 3.5 Characterization Techniques Matrix | Property Measured | Technique Used | KTU Point of Note | |-------------------|----------------|-------------------| | Vesicle size & PDI | Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS / Photon Correlation Spectroscopy) | Most common | | Zeta potential | Electrophoretic Light Scattering (ELS) / Laser Doppler Anemometry | > ±30 mV = stable | | Morphology | Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), SEM, AFM | TEM with negative staining | | Entrapment efficiency | Ultracentrifugation + UV-Vis / HPLC | Most asked calculation | | Drug release | Franz diffusion cell, dialysis bag method | Higuchi model fitting | | Phase transition temp | Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) | Bilayer integrity marker | | Bilayer integrity | ³¹P-NMR, FTIR | Chemical stability | | In vitro stability | Turbidity measurement, particle sizing over time | Shelf-life marker | | Drug-excipient interaction | DSC, FTIR, XRD | Confirms no incompatibility | | Lamellarity | ³¹P-NMR, Cryo-TEM, Small-Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS) | MLV vs LUV determination | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Architecture of a Niosome — Detailed Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A["AQUEOUS CORE
Hydrophilic drug dissolved
Buffer pH 7.4 / Saline"] B["INNER HYDROPHILIC HEAD REGION
O and OH groups face aqueous core
Nonionic surfactant polar heads"] C["BILAYER MEMBRANE LAYER 1
Hydrophobic tails C16 to C18
Span 60 Span 80 Tween class
Cholesterol intercalated for rigidity"] D["INTERBILAYER WATER LAYER
Between concentric lamellae
Traps additional hydrophilic drug"] E["BILAYER MEMBRANE LAYER 2
Second hydrophobic core
Same surfactant plus cholesterol"] F["OUTER HYDROPHILIC HEAD REGION
Polar heads face external aqueous phase
Surface charge inducer optional"] G["EXTERNAL AQUEOUS MEDIUM
Biological fluid or PBS pH 7.4
Dispersion medium"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > The above is a **cross-sectional functional architecture** of a **multilamellar niosome (MLV)**, showing the alternating hydrophilic–hydrophobic–hydrophilic sandwich structure responsible for dual (hydrophilic + lipophilic) drug entrapment. ### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram: Niosome vs Liposome vs Ethosome ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph N["Niosome System"] N1["Nonionic surfactant Span 60"] N2["Cholesterol"] N3["Phosphate buffer pH 7.4"] N1 --> N4["Synthetic bilayer vesicle"] N2 --> N4 N3 --> N4 end subgraph L["Liposome System"] L1["Phospholipid lecithin"] L2["Cholesterol"] L3["Phosphate buffer pH 7.4"] L1 --> L4["Natural bilayer vesicle"] L2 --> L4 L3 --> L4 end subgraph E["Ethosome System"] E1["Phospholipid lecithin"] E2["Ethanol 20 to 40 percent"] E3["Water or buffer"] E1 --> E4["Fluidized bilayer vesicle"] E2 --> E4 E3 --> E4 end N4 --> CMP["Comparative Features"] L4 --> CMP E4 --> CMP CMP --> R1["Stability Niosome highest"] CMP --> R2["Skin penetration Ethosome highest"] CMP --> R3["Cost Niosome lowest"] CMP --> R4["Scalability Niosome best"] ``` ### 4.3 Process Flowchart: Preparation of Niosomes by Thin-Film Hydration Method ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1 Dissolve surfactant plus cholesterol
plus drug in organic solvent
Round bottom flask"] S2["Step 2 Rotary evaporation at 60 C
Reduced pressure 200 to 400 mmHg"] S3["Step 3 Thin dry lipid film forms
on inner wall of flask"] S4["Step 4 Hydrate with aqueous buffer pH 7.4
Above phase transition temperature Tc"] S5["Step 5 Gentle swirling for 2 hours
MLV dispersion forms"] S6["Step 6 Optional sonication
to reduce size to SUV"] S7["Step 7 Optional extrusion
through polycarbonate membrane
for LUV"] S8["Step 8 Final niosomal dispersion
characterize and store"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 S5 --> S8 ``` ### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram: Drug Release Pathways from a Niosome ```mermaid flowchart TB VES["Niosome Vesicle Bilayer"] P1["Pathway 1 Diffusion through bilayer
Fickian Higuchi kinetics"] P2["Pathway 2 Bilayer erosion or degradation
pH or enzyme triggered"] P3["Pathway 3 Surfactant exchange with
cell membrane components"] P4["Pathway 4 Endocytosis by target cell
intracellular release"] VES --> P1 VES --> P2 VES --> P3 VES --> P4 P1 --> SITE["Drug reaches site of action"] P2 --> SITE P3 --> SITE P4 --> SITE ``` ### 4.5 Decision Tree: Choice of Niosome Type Based on Drug and Route ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Drug is hydrophilic
or lipophilic"} Q1 -->|Hydrophilic| Q2{"Route of administration"} Q1 -->|Lipophilic| Q3{"Route of administration"} Q2 -->|Parenteral| A1["LUV by REV method"] Q2 -->|Ocular| A2["SUV by sonication"] Q2 -->|Oral| A3["MLV by TFH with enteric coat"] Q3 -->|Transdermal| B1["MLV by TFH with penetration enhancer"] Q3 -->|Topical cosmetic| B2["LUV by extrusion"] Q3 -->|Pulmonary| B3["SUV by microfluidization for aerosol"] ``` > [!TIP] > The above decision tree should be **memorized as a flowchart** for the **Part A and Part B viva** — examiners frequently test your ability to choose the right niosome type for a given drug and route. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBB862]** **Q1.** Define niosomes. List any two advantages of niosomes over liposomes. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (2 marks):** > Niosomes are non-ionic surfactant-based vesicular drug delivery systems consisting of one or more concentric bilayers formed by self-assembly of non-ionic surfactants (e.g., Span 60, Tween 80) and cholesterol in aqueous media. They are capable of encapsulating both hydrophilic and lipophilic drugs within their aqueous core and lipid bilayer, respectively. > > **Two advantages over liposomes (1 mark):** > 1. **Greater chemical stability** — non-ionic surfactants resist oxidation and hydrolysis, unlike phospholipids. > 2. **Lower cost and easier scale-up** — synthetic surfactants are cheaper and more reproducible than purified natural phospholipids. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBB862]** **Q2.** What is the role of cholesterol in the niosomal bilayer? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Cholesterol is an essential component of the niosomal bilayer and performs the following key functions: 1. **Increases membrane rigidity** by filling voids between surfactant monomers, decreasing fluidity. 2. **Reduces drug leakage** from the vesicles, improving entrapment efficiency. 3. **Modulates the phase transition temperature (Tc)**, increasing it for surfactants with low Tc. 4. **Improves physical stability** of niosomes during storage and in biological fluids. The optimal **cholesterol-to-surfactant molar ratio** is typically **1:1 to 1:2**; excess cholesterol disrupts bilayer packing and reduces entrapment. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBB862 — Module 4 Choice]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Describe the structural components, types, and methods of preparation of niosomes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare niosomes with liposomes in terms of composition, stability, cost, encapsulation efficiency, and applications. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Structural Components, Types & Methods of Preparation (7 marks):** **Structural Components (2 marks):** - **Non-ionic surfactant** (e.g., Span 40, Span 60, Tween 20, Tween 80) – forms the bilayer - **Cholesterol** – imparts rigidity and stability - **Charge inducers** (dicetyl phosphate for negative charge, stearylamine for positive charge) - **Hydration medium** – phosphate buffer pH 7.4 or normal saline **Types of Niosomes (2 marks):** | Type | Size | |------|------| | MLV – Multilamellar Large Vesicles | 400–3500 nm | | LUV – Large Unilamellar Vesicles | 200–400 nm | | SUV – Small Unilamellar Vesicles | 20–100 nm | | GUV – Giant Unilamellar Vesicles | >1000 nm | **Methods of Preparation (3 marks):** 1. **Thin-Film Hydration (TFH):** Surfactant + cholesterol + drug dissolved in organic solvent → rotary evaporation → thin lipid film → hydrated with aqueous buffer above Tc → MLVs formed. 2. **Sonication:** MLVs are probe or bath sonicated to yield SUVs (20–100 nm). 3. **Extrusion:** MLVs passed through polycarbonate membranes (100–800 nm pore size) under nitrogen pressure to form uniformly sized LUVs. 4. **Reverse-Phase Evaporation (REV):** Surfactant dissolved in ether/chloroform → aqueous phase added → sonicated to form inverted micelles → solvent evaporated → LUVs with high aqueous entrapment form. 5. **Ether Injection:** Surfactant dissolved in ether → slowly injected into hot aqueous phase → ether evaporates → SUVs form. 6. **Bubble Method / Heating Method / Mozafari Method:** Green, solvent-free methods suitable for industrial scale-up. --- **(b) Comparison of Niosomes vs Liposomes (7 marks):** | Parameter | Niosomes | Liposomes | |-----------|----------|-----------| | **Composition** | Non-ionic surfactants (Span, Tween) + cholesterol | Phospholipids (e.g., lecithin) + cholesterol | | **Stability** | High; resistant to oxidation | Low; phospholipids undergo oxidation | | **Cost** | Low; synthetic, scalable | High; requires purified natural lipids | | **Toxicity** | Low | Variable; lipid impurities may cause toxicity | | **Encapsulation Efficiency** | High for both hydrophilic & lipophilic | High, but more variable | | **Reproducibility** | High (synthetic) | Lower (batch variation in natural lipids) | | **Shelf-life** | Long (months–years) | Short (weeks) | | **Applications** | Cosmetics, transdermal, ocular, oral, gene delivery | Cancer therapy, vaccines, IV delivery | | **Storage conditions** | Ambient or refrigerated | Strict cold chain required | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Niosomes are increasingly preferred over liposomes for pharmaceutical formulations due to their superior stability, cost-effectiveness, and tunable physicochemical properties. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the concept of the Critical Packing Parameter (CPP). Derive its mathematical expression and discuss its importance in predicting niosome formation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the applications of niosomes in (i) ocular drug delivery, (ii) transdermal drug delivery, and (iii) cancer chemotherapy. Mention one marketed/patented formulation in each. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Critical Packing Parameter (7 marks):** **Concept (2 marks):** The Critical Packing Parameter (CPP), introduced by Israelachvili, is a dimensionless geometric parameter that predicts the **preferred self-assembled structure** formed by an amphiphile in aqueous solution. For niosome formation, the surfactant must adopt a "wedge-shaped" geometry conducive to bilayer curvature. **Derivation (3 marks):** For an amphiphile at the hydrocarbon-water interface: - $v$ = volume of the hydrophobic tail (nm³) - $l_c$ = critical length of the hydrophobic tail (nm) - $a_0$ = optimal cross-sectional area of the hydrophilic head (nm²) The geometric condition for bilayer closure without exposing hydrophobic tails to water is: $$v \leq l_c \cdot a_0$$ Rearranging gives the CPP: $$CPP = \frac{v}{l_c \cdot a_0}$$ **Prediction Rules (2 marks):** | CPP Value | Structure Formed | |-----------|------------------| | < 0.5 | Spherical / rod-like micelles | | 0.5 – 1.0 | **Bilayers → Niosomes (vesicles)** | | > 1.0 | Inverted (reverse) micelles | **Numerical Example:** For Span 60, $v = 0.46$ nm³, $l_c = 1.7$ nm, $a_0 = 0.45$ nm²: $$CPP = \frac{0.46}{1.7 \times 0.45} = 0.601 \Rightarrow \text{Vesicle (niosome) form} \checkmark$$ --- **(b) Applications of Niosomes (7 marks):** **(i) Ocular Drug Delivery (2.5 marks):** - Niosomes provide **prolonged precorneal residence time** and **sustained drug release**, reducing dosing frequency. - They improve **bioavailability** of poorly absorbed drugs (e.g., acetazolamide, timolol maleate) by enhancing corneal penetration. - *Example formulation:* **Timolol maleate niosomal eye drops** — patented for glaucoma management with once-daily dosing (vs. twice-daily conventional drops). **(ii) Transdermal Drug Delivery (2.5 marks):** - Niosomes act as **penetration enhancers** by disrupting stratum corneum lipid structure and delivering drug into deeper skin layers. - They provide a **reservoir effect** in the skin, enabling sustained drug release for up to 24–48 hours. - *Example formulation:* **Niosomal gel of ketoconazole / diclofenac** for topical antifungal/anti-inflammatory therapy. **(iii) Cancer Chemotherapy (2 marks):** - Niosomes enable **passive tumor targeting** via the EPR (Enhanced Permeability and Retention) effect due to their nanoscale size. - They reduce the **systemic toxicity** of chemotherapeutic agents (e.g., doxorubicin, methotrexate) by selective accumulation at tumor sites. - Surface functionalization (e.g., folate, transferrin) enables **active targeting** of cancer cells. - *Example formulation:* **Doxorubicin-loaded niosomes** with PEGylated surface for prolonged circulation and tumor targeting. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Niosome Questions (KTU Board Pattern):** > > 1. **Confusing "non-ionic surfactant" with "anionic/cationic surfactant":** Niosomes **must** use non-ionic surfactants (Span, Tween class). Mentioning SDS, CTAB, or lecithin **loses 1 mark**. > > 2. **Forgetting cholesterol's role:** Any structural question on niosomes without mentioning cholesterol and its rigidizing effect **loses 1–2 marks**. > > 3. **Wrong temperature in TFH:** Hydration **must be above the phase transition temperature (Tc)** of the surfactant. Writing "at room temperature" is incorrect for Span 60 (Tc ≈ 53 °C). > > 4. **No CPP calculation in question A(a):** Numerical problems **require** the CPP formula explicitly written, with values and the final CPP value stated. > > 5. **Writing "Span 20"** for niosome preparation — Span 20 is **not** commonly used; prefer **Span 40, 60, 80** and **Tween 20, 80**. Lose 0.5 mark for wrong choice. > > 6. **Mixing up %EE and %DL:** Entrapment efficiency = drug / drug total; Drug loading = drug / lipid total. Interchanging these formulas **costs 1 mark**. > > 7. **Skipping the diagram:** In a 14-mark structural question, **always include a labeled diagram** of a niosome. Skipping it costs **1–2 marks** in the presentation category. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Niosomes (Module 4, PEBB862):** > > - **Definition:** Non-ionic surfactant-based bilayer vesicles (synthetic alternative to liposomes). > - **Key Components:** Surfactant (Span/Tween) + Cholesterol + Buffer (pH 7.4). > - **Optimal CSMR:** 1:1 to 1:2 (Cholesterol : Surfactant molar ratio). > - **CPP requirement:** 0.5 – 1.0 for vesicle formation. > - **Hydration temperature:** Above the phase transition temperature (Tc) of the surfactant. > - **Types:** MLV, LUV, SUV, GUV (size + lamellarity-based classification). > - **Methods:** TFH (→ MLV), Sonication (→ SUV), Extrusion (→ LUV), REV (→ LUV with high aqueous EE). > - **Green methods:** Heating method, Mozafari method, Bubble method — solvent-free, industrial-friendly. > - **Drug Loading:** %EE = (D_total − D_free)/D_total × 100; %DL = D_entrapped/W_lipid × 100. > - **Release kinetics:** Higuchi square-root model for diffusion-controlled Fickian release from niosomal bilayers. > - **Stability markers:** Zeta potential > ±30 mV, low PDI (< 0.3), DSC for Tc integrity. > - **Key advantages over liposomes:** Higher stability, lower cost, longer shelf-life, scalable synthesis. > - **Major applications:** Ocular, transdermal, oral, parenteral, cancer, gene delivery, cosmetics, vaccines. > - **Comparison trio:** Niosome vs Liposome vs Ethosome (know differences in composition, fluidity, skin penetration). > - **First reported:** Vanlerberghe & Handjani-Vila, **1972**, L'Oréal, France. > - **Standard vesicle size:** 100–500 nm (therapeutic range). > - **Charge inducers:** Dicetyl phosphate (negative), Stearyl amine (positive). > - **Characterization techniques:** DLS (size), ELS (zeta), TEM (morphology), DSC (Tc), HPLC (EE), Franz cell (release). > - **Active targeting:** Surface functionalization with folic acid, transferrin, lactoferrin, RGD peptides. > - **Industrial scale-up:** Mozafari method and supercritical CO₂ method preferred. > - **Key synonyms to recognize in exam:** Non-ionic surfactant vesicles, NSVs, noisome, niosomal dispersion. --- > **End of Module 4 — Niosomes (PEBB862, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Nanoparticles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB862 - DRUG DELIVERY PRINCIPLE & APPLICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nanoparticles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Nanoparticles in Drug Delivery — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nanoparticles (NPs)** are solid, colloidal particles ranging in size from **1 nm to 1000 nm** (typically **10 nm to 200 nm** for pharmaceutical applications), composed of polymeric, lipidic, metallic, or ceramic materials, in which an active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) is dissolved, entrapped, encapsulated, or surface-adsorbed to enable controlled, targeted, and enhanced drug delivery.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PEBB862)**, nanoparticles are classified as a sub-category of **colloidal drug carrier systems** (alongside microspheres, liposomes, and nanoemulsions). They are engineered at the nanoscale to exploit unique physicochemical properties — such as an extraordinarily high surface-area-to-volume ratio, quantum size effects, and enhanced cellular uptake — that are absent in their bulk counterparts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Emphasis Point:**
> The defining feature of a pharmaceutical nanoparticle is **NOT** merely its size. It is the **integration of three engineering parameters simultaneously**: (i) sub-cellular/nanoscale dimension, (ii) biocompatible carrier matrix, and (iii) functionalized drug payload. A particle that is small but lacks a carrier matrix is simply a "drug crystal," not a nanoparticle.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Courier" Model

Imagine you need to deliver a fragile, valuable package (the **drug molecule**) across a hostile city (the **human body**) without it being stolen by traffic police (**enzymes**), damaged by weather (**stomach acid / pH changes**), or intercepted by the wrong recipients (**non-target tissues**).

A **nanoparticle acts like a smart, armored courier van**:

| Courier Van Component | Nanoparticle Equivalent | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Outer armored shell | Polymer / lipid coat | Protects drug from enzymatic & chemical degradation |
| Internal cargo bay | Core (matrix / cavity) | Carries the active drug in dissolved or dispersed form |
| GPS tracking system | Surface ligand (e.g., folic acid, antibody) | Actively targets specific tissues (tumors, brain) |
| Timed-release door | Biodegradable polymer (PLGA, PLA) | Releases drug slowly over hours/days/weeks |
| Tiny size (navigates narrow alleys) | Nanoscale dimension (10–200 nm) | Penetrates capillaries, avoids renal clearance, enters cells via endocytosis |

This analogy is critical because it unifies the **four functional pillars** of nanoparticle engineering: **protection, transport, targeting, and controlled release**.

---

## 1.3 Why Nanoparticles? — The Pharmacokinetic Justification

Conventional dosage forms (tablets, injections) suffer from several limitations that nanoparticles directly address:

1. **Poor solubility** of BCS Class II/IV drugs → Solubility enhancement via nano-formulation.
2. **Non-specific biodistribution** → Targeted delivery reduces systemic toxicity.
3. **Rapid clearance** → Surface PEGylation (stealth coating) extends circulation time.
4. **Multidrug resistance (MDR)** in cancer → Endocytic uptake bypasses P-glycoprotein efflux pumps.
5. **Blood–Brain Barrier (BBB) penetration** → Surfactant-coated NPs (e.g., polysorbate 80) enable CNS delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Threshold Sizes in Drug Delivery (must memorize):**
> - **< 5 nm**: Renal clearance (filtered out by kidneys)
> - **10–100 nm**: Optimal for tumor accumulation via **Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect**
> - **> 200 nm**: Captured by spleen / RES (mononuclear phagocyte system)
> - **> 5 µm**: Trapped in lung capillaries

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification Preview

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups nanoparticles into the following families (this will be expanded in Section 4):

- **Polymeric Nanoparticles** (PNPs) — biodegradable polymers (PLGA, PLA, chitosan)
- **Solid Lipid Nanoparticles** (SLNs) — solid lipid core stabilized by surfactant
- **Nanostructured Lipid Carriers** (NLCs) — second-generation lipid NPs
- **Metallic Nanoparticles** — gold, silver, iron oxide (diagnostic + therapeutic)
- **Ceramic Nanoparticles** — silica, titania (porous, high drug loading)
- **Polymeric Micelles / Dendrimers** — branched architectures
- **Nanoemulsions / Nanosuspensions** — kinetically stable dispersed systems

This foundational understanding transitions directly into the theoretical analysis in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Nanoparticle Design

The performance of a nanoparticulate drug delivery system is governed by **five inter-dependent engineering parameters**:

### Pillar 1 — Nanoscale Geometry & Surface Area

As particle size decreases to the nanoscale, the fraction of atoms/molecules present on the **surface** increases dramatically. For a spherical particle of diameter `d`, the surface-area-to-volume ratio is given by:

$$SA/V = \frac{6}{d}$$

This means a **100 nm particle has a surface area approximately 1000× greater** than a **100 µm microparticle** of the same material. Consequences:
- Higher dissolution rate (Noyes-Whitney equation, see Section 3)
- Greater drug loading per unit mass
- Faster enzymatic degradation of the carrier matrix

### Pillar 2 — Composition & Biocompatibility

The carrier matrix must be:
- **Biocompatible** (non-toxic, non-immunogenic)
- **Biodegradable** for polymeric systems (PLGA, PLA, PCL degrade via hydrolysis into natural metabolites)
- **Sterilizable** (autoclavable or filter-sterilizable)

### Pillar 3 — Drug Loading Mechanism

Drugs can be associated with nanoparticles via **four mechanistic pathways**:

| Mechanism | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Dissolution in matrix** | Drug molecularly dispersed within polymer/lipid | Hydrophobic drug in PLGA |
| **Adsorption on surface** | Drug binds to outer surface via electrostatic/H-bond | Proteins on chitosan NPs |
| **Covalent conjugation** | Drug chemically bonded to polymer chain | Antibody-drug conjugates |
| **Entrapment in core** | Drug enclosed within a hollow cavity | Liposomes, nanocapsules |

### Pillar 4 — Surface Functionalization (Stealth & Targeting)

To evade the **mononuclear phagocyte system (MPS)**, the nanoparticle surface is often grafted with **polyethylene glycol (PEG)** — this is called **PEGylation**, creating "stealth" NPs that circulate for hours instead of minutes. For active targeting, ligands such as folic acid, transferrin, RGD peptides, or monoclonal antibodies are conjugated to the PEG terminus.

### Pillar 5 — Release Kinetics

Drug release from nanoparticles follows **mathematical models** (zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, Hixson-Crowell) — these are derived in Section 3.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must know for the **End Semester Examination (ESE)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Every variable, unit, and boundary condition is documented.

| Sl. No. | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SA/V = \dfrac{6}{d}$ | $d$ = diameter (nm) | Surface-area-to-volume ratio for a spherical NP |
| 2 | $DLC\ (\%) = \dfrac{W_{drug}}{W_{NP}} \times 100$ | $W_{drug}$ = mass of drug in NPs (mg); $W_{NP}$ = total mass of NPs recovered (mg) | **Drug Loading Capacity** |
| 3 | $EE\ (\%) = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | $W_{actual}$ = measured drug; $W_{theoretical}$ = drug added during prep | **Entrapment / Encapsulation Efficiency** |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = \dfrac{D \cdot A}{V \cdot h} \cdot (C_s - C_b)$ | $D$ = diffusion coefficient; $A$ = surface area; $h$ = diffusion layer thickness; $C_s$ = saturation solubility; $C_b$ = bulk concentration | **Noyes-Whitney Dissolution Equation** |
| 5 | $Q = K_H \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | $Q$ = cumulative drug released per unit area; $K_H$ = Higuchi constant; $t$ = time | **Higuchi Square-Root Model** (matrix diffusion) |
| 6 | $Q_t = Q_0 + K_0 \cdot t$ | $K_0$ = zero-order rate constant | **Zero-Order Release** (ideal for sustained delivery) |
| 7 | $\log Q = \log Q_0 - \dfrac{K_1 \cdot t}{2.303}$ | $K_1$ = first-order rate constant | **First-Order Release** (concentration-dependent) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = K \cdot t^{n}$ | $M_t/M_\infty$ = fractional release; $K$ = kinetic constant; $n$ = diffusion exponent | **Korsmeyer-Peppas Power Law** |
| 9 | $n = 0.43$ (slab), $n = 0.45$ (cylinder), $n = 0.85$ (sphere) — boundaries | — | Korsmeyer-Peppas diffusional exponent (Fickian) |
| 10 | $W_0^{1/3} - W_t^{1/3} = K_{HC} \cdot t$ | $W_0, W_t$ = initial and residual mass; $K_{HC}$ = Hixson-Crowell constant | **Hixson-Crowell Cube-Root Model** (erosion) |
| 11 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{K_1}$ | $t_{1/2}$ = half-life; $K_1$ = first-order constant | First-order half-life calculation |
| 12 | $\Delta G = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K; $T$ = temperature (K); $K_{eq}$ = equilibrium constant | Thermodynamic favorability of loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Renal Filtration Threshold:** Particles with hydrodynamic diameter **< 5.5 nm** are filtered by glomeruli. Particles **> 200 nm** are cleared by spleen. The **10–100 nm window** is the **"sweet spot"** for prolonged systemic circulation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are not merely theoretical — they are applied in:

- **Cancer nanomedicine**: Doxil® (liposomal doxorubicin) and Abraxane® (albumin-bound paclitaxel NPs) — FDA-approved.
- **mRNA vaccine delivery**: Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna COVID-19 vaccines used **lipid nanoparticles (LNPs)** to deliver mRNA into cells — the largest medical deployment of NPs in history (billions of doses).
- **CNS drug delivery**: Surface-modified NPs (e.g., transferrin-conjugated) cross the **blood–brain barrier (BBB)** for glioma and Parkinson's therapy.
- **Antimicrobial NPs**: Silver NPs embedded in wound dressings, catheters, and bone cement.
- **Diagnostic imaging**: Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) as MRI contrast agents.

This establishes the engineering and clinical relevance before we move to mathematical derivations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Surface Area to Volume Ratio for a Sphere

**Starting Premise:** All nanoparticles are idealized as spheres for theoretical calculation. A sphere of radius `r` has surface area `A = 4\pi r^{2}` and volume `V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}`.

### Step 1: Form the ratio

$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}}$$

### Step 2: Cancel common terms

The `4\pi` cancels; one factor of `r` cancels from numerator with the cubic in denominator:

$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{3}{r}$$

### Step 3: Express in terms of diameter

Since $r = d/2$, substitute:

$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{3}{d/2} = \frac{6}{d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A nanoparticle of diameter **100 nm** has a surface-area-to-volume ratio of **60 nm⁻¹**, whereas a microparticle of **100 µm (100,000 nm)** has only **0.06 nm⁻¹** — a **1000-fold** difference. This is the fundamental reason why nanoscale drug carriers exhibit dramatically enhanced dissolution and bioavailability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Higuchi Square-Root Model (Matrix Diffusion)

This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU ESE.

**Assumptions:**
1. Drug is uniformly dispersed in a homogeneous matrix.
2. The matrix does not dissolve during release.
3. Drug concentration is much higher than its solubility in the matrix (pseudo-steady state).
4. Fickian diffusion governs release.
5. One-dimensional diffusion through a planar slab of thickness `h`.

### Step 1: Fick's Second Law (1-D slab)

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### Step 2: Apply pseudo-steady-state assumption

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot D \cdot C_s}{h}$$

where `M` = cumulative mass released, `A` = area, `D` = diffusion coefficient, `C_s` = drug solubility in matrix, `h` = diffusion path length.

### Step 3: Integrate from 0 to time `t`

$$M(t) = \frac{A \cdot D \cdot C_s}{h} \cdot t$$

### Step 4: For a matrix with initial drug loading `A₀` (mg/cm³)

Higuchi's full derivation (accounting for the moving boundary of the depleted zone) yields:

$$Q(t) = \sqrt{D \cdot t \cdot \left(2 A_0 - C_s\right) \cdot C_s}$$

### Step 5: Simplified Engineering Form

Defining the Higuchi constant $K_H = \sqrt{D \cdot (2A_0 - C_s) \cdot C_s}$:

$$Q(t) = K_H \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Examination Tip:** A plot of **cumulative % drug released (Y-axis) versus square root of time (X-axis)** should yield a **straight line** for a matrix-diffusion-controlled system. The slope equals `K_H`.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Korsmeyer-Peppas Power Law

This model is used **specifically for swellable or porous polymeric systems** where the diffusion exponent `n` provides mechanistic insight.

### Starting equation

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = K \cdot t^{n}$$

where:
- $M_t / M_\infty$ = fraction of drug released at time `t`
- $K$ = kinetic constant (depends on geometry)
- $n$ = diffusional exponent (mechanism indicator)

### Step-by-step linearization for plotting

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{M_t}{M_\infty}\right) = \ln K + n \cdot \ln(t)$$

### Mechanistic Interpretation (Boundary Values)

| Geometry | Fickian Diffusion (Case I) | Anomalous (Non-Fickian) | Case-II Transport (Zero-Order) | Super Case-II |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thin slab | $n = 0.5$ | $0.5 < n < 1.0$ | $n = 1.0$ | $n > 1.0$ |
| Cylinder | $n = 0.45$ | $0.45 < n < 0.89$ | $n = 0.89$ | $n > 0.89$ |
| Sphere | $n = 0.43$ | $0.43 < n < 0.85$ | $n = 0.85$ | $n > 0.85$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** When `n < 0.5` (slab), diffusion dominates (Fickian). When `n` approaches 1.0, polymer chain relaxation/erosion dominates (zero-order release — clinically preferred for sustained delivery).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Entrapment Efficiency Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the type of numerical that appears in Part B questions (7-mark sub-parts). Master the structure.**

### Given Data

During the preparation of PLGA nanoparticles loaded with the anti-cancer drug **5-fluorouracil (5-FU)**:
- Total drug added during preparation = **50 mg**
- Drug recovered in the supernatant (unentrapped) after ultracentrifugation = **12 mg**
- Total mass of dried nanoparticles recovered = **120 mg**

### Step 1: Calculate the actual drug content entrapped

$$W_{actual} = W_{theoretical} - W_{supernatant}$$

$$W_{actual} = 50\ \text{mg} - 12\ \text{mg} = 38\ \text{mg}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Encapsulation Efficiency (EE)

$$EE\ (\%) = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{theoretical}} \times 100$$

$$EE\ (\%) = \frac{38}{50} \times 100 = 76\%$$

### Step 3: Calculate Drug Loading Capacity (DLC)

$$DLC\ (\%) = \frac{W_{drug}}{W_{NP}} \times 100 = \frac{38}{120} \times 100$$

$$DLC\ (\%) = 31.67\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 76% EE is considered **good** for a nanoprecipitation method; a DLC of ~32% is **high**, indicating efficient use of carrier material. Both metrics must be **reported together** in a KTU-style answer — calculating only one is incomplete.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Method vs. Drug Properties

| Drug Property | Recommended NP Type | Preparation Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrophobic drug (e.g., paclitaxel) | PLGA NPs / SLNs | Nanoprecipitation / Emulsification | High affinity for polymer/lipid core |
| Hydrophilic drug (e.g., doxorubicin HCl) | Chitosan NPs / Nanocapsules | Double emulsion (W/O/W) | Prevents burst release into aqueous phase |
| Protein / peptide (e.g., insulin) | Alginate / PLGA NPs | Ionic gelation / Coacervation | Mild conditions preserve bioactivity |
| Nucleic acid (mRNA, siRNA) | Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) | Microfluidic mixing | Cationic lipids complex with anionic nucleic acids |
| Heat-labile drug | SLNs / NLCs | Hot or cold homogenization | Avoids high-temperature exposure |

This analytical matrix directly bridges theory to the question patterns in Section 5.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
